learnIt.realLife: You have 47 chocolates to share equally among 5 friends. Each friend gets 9, and 2 are left in your hand that simply refuse to divide. That leftover 2 is a remainder. Almost every Number System question in a placement test is a smarter version of sharing chocolates: how many go in each group, how many are left over, and what the very last one looks like.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Number System is about the hidden structure of whole numbers — the counting numbers 1, 2, 3 and so on. A few words you will keep meeting: a factor of a number divides it with nothing left over (3 is a factor of 12); a multiple is what you get by multiplying (12 is a multiple of 3); a prime number has exactly two factors, 1 and itself (2, 3, 5, 7, 11…); even numbers end in 0,2,4,6,8 and odd numbers end in 1,3,5,7,9. This topic asks how a number splits into its prime pieces, what remainder it leaves when divided, and what its last digit becomes when raised to a power.
learnIt.mathIntro: Three ideas run through almost every question. First, any division splits a number as Dividend = Divisor × Quotient + Remainder. Second, every whole number is built by multiplying prime numbers together, and that prime build-up tells you how many factors it has. Third, the last digit of a number repeats in a short cycle (length 4 or less) as you raise it to higher powers.

formulas.label: The division identity
formulas.breakdown: Dividend is the number being divided, Divisor is what you divide by, Quotient is how many whole times it fits, and Remainder is what is left over (always smaller than the divisor). It holds because the quotient counts the complete groups and the remainder is the piece too small to make one more group.
formulas.example: 47 ÷ 5: five fits nine times (45), with 2 left. So 47 = 5 × 9 + 2.

formulas.label: Number of factors
formulas.formula: If N = p^a × q^b × r^c, then number of factors = (a+1)(b+1)(c+1)
formulas.breakdown: Break N into its prime powers. To build any factor you decide how many of each prime to include — from 0 up to its full power — which is (power + 1) choices for each prime, and you multiply the choices together.

formulas.label: Cyclicity (last digit of a power)
formulas.formula: Last digit repeats every 4 powers → use (power mod 4)
formulas.breakdown: Only the base's last digit matters, and it loops in a cycle of length 4 or less. Divide the power by 4 and read the remainder: it points to a position in the cycle, with remainder 0 meaning the 4th (last) position.
formulas.example: 7 cycles 7, 9, 3, 1. For 7^623: 623 ÷ 4 leaves remainder 3, so take the 3rd in the cycle → last digit 3.

formulas.label: Trailing zeros in a factorial
formulas.breakdown: A trailing zero needs a 10, which is 2 × 5. Inside a factorial the 5s are rarer than the 2s, so you only count 5s: multiples of 5 give one each, multiples of 25 give a second, 125 a third, and so on.

formulas.label: Sum of first n natural numbers
formulas.breakdown: Pair the first with the last (1 + n), the second with the second-last (2 + (n−1)); each pair totals n+1 and there are n/2 pairs. Useful whenever you must count or add a run of numbers.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Last (unit) digit of a big power
questionTypes.idea: The question gives a number raised to a large power and asks only for the last digit. What makes it different: you never compute the full number — only the base's last digit matters, and it repeats in a cycle of 4.
questionTypes.problem: Find the unit digit of 2^54.

steps.action: Look only at the base's last digit, 2, and write its cycle: 2, 4, 8, 6.
steps.why: The unit digit of a power depends only on the unit digit of the base, and it repeats every four powers.
The remainder tells you which position in the 4-long cycle you land on.
Remainder 1 is the 1st, 2 the 2nd, 3 the 3rd, and remainder 0 the 4th (last).

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Remainder of a division
questionTypes.idea: The question gives a number, product or expression divided by some divisor and asks the remainder. What makes it different: you reduce each part to its own remainder first, instead of dividing the whole big number.
questionTypes.problem: Find the remainder when 17 × 23 × 29 is divided by 5.

steps.action: Replace each number by its remainder on division by 5: 17 → 2, 23 → 3, 29 → 4.
steps.why: In a remainder problem you may swap any number for its own remainder without changing the final answer.
The remainder of a product equals the product of the remainders (then reduced again).
You reduce once more because 24 is still bigger than the divisor 5.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Number of factors (divisors)
questionTypes.idea: The question gives a number and asks how many factors it has (sometimes the sum of factors). What makes it different: you factor it into primes first and use the powers, rather than listing every divisor by hand.
questionTypes.problem: How many factors does 72 have?

steps.why: The factor-count rule works from the prime powers, so you must reach them first.

steps.action: Add 1 to each power and multiply: (3+1)(2+1).
steps.why: Each prime can appear 0 up to its full power in a factor, giving (power + 1) independent choices.
Multiplying the choices counts every factor exactly once.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Trailing zeros in a factorial
questionTypes.idea: The question asks how many zeros are at the end of n! (n factorial). What makes it different: you count how many 5s hide inside the factorial, not the zeros directly.
questionTypes.problem: How many zeros are at the end of 50!?

steps.why: Each multiple of 5 supplies at least one 5, and a zero needs a 5 paired with a 2 (2s are plentiful).
Numbers like 25 and 50 carry a second 5, so they are counted again.
Stop when the division gives 0; the total number of 5s equals the number of trailing zeros.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Find the number from divisor, quotient and remainder
questionTypes.idea: The question gives clues like 'divided by d gives quotient q and remainder r' (or a successive-division chain) and asks for the number. What makes it different: you rebuild the number with the division identity.
questionTypes.problem: A number, when divided by 7, gives quotient 12 and remainder 3. Find the number.

steps.action: Write the identity: Number = Divisor × Quotient + Remainder.
steps.why: Every division can be reversed with this single relationship.
Divisor 7, quotient 12 and remainder 3 are given directly.
Multiply first, then add the remainder.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Counting numbers with a property in a range
questionTypes.idea: The question asks how many numbers in a range are divisible by something (or are even/odd). What makes it different: you count with a division, not by listing every number.
questionTypes.problem: How many numbers from 1 to 100 are divisible by 3?

steps.action: Divide the top of the range by 3: ⌊100/3⌋ = 33.
steps.why: The count of multiples of 3 up to 100 is simply how many whole times 3 fits into 100.
The largest multiple not above 100 is 99 = 3 × 33, which confirms the count.

commonMistakes: In cyclicity, treating remainder 0 as 'the 0th' position. There is no 0th — when the power divides evenly by 4 (remainder 0) you take the 4th (last) number in the cycle, not the first.
For trailing zeros, counting 2s instead of 5s, or forgetting the extra 5 inside 25, 50, 125. Skipping ⌊n/25⌋ silently undercounts the zeros.
In factor counting, forgetting to add 1 to each power. 2^3 gives (3+1) = 4 choices for the prime 2, not 3.
In remainder questions, reducing only some of the numbers. If you replace 17 by 2 but leave 23 unchanged, the arithmetic breaks — reduce every part before multiplying.
Confusing 'digit' with 'number'. 'Unit digit' asks for a single digit from 0 to 9; it is never the whole value of the power.
Treating 1 as a prime. 1 is neither prime nor composite, so it never appears in a prime factorisation.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Three anchors cover most of this topic: 'D = dq + r' for anything about division, 'add 1 to each power and multiply' for counting factors, and 'power ÷ 4' for the last digit. For factorial zeros, just chant 'count the fives'.

crackIt.oneLine: Number System is three reflexes — last digit? divide the power by 4; how many factors? add 1 to each prime power and multiply; how many zeros in a factorial? count the fives.

shortcuts.name: Unit digit — power ÷ 4
shortcuts.method: Take the base's last digit → its 4-cycle; use (power mod 4), where 0 means the last one.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: The base's last digit picks the cycle; the exponent goes into 'mod 4' to pick the position.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: 2^54 is impossible to compute by hand · Shortcut: 2 steps.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any single base raised to a whole-number power.
shortcuts.whenFails: Bases ending in 0, 1, 5 or 6 stay the same digit (no mod needed); and it never gives the last two digits.

shortcuts.name: Remainder — reduce first
shortcuts.method: Replace each part by its remainder, multiply, then reduce once more.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Each number → its remainder mod d; multiply those; take mod d again.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: multiply to 11,339 then divide · Shortcut: 3 tiny steps.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Products, sums and powers under a divisor.
shortcuts.whenFails: Do not reduce the divisor itself; and turn any negative remainder positive by adding d.

shortcuts.name: Factor count — (powers + 1) product
shortcuts.method: N = p^a·q^b·… → factors = (a+1)(b+1)…
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Each prime's exponent, plus 1, all multiplied together.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: list every divisor · Shortcut: 1 multiplication.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Counting how many factors/divisors a number has.
shortcuts.whenFails: You must factor fully into primes first — a wrong factorisation gives a wrong count; the sum of factors needs a different formula.

shortcuts.name: Factorial zeros — count the fives
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: n divided by 5, then 25, then 125 …; add the whole-number parts.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: multiplying out the factorial is impossible · Shortcut: 2–3 divisions.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Trailing zeros of a plain factorial n!.
shortcuts.whenFails: Products that are not factorials; then factor them and count 5s and 2s separately.

shortcuts.name: Rebuild — D = dq + r
shortcuts.method: Number = divisor × quotient + remainder.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Multiply divisor by quotient, then add the remainder.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any 'find the number' from division clues; for successive division, work backward from the last division.
shortcuts.whenFails: The remainder must be smaller than the divisor — if it is not, the data is inconsistent.

shortcuts.name: Count multiples — floor divide
shortcuts.method: Count of multiples of k up to N = ⌊N/k⌋.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Top of the range ÷ k, keeping only the whole part.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: list them all · Shortcut: 1 division.
shortcuts.whenFails: If the range starts above 1, subtract the count below the start: ⌊N/k⌋ − ⌊(start−1)/k⌋.

patternGuide.signal: 'unit digit' / 'last digit' / 'ones digit' of a power
'remainder when … is divided by' a product, sum or power
patternGuide.use: Reduce each part to its remainder first, then multiply.

patternGuide.signal: 'how many factors / divisors' of a number
'number of zeros at the end of' a factorial (n!)
'divided by … quotient … remainder …, find the number' or 'successive division'
patternGuide.use: D = dq + r (work backward for successive division).

patternGuide.signal: 'how many numbers between … are divisible by / even / odd'

topics.description: Find the highest common factor and lowest common multiple, and where each is used.

videos.label: HCF & LCM — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: Concept + how to spot HCF vs LCM in word problems

learnIt.realLife: You have two ribbons — one 12 cm long, the other 18 cm — and you want to cut both into equal pieces with none left over, using the longest possible piece. You also wonder: if you keep laying 12 cm and 18 cm ribbons end to end in two separate lines, when will the two lines first reach exactly the same length? The longest cutting piece is the HCF, and the first matching length is the LCM. Almost every question on this topic is one of those two questions in disguise.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Two ideas sit at the heart of this topic. The HCF (Highest Common Factor, also called GCD) is the biggest number that divides two or more numbers with nothing left over — think 'the largest piece that fits into all of them exactly'. The LCM (Least Common Multiple) is the smallest number that all of them divide into with nothing left over — think 'the first common landmark they all reach'. A factor goes into a number; a multiple is built from a number. HCF is about factors (it is small, never bigger than the numbers). LCM is about multiples (it is large, never smaller than the biggest number).
learnIt.mathIntro: The cleanest way to see both is to break each number into its prime building blocks. Once you have the primes, HCF takes the primes they all share at the smallest power present, and LCM takes every prime that appears at the largest power present. One special shortcut ties them together: for exactly two numbers, HCF × LCM equals the two numbers multiplied.

formulas.label: HCF from prime factors
formulas.formula: HCF = product of common primes, each at its LOWEST power
formulas.breakdown: A number that divides all of them can only use primes every number has, and no more copies than the poorest number supplies. So you keep the shared primes at their smallest power.
formulas.example: 12 = 2²×3 and 18 = 2×3². Shared primes: 2 (lowest power 1) and 3 (lowest power 1) → HCF = 2×3 = 6.

formulas.label: LCM from prime factors
formulas.formula: LCM = product of ALL primes, each at its HIGHEST power
formulas.breakdown: A number divisible by all of them must carry enough of every prime to cover the greediest one. So you take each prime that appears anywhere, at its largest power.

formulas.label: Product relation (two numbers only)
formulas.formula: HCF × LCM = first number × second number
formulas.breakdown: Between them, the HCF (lowest powers) and the LCM (highest powers) use each prime exactly as many times as the two numbers do together, so their product matches the numbers multiplied. This holds for two numbers only.

formulas.label: Division (Euclidean) method for HCF
formulas.formula: HCF(a, b) = HCF(b, remainder of a ÷ b), repeat until remainder is 0
formulas.breakdown: Any number that divides both a and b also divides what is left over when you divide them, so you can replace the larger number by the remainder and shrink the problem. The last divisor before the remainder hits 0 is the HCF.

formulas.label: HCF & LCM of fractions
formulas.formula: HCF of fractions = HCF(numerators) / LCM(denominators);  LCM of fractions = LCM(numerators) / HCF(denominators)
formulas.breakdown: A fraction that divides two fractions must have a top that divides both tops (HCF) and a bottom that is a multiple of both bottoms (LCM); the roles flip for the LCM of fractions.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Plain HCF and LCM of numbers
questionTypes.idea: Gives two or more numbers and asks their HCF, LCM, or both. What makes it different: it is pure computation — factor into primes, then read off lowest powers for HCF and highest powers for LCM.
questionTypes.problem: Find the HCF and LCM of 24 and 36.

steps.why: Both answers are read straight from the prime powers.

steps.action: HCF = shared primes at lowest power = 2² × 3 = 12.
steps.why: A common factor cannot use more 2s than 36 supplies (2²) or more 3s than 24 supplies (3¹).

steps.action: LCM = all primes at highest power = 2³ × 3² = 72.
steps.why: A common multiple must carry enough of each prime to be divisible by both numbers.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Product relation (find the missing value)
questionTypes.idea: Gives some of {HCF, LCM, the two numbers} and asks for the missing one. What makes it different: you use HCF × LCM = product of the two numbers instead of factorising.
questionTypes.problem: The HCF of two numbers is 8 and their LCM is 96. If one number is 24, find the other.

steps.action: Write HCF × LCM = first × second → 8 × 96 = 24 × second.
steps.why: For two numbers, the product of the HCF and LCM equals the product of the numbers.
Divide to isolate the unknown number.
Complete the division.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Greatest number that divides leaving remainders
questionTypes.idea: Asks the greatest number dividing several numbers leaving the SAME remainder (remainder unknown), or leaving stated remainders. What makes it different: you take the HCF of the differences, or of (number − its remainder).
questionTypes.problem: Find the greatest number that divides 43, 91 and 183 leaving the same remainder in each case.

steps.why: When the remainder is equal, it cancels in a subtraction, so the divisor must divide each difference exactly.
The greatest number dividing all the differences is their HCF.
Only the factor 2² is shared by all three differences.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Least number leaving a common remainder
questionTypes.idea: Asks the smallest number that, divided by each given number, leaves the same remainder r (or none). What makes it different: the answer is the LCM of the divisors, then add r.
questionTypes.problem: Find the least number which, when divided by 6, 7 and 8, leaves a remainder of 3 in each case.

steps.why: A number divisible by all three must be a multiple of their LCM, and the least is the LCM itself.
Leaving remainder 3 means the number is exactly 3 more than a multiple of the LCM.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Bells / lights ringing together
questionTypes.idea: Several bells (or lights, or runners returning to a start) repeat at fixed intervals and you must find when they coincide again, or how often in a period. What makes it different: the meeting gap is the LCM of the intervals.
questionTypes.problem: Four bells ring at intervals of 6, 8, 12 and 18 seconds. If they ring together now, after how many seconds will they next ring together?

steps.why: They coincide only at a time that is a whole number of every interval, and the least such time is the LCM.

steps.action: So they ring together after 72 seconds.
steps.why: 72 is the smallest gap divisible by all four intervals (highest power of 2 is 2³ from 8, of 3 is 3² from 18).

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — HCF and LCM of fractions
questionTypes.idea: Asks the HCF or LCM of a set of fractions. What makes it different: numerators and denominators are handled separately, and the roles flip between HCF and LCM.
questionTypes.problem: Find the LCM of 3/4, 6/7 and 9/8.

steps.action: Use LCM of fractions = LCM(numerators) ÷ HCF(denominators).
steps.why: The result must be a whole-number multiple of each fraction, which needs the LCM on top and the HCF on the bottom.
18 is the least common multiple of the tops; 4, 7, 8 share no common factor, so their HCF is 1.
Divide the numerator-LCM by the denominator-HCF.

questionTypes.name: Type 7 — Real-world HCF and ratio numbers
questionTypes.idea: Word problems: largest tile to pave a floor, longest tape to measure lengths, maximum equal rows, or two numbers in a ratio with a known HCF. What makes it different: 'greatest size that fits exactly' means HCF; ratio numbers are (HCF × each ratio part).
questionTypes.problem: What is the largest square tile that can exactly pave a floor 12 m by 18 m?

steps.action: The tile's side must divide both 12 and 18 exactly, so take HCF(12, 18).
steps.why: A tile that fits with no cutting must measure a length that divides each side of the floor.
The largest length dividing both sides is their highest common factor.

steps.action: So the largest tile is 6 m × 6 m.
steps.why: 6 divides 12 (twice) and 18 (three times) with nothing left over.

commonMistakes: Swapping the two fraction formulas. HCF of fractions = HCF(tops)/LCM(bottoms); LCM of fractions = LCM(tops)/HCF(bottoms). Mixing them gives a neat-looking but wrong fraction.
Using HCF × LCM = product of numbers for THREE numbers. That identity is true for two numbers only; with three it can fail.
In 'same remainder' problems (remainder not stated), forgetting to use the differences of the numbers. Take bigger − smaller in pairs, then HCF.
Choosing the wrong quantity. 'Greatest / largest / maximum that divides' means HCF; 'least / smallest / together again' means LCM. Read the size word first.
Forgetting to add the remainder in LCM-remainder problems. The LCM only covers the divisible part — you must add r at the end.
Taking the wrong powers when factorising: using highest powers for HCF or lowest powers for LCM. HCF = lowest shared powers, LCM = highest powers overall.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Size word decides everything: greatest / largest / maximum → HCF (lowest common powers, a small answer); least / smallest / together-again → LCM (highest powers, a large answer). And for two numbers only, HCF × LCM = the two numbers multiplied.

crackIt.oneLine: LCM & HCF is one decision — 'greatest/largest' means HCF (share the least of each prime), 'least/together-again' means LCM (grab the most of each prime), and for two numbers HCF × LCM = their product.

shortcuts.name: HCF & LCM — prime powers
shortcuts.method: HCF = common primes at lowest power; LCM = all primes at highest power.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Factor each number; lowest shared powers build the HCF, highest powers overall build the LCM.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: list every factor and multiple · Shortcut: 1 factorisation.
shortcuts.whenFails: Big awkward primes are slow to factor — switch to the division (Euclidean) method for HCF.

shortcuts.name: Missing value — product rule
shortcuts.method: Missing number = (HCF × LCM) ÷ known number.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: HCF times LCM on top, the number you already have on the bottom.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: factor and rebuild · Shortcut: 1 division.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Exactly two numbers, with three of the four quantities known.
shortcuts.whenFails: Three or more numbers — the identity breaks.

shortcuts.name: Greatest divisor, same remainder — HCF of differences
shortcuts.method: Take pairwise differences of the numbers, then their HCF.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Subtract the numbers in pairs; HCF of those differences is the answer.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: trial divisors · Shortcut: differences then one HCF.
shortcuts.whenWorks: 'Same remainder' problems where the remainder is not given.
shortcuts.whenFails: If the remainders ARE given and differ — subtract each remainder from its number first, then take HCF.

shortcuts.name: Least number, common remainder — LCM + r
shortcuts.method: Answer = LCM of the divisors + the common remainder.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: LCM of the divisors, then add r.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: test numbers one by one · Shortcut: LCM then +r.
shortcuts.whenFails: If instead (divisor − remainder) is a constant gap, use LCM − that gap.

shortcuts.name: Ring together — LCM of intervals
shortcuts.method: Next-together time = LCM of intervals; count in time T = T ÷ LCM.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: LCM of all intervals; divide the time window by it.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: list each ringing time · Shortcut: 1 LCM.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Bells, lights, or runners meeting at the START point.
shortcuts.whenFails: Runners meeting ANYWHERE on the track — that is a relative-speed problem, a different topic.

shortcuts.name: Fractions — flip the roles
shortcuts.method: HCF = HCF(tops)/LCM(bottoms); LCM = LCM(tops)/HCF(bottoms).
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Numerators feed the top operation, denominators the bottom.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: common-denominator juggling · Shortcut: two mini HCF/LCMs.
shortcuts.whenFails: Un-reduced fractions — simplify each one first, or the tops/bottoms are wrong.

shortcuts.name: Ratio numbers — HCF × parts
shortcuts.method: Numbers in ratio a:b with HCF h are ah and bh; their LCM = a·b·h.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Multiply each ratio part by the HCF.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: guess the numbers · Shortcut: multiply parts by the HCF.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Ratio given in lowest terms with its HCF.
shortcuts.whenFails: Ratio not in lowest terms — simplify it before using this.

topics.description: Quick checks for divisibility by 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 11 and more.

videos.label: All Divisibility Rules 1–13 in one video
videos.note: Fast, complete rundown of every rule

videos.label: Divisibility Rule shortcuts — solve in seconds
videos.note: Applying the rules to exam questions

learnIt.realLife: You and three friends split a restaurant bill and you want to know instantly whether it divides evenly — without pulling out a calculator. If the total is 348, you glance at it and know it splits by 3 (because 3+4+8 = 15 divides by 3) and by 4 (because 48 divides by 4). Divisibility rules are exactly these quick glances: shortcuts that tell you 'yes, it divides' or 'no, it doesn't' in a second, without doing the full division.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A divisibility rule is a quick test that checks whether one number divides another exactly (leaving no remainder), by looking at only a few digits or a small sum instead of dividing the whole thing. Each divisor has its own kind of test: some care only about the last digit, some about the last two or three digits, some about the sum of all digits, and a couple need a small back-and-forth trick. Once you know which test belongs to which divisor, you never have to do long division to answer 'is it divisible?' again.
learnIt.mathIntro: Every rule comes from how the powers of 10 behave under that divisor. For 2, 5 and 10, all the higher place values are already multiples, so only the units digit matters. For 4, 8 and 25, the powers of 10 from a certain point on are multiples, so only the last few digits matter. For 3 and 9, each power of 10 leaves remainder 1, so the whole number behaves like its digit sum. For 11, powers of 10 alternate +1 and −1, giving the alternating-sum test. And 7 and 13, which have no neat digit rule, use a small chop-and-adjust step instead.

formulas.label: Rules for 2, 5, 10 — look at the units digit
formulas.formula: ÷2: last digit even (0,2,4,6,8)   ·   ÷5: last digit 0 or 5   ·   ÷10: last digit 0
formulas.breakdown: Ten is 2 × 5, so tens, hundreds and every higher place value are already multiples of 2, 5 and 10. Only the units digit can leave a remainder, so it alone decides these tests.
formulas.example: 2470 ends in 0 → divisible by 2, 5 and 10. 2475 ends in 5 → by 5 only.

formulas.label: Rules for 4, 8, 25 — read the end digits
formulas.formula: ÷4: last 2 digits form a multiple of 4   ·   ÷8: last 3 digits form a multiple of 8   ·   ÷25: last 2 digits are 00, 25, 50 or 75
formulas.breakdown: 100 is a multiple of 4 and 25, and 1000 is a multiple of 8, so everything above the last two (or three) digits is already divisible. Only the tail can decide.
formulas.example: 73,528 → last 3 digits 528, and 528 ÷ 8 = 66 → divisible by 8. 4316 → last 2 digits 16 → divisible by 4.

formulas.label: Rules for 3 and 9 — add the digits
formulas.formula: ÷3: digit sum is a multiple of 3   ·   ÷9: digit sum is a multiple of 9
formulas.breakdown: Each power of 10 leaves remainder 1 when divided by 3 or 9 (10 = 9+1, 100 = 99+1 …), so the number and its digit sum leave the same remainder. If the digit sum divides, the number divides.

formulas.label: Rules for 6, 12, 15 — split into coprime factors
formulas.formula: N ÷ (a×b) with a, b coprime  ⇔  N ÷ a AND N ÷ b. So ÷6 = ÷2 & ÷3;  ÷12 = ÷3 & ÷4;  ÷15 = ÷3 & ÷5
formulas.breakdown: If two factors share no common prime, a number divisible by both must contain both sets of primes, so it is divisible by their product. Always pick coprime pieces — for 12 use 3 and 4, never 2 and 6.
formulas.example: 5148 → digit sum 18 (divisible by 3) and last two digits 48 (divisible by 4) → divisible by 12.

formulas.label: Rule for 11 — alternating digit sum
formulas.formula: ÷11: (sum of digits in odd positions) − (sum of digits in even positions) is 0 or a multiple of 11
formulas.breakdown: Powers of 10 alternate as +1, −1 under division by 11 (10 ≡ −1, 100 ≡ +1 …), so the number's remainder equals this alternating sum. Number the positions from the right.
formulas.example: 1210 → odd-position digits (0 + 2) = 2, even-position digits (1 + 1) = 2, difference 0 → divisible by 11 (1210 = 11 × 110).

formulas.label: Rules for 7 and 13 — chop and adjust
formulas.formula: ÷7: drop the last digit, subtract 2 × it from the rest, repeat.   ÷13: drop the last digit, add 4 × it to the rest, repeat.
formulas.breakdown: Each step swaps the number for a smaller one with the same divisibility, because the adjustment removes a known multiple of 7 (or 13). Keep repeating until the number is small enough to judge.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Apply one rule (is N divisible by d?)
questionTypes.idea: Gives a number and a divisor and asks yes/no, or asks which of several numbers is divisible. What makes it different: you apply exactly one rule and never do full division.
questionTypes.problem: Is 43,152 divisible by 8?

steps.action: For 8, look only at the last three digits: 152.
steps.why: 1000 is a multiple of 8, so all higher place values are already divisible — only the last three digits decide.
If the last three digits form a multiple of 8, the whole number is a multiple of 8.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Find the missing digit
questionTypes.idea: A digit is hidden (shown as * or a letter) and you must choose it so the number is divisible by a given divisor. What makes it different: you set up the matching rule and solve for the digit.
questionTypes.problem: Find the digit * so that 34*5 is divisible by 9.

steps.action: For 9, the digit sum must be a multiple of 9. Sum = 3 + 4 + * + 5 = 12 + *.
steps.why: A number is divisible by 9 exactly when its digit sum is.

steps.action: Pick * so 12 + * is a multiple of 9. The next multiple is 18, so * = 6.
steps.why: 12 + 6 = 18 = 9 × 2, and a single digit must be 0–9, so 6 is the fit.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Divisibility by a composite number
questionTypes.idea: Asks divisibility by a composite like 6, 12, 15, 88. What makes it different: split it into coprime factors and test each — never use factors that share a prime.
questionTypes.problem: Is 7,920 divisible by 12?

steps.why: 3 and 4 share no prime, so passing both guarantees divisibility by 12; using 2 and 6 would double-count the factor 2 and could mislead.
The digit-sum rule tests 3.
The last-two-digits rule tests 4; both pass, so 12 divides the number.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Divisibility by 11
questionTypes.idea: Specifically tests 11. What makes it different: you use the difference between odd-position and even-position digit sums, not a plain digit sum.
questionTypes.problem: Is 91,839 divisible by 11?

steps.action: Number the digits from the right: 9 (1st), 3 (2nd), 8 (3rd), 1 (4th), 9 (5th).
steps.why: The 11-rule depends on position, so fix the positions from the right first.

steps.action: Add odd-position digits: 9 + 8 + 9 = 26. Add even-position digits: 3 + 1 = 4.
steps.why: Powers of 10 alternate +1, −1 under division by 11, which is exactly this odd-minus-even pattern.
A difference of 0 or any multiple of 11 means the number is divisible by 11.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Remainder via digit-sum / alternating-sum
questionTypes.idea: Asks the remainder when a number is divided by 9, 3 or 11 — not just yes/no. What makes it different: the remainder equals the digit sum mod 9 (or 3), or the alternating sum mod 11.
questionTypes.problem: Find the remainder when 74,532 is divided by 9.

steps.why: A number and its digit sum leave the same remainder when divided by 9.

steps.action: 21 is more than 9, so add again: 2 + 1 = 3.
steps.why: You may repeat the digit sum until it drops below 9; the remainder is unchanged.

steps.action: 3 is below 9, so the remainder is 3.
steps.why: Once the reduced digit sum is below the divisor, it is the remainder itself.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Divisibility by 7 or 13
questionTypes.idea: Tests 7 or 13, which have no simple digit-sum rule. What makes it different: use the chop-and-adjust step and repeat until the number is small.
questionTypes.problem: Is 1,071 divisible by 7?

steps.action: Drop the last digit (1), leaving 107, then subtract twice the dropped digit: 107 − 2×1 = 105.
steps.why: This removes a known multiple of 7, giving a smaller number with the same divisibility by 7.
Keep chopping until the number is small enough to judge at a glance.

steps.action: 0 is divisible by 7, so 1,071 is too.
steps.why: Reaching 0 (or a visible multiple of 7) confirms it — 1,071 = 7 × 153.

commonMistakes: Splitting a composite into factors that share a prime. To test 12, use the coprime pair 3 and 4 — not 2 and 6. The number 18 passes both ÷2 and ÷6, yet it is not divisible by 12.
Using a plain digit sum for 11. Eleven needs the ALTERNATING sum (odd positions minus even positions); a normal digit sum tests only 3 and 9.
For 4 and 8, checking the wrong number of end digits. Four looks at the last two digits, eight at the last three — swapping them gives wrong answers.
Treating a difference of 0 as 'not divisible' in the 11-rule. Zero is a multiple of 11, so a difference of 0 means the number IS divisible.
Thinking the repeated digit sum gives the remainder for 10. It gives the remainder on division by 9 (and by 3), never by 10.
Adding instead of subtracting in the ÷7 rule. For 7 you subtract 2 × (last digit); adding 4 × (last digit) is the rule for 13 — mixing them fails.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Group the rules by where you look: last 1 digit → 2, 5, 10; last 2 or 3 digits → 4, 8, 25; add the digits → 3, 9; alternate the digits → 11; split into coprime factors → 6, 12, 15. Only 7 and 13 need the chop-and-adjust trick.

crackIt.oneLine: Match the divisor to where you look — units digit (2/5/10), last two-or-three digits (4/8/25), digit sum (3/9), alternating sum (11), coprime split (6/12/15) — and only 7 and 13 need the chop trick.

shortcuts.name: 2, 5, 10 — glance at the units digit
shortcuts.method: Even → ÷2; ends in 0 or 5 → ÷5; ends in 0 → ÷10.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Only the last digit; ignore everything else.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: full division · Shortcut: read 1 digit.
shortcuts.whenFails: Never use last-digit alone for 3, 7, 9 or 11.

shortcuts.name: 4, 8, 25 — read the tail
shortcuts.method: ÷4: last 2 digits ÷4; ÷8: last 3 digits ÷8; ÷25: last 2 digits are 00/25/50/75.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Last two digits for 4 and 25, last three for 8.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: long division · Shortcut: divide a 2–3 digit tail.
shortcuts.whenFails: Don't apply directly to 6 or 12 — split those into coprime factors.

shortcuts.name: 3, 9 — add the digits
shortcuts.method: Digit sum ÷3 → ÷3; digit sum ÷9 → ÷9.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: full division · Shortcut: one digit sum.
shortcuts.whenWorks: 3, 9 (and their remainders — see the remainder trick).
shortcuts.whenFails: A digit sum does NOT test 6, 12 alone, and never tests 11.

shortcuts.name: 6, 12, 15 — coprime split
shortcuts.method: Test the two coprime factors: 6 = 2 & 3, 12 = 3 & 4, 15 = 3 & 5.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Run the two smaller rules; both must pass.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: divide by the big number · Shortcut: two quick rules.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any composite you can split into coprime factors.
shortcuts.whenFails: Picking non-coprime factors (2 & 6 for 12) can wrongly pass — always use coprime pieces.

shortcuts.name: 11 — alternating sum
shortcuts.method: (odd-position digits) − (even-position digits) = 0 or a multiple of 11.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Number the positions from the right, add alternately.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: divide by 11 · Shortcut: two small sums.
shortcuts.whenFails: A plain digit sum won't work; and remember a difference of 0 counts as divisible.

shortcuts.name: 7, 13 — chop and adjust
shortcuts.method: ÷7: rest − 2 × (last digit); ÷13: rest + 4 × (last digit); repeat.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Split off the last digit, adjust the rest, loop.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: long division · Shortcut: 2–3 chop steps.
shortcuts.whenWorks: 7 and 13, each with its own multiplier.
shortcuts.whenFails: Using the wrong multiplier — subtract 2× is for 7, add 4× is for 13.

shortcuts.name: Remainder — digit sum / alternating sum mod
shortcuts.method: Remainder by 9 = digit sum mod 9; by 3 = digit sum mod 3; by 11 = alternating sum mod 11.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Reduce the digit sum (or alternating sum) by the divisor.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: full division for a remainder · Shortcut: add digits.
shortcuts.whenFails: It gives no remainder for 7 or 13 — divide for real there.

patternGuide.use: read the last two (or three) digits.
split into coprime factors and test each.

patternGuide.signal: asks the REMAINDER by 3, 9 or 11 (not yes/no)
patternGuide.use: digit sum mod 9/3, or alternating sum mod 11.

patternGuide.signal: a digit is missing (* or a letter) for divisibility
patternGuide.use: set up the matching rule and solve for the digit.

drill.question: Smallest digit * in 5*2 so it is divisible by 3?

topics.description: Work with decimals and fractions and convert cleanly between them.

videos.label: Fractions & Decimals — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: Conversion, comparison & ordering methods for exams

learnIt.realLife: You are at a shop and three price tags read ₹0.7, ₹0.68 and ₹0.702 per gram. Which is cheapest? Or you split ₹4.5 among some friends and need it to come out evenly. Every time you handle money, marks, or measurements that are 'between whole numbers', you are working with decimal fractions — and the whole topic is just learning to read, compare, and calculate with those in-between numbers quickly and correctly.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A decimal fraction is simply a fraction whose denominator is a power of 10 — that is what the dot means. The digit right after the point is tenths (÷10), the next is hundredths (÷100), the next thousandths (÷1000), and so on. So 0.35 means 3 tenths and 5 hundredths, which is the same as 35/100. Because decimals and fractions are two ways of writing the same value, this topic is about moving smoothly between them, doing arithmetic (add, multiply, divide) without misplacing the point, comparing which is bigger, and knowing when a fraction turns into a neat ending decimal versus one that repeats forever.
learnIt.mathIntro: Three anchors carry the whole topic. First, the number of decimal places tells you the power of 10 underneath (2 places → /100). Second, when you multiply decimals you add up the decimal places; when you divide you shift the point in both numbers equally. Third, a fraction in lowest terms ends cleanly only when its denominator is built from 2s and 5s — any other prime (like 3 or 7) makes it repeat, and a repeating block can be turned back into a fraction using 9s and 0s.

formulas.label: Decimal ↔ fraction (place value)
formulas.formula: A decimal with n places = (its digits) ÷ 10ⁿ, then simplify. Fraction → decimal = numerator ÷ denominator.
formulas.breakdown: Each place after the point divides by another 10, so n decimal places sit over 10ⁿ. Going the other way, a fraction is a division you can carry out with long division.

formulas.label: Terminating vs recurring test
formulas.formula: A fraction in lowest terms terminates ⇔ its denominator's only prime factors are 2 and/or 5
formulas.breakdown: A terminating decimal is a fraction over some 10ⁿ = 2ⁿ×5ⁿ, so the denominator can only carry 2s and 5s. Any other prime cannot be cleared and forces the decimal to repeat.

formulas.label: Pure recurring → fraction
formulas.formula: 0.(block repeated) = block ÷ (as many 9s as digits in the block)
formulas.breakdown: If the whole part after the point repeats, the value equals the block over a string of 9s, one 9 per repeating digit — a standard result from the repeating pattern.

formulas.label: Mixed recurring → fraction
formulas.formula: Value = (all digits after point − non-recurring digits) ÷ (a 9 for each recurring digit, then a 0 for each non-recurring digit)
formulas.breakdown: The non-repeating digits after the point need 0s in the denominator, and the repeating ones need 9s, so the denominator mixes 9s then 0s. Subtract the non-recurring part on top.

formulas.label: Multiply and divide decimals
formulas.formula: Multiply: multiply as integers, then decimal places = sum of the factors' places.  Divide: shift the point in BOTH numbers until the divisor is whole.
formulas.breakdown: Multiplying tenths by hundredths gives thousandths, so the places add. Dividing is unchanged if you scale both numbers by the same power of 10.

formulas.label: Comparing decimals
formulas.formula: Pad all numbers to the same number of decimal places, then compare digit by digit from the left
formulas.breakdown: Adding trailing zeros does not change a decimal's value but lines them up so the first differing digit from the left decides which is larger.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Convert between fraction and decimal
questionTypes.idea: Gives a fraction to write as a decimal, or a decimal to write as a fraction in lowest terms. What makes it different: pure conversion using place value and simplification.
questionTypes.problem: Convert 5/8 to a decimal, and convert 0.35 to a fraction in lowest terms.

steps.why: A fraction is a division; carry it out adding zeros after the point.

steps.action: Decimal → fraction: 0.35 has 2 decimal places, so write 35/100.
steps.why: Two decimal places means hundredths, so the denominator is 10² = 100.

steps.action: Simplify 35/100 by dividing top and bottom by 5 → 7/20.
steps.why: Always reduce to lowest terms for the final answer.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Recurring decimal to fraction
questionTypes.idea: Gives a repeating decimal (a bar or dots over digits) to write as a fraction. What makes it different: you use the 9s-and-0s method, and pure vs mixed recurring are handled slightly differently.
questionTypes.problem: Convert 0.363636… (0.‾36) to a fraction.

steps.action: Identify the repeating block: '36', which has 2 digits.
steps.why: For a pure recurring decimal the block sits over as many 9s as its digit count.
Two repeating digits → a denominator of 99 (two 9s).
Reduce to lowest terms.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Multiplying and dividing decimals
questionTypes.idea: Asks for a product or quotient of decimals. What makes it different: the whole challenge is placing the decimal point — add places when multiplying, shift equally when dividing.

steps.why: Ignore the points at first and just multiply the digits.

steps.action: Count total decimal places: 0.6 has 1 and 0.05 has 2, so 3 places.
steps.why: The product's decimal places equal the sum of the factors' places.

steps.action: Place the point 3 from the right in 30 → 0.030 = 0.03.
steps.why: Insert leading zeros as needed to reach 3 decimal places.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Comparing and ordering
questionTypes.idea: Asks which decimal (or fraction) is greatest/smallest, or to arrange them in order. What makes it different: you line them up by place value, not by how many digits they have.
questionTypes.problem: Which is greatest: 0.7, 0.68, 0.702 or 0.71?

steps.action: Pad all to the same length: 0.700, 0.680, 0.702, 0.710.
steps.why: Equal decimal length lets you compare digit by digit fairly.

steps.action: Compare from the left: tenths are 7, 6, 7, 7 — 0.680 is out; then hundredths 0, 0, 1 → 0.710 leads.
steps.why: The first differing digit from the left decides which number is larger.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Simplifying a decimal expression
questionTypes.idea: Gives a long expression of decimals, often matching an algebra identity. What makes it different: you factor with an identity so most of it cancels, instead of squaring big decimals.

steps.action: Recognise the top as a² − b² with a = 0.48, b = 0.12 → (a + b)(a − b).
steps.why: a² − b² factorises, and one factor will match the denominator.
Putting the factorised top over the same denominator exposes the common factor.
The identical factor cancels top and bottom.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Terminating or recurring?
questionTypes.idea: Asks whether a fraction gives a terminating or a recurring decimal (or which of several does). What makes it different: you never divide — you factor the reduced denominator.
questionTypes.problem: Does 7/40 give a terminating or a recurring decimal?

steps.why: A fraction in lowest terms terminates only if the denominator's primes are just 2 and/or 5.
No other prime (such as 3 or 7) sits in the denominator to force repetition.
Confirms a clean, ending decimal.

commonMistakes: Miscounting decimal places in multiplication. 0.6 × 0.05 has 1 + 2 = 3 places, giving 0.03 — students who count only 2 places get 0.3, which is ten times too big.
Using 9s for a mixed recurring decimal as if it were pure. For 0.1666… the fraction is (16 − 1)/90 = 1/6, not 16/99.
Comparing decimals by digit count instead of value. 0.68 is not bigger than 0.7 just because it has more digits — pad to 0.70 vs 0.68 and compare from the left.
Forgetting to simplify a decimal-to-fraction answer. 0.35 = 35/100 must be reduced to 7/20.
Shifting the point in only one number when dividing. For 4.5 ÷ 0.9, move the point one place in BOTH to get 45 ÷ 9 = 5; shifting only the divisor gives a wrong answer.
Assuming every fraction terminates. 1/3 = 0.333… recurs because 3 divides no power of 10 — always check the denominator's primes first.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Multiplying decimals: add the decimal places. Recurring block → put it over 9s (one 9 per repeating digit) with a 0 for each non-repeating digit after the point. A fraction terminates only if its denominator is built from 2s and 5s alone.

crackIt.oneLine: Decimals are just fractions over powers of 10 — count places when you multiply, put the repeating block over 9s (and 0s) to un-recur, and a fraction ends cleanly only if its denominator is made of 2s and 5s.

shortcuts.name: Fraction ↔ decimal — divide or count places
shortcuts.method: Fraction→decimal: divide top by bottom. Decimal→fraction: digits over 10^(places), then simplify.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Number of decimal places = number of zeros in the denominator.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: hesitate over zeros · Shortcut: places = zeros.

shortcuts.name: Recurring → fraction — 9s and 0s
shortcuts.method: Pure: block over 999…(one 9 per digit). Mixed: (all digits − non-recurring digits) over (9s for recurring, 0s for non-recurring).
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Repeating digits → 9s; non-repeating digits after point → 0s.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: set x = 0.36… and do algebra · Shortcut: write it straight down.
shortcuts.whenFails: Terminating decimals — just use place value.

shortcuts.name: Multiply — count then place
shortcuts.method: Multiply as integers, then move the point left by the sum of the factors' decimal places.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Total places = places in factor 1 + places in factor 2.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: align points nervously · Shortcut: multiply then count.

shortcuts.name: Divide — shift both equally
shortcuts.method: Move the point in the divisor to make it whole, and move the dividend's point the same amount.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Identical number of shifts in both numbers.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: messy decimal division · Shortcut: turn both into whole numbers.
shortcuts.whenFails: Shifting only one number scales the answer wrongly.

shortcuts.name: Compare — pad and read left
shortcuts.method: Pad all to equal decimal length, then compare digit by digit from the left.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Add trailing zeros so every number has the same places.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: convert to fractions · Shortcut: pad and scan.
shortcuts.whenFails: Judging by digit count instead of value.

shortcuts.name: Expression — spot a²−b² and cancel
shortcuts.method: Look for (a²−b²)/(a−b) = a+b, or (a+b)² patterns, then cancel or collapse.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Identify a and b in the decimals; cancel the shared factor.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: square each decimal · Shortcut: factor and cancel.
shortcuts.whenFails: Random expressions with no identity — compute directly.

shortcuts.name: Terminate? — check the bottom's primes
shortcuts.method: In lowest terms, denominator with only 2s and 5s → terminates; any other prime → recurs.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: do the division · Shortcut: glance at the denominator.

patternGuide.signal: 'convert to a decimal / to a fraction'
a bar or dots over digits (recurring decimal)

patternGuide.use: multiply as integers, add the decimal places.
shift the point in both numbers equally.

patternGuide.signal: 'which is greatest / smallest / arrange in order'
patternGuide.use: pad to equal length, compare from the left.
factor with an identity and cancel.
reduce, then check the denominator has only 2s and 5s.

drill.question: Write 0.75 as a fraction in lowest terms.

topics.description: Use BODMAS and smart rounding to solve long expressions fast.

videos.note: Navneet Sir — concept + speed tricks for exams

videos.label: Simplification, Approximation & Percentage basics
videos.note: Beginner-friendly foundation class

learnIt.realLife: You are splitting a long bill in your head: 'two plates at ₹120, plus half of a ₹300 dessert, minus a ₹50 coupon'. You don't add left to right blindly — you know the 'two plates' and the 'half of' happen before the plus and minus, or the total comes out wrong. Simplification is doing a long expression in the correct order; approximation is quickly estimating a messy answer closely enough to pick the right option.
learnIt.plainMeaning: There is one agreed order for doing a mix of brackets, powers, ×, ÷, + and −. If everyone chose their own order, the same expression would give different answers, so we all follow the same rule (BODMAS). Simplification means applying that order carefully to reach the exact value. Approximation is a partner skill: when the answer choices are far apart, you round each number to a friendly value and estimate, instead of doing exact and slow arithmetic.
learnIt.mathIntro: BODMAS is the order: Brackets first, then Orders (powers and roots), then Division and Multiplication together from left to right, then Addition and Subtraction together from left to right. The word 'of' means multiply and is resolved with the bracket stage. For approximation, replace each number by the nearest round value, and recognise messy percents as clean fractions (25% is 1/4).

formulas.label: BODMAS — the order of operations
formulas.breakdown: One shared order guarantees one answer. Division and multiplication rank equally (do whichever comes first from the left), and addition and subtraction rank equally too. 'Of' means multiply and is done with the brackets.

formulas.label: 'of' and fraction of a quantity
formulas.formula: (a/b) of N = (a/b) × N ;   x% of N = (x/100) × N
formulas.breakdown: 'Of' is multiplication in disguise, but it binds early (with the brackets). A fraction or percent of a number simply scales that number.

formulas.label: Approximation by rounding
formulas.formula: Round each number to a nearby friendly value, compute, then choose the closest option
formulas.breakdown: When the options are far apart, an exact answer is wasted effort — round to the nearest ten, hundred or simple fraction and the rounded result lands on one option.

formulas.label: Near-percentages as clean fractions
formulas.breakdown: Recognising a messy percent as a clean fraction turns a hard decimal multiplication into an easy division.

formulas.label: Finding a missing number (?)
formulas.formula: Isolate ? by doing the inverse operation to both sides, undoing +/− before ÷/×
formulas.breakdown: Treat the expression as a balance and peel off the operations around ? in reverse BODMAS order until ? stands alone.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — BODMAS evaluation
questionTypes.idea: Gives an expression with a mix of operations and asks its exact value. What makes it different: the whole task is applying the correct order, not the arithmetic itself.
questionTypes.problem: Evaluate 36 ÷ 4 × 3 + 5 − 2.

steps.action: No brackets or powers, so do ÷ and × from the left: 36 ÷ 4 = 9, then 9 × 3 = 27.
steps.why: Division and multiplication share the top priority and are done left to right.

steps.action: Now do + and − from the left: 27 + 5 = 32, then 32 − 2 = 30.
steps.why: Addition and subtraction come last, also left to right.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — 'of' and fraction of a quantity
questionTypes.idea: Uses the word 'of', or a fraction/percent of a number. What makes it different: 'of' is multiplication that binds early, before a following ÷.
questionTypes.problem: Find 2/3 of 90 + 1/4 of 80.

steps.action: Resolve each 'of' first: 2/3 of 90 = 60 and 1/4 of 80 = 20.
steps.why: 'Of' means multiply and is handled early, before the addition.
Addition happens after the multiplications.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Approximation by rounding
questionTypes.idea: Says 'approximate value' or uses wavy numbers like 49.8. What makes it different: you deliberately round instead of computing exactly.
questionTypes.problem: Find the approximate value of 4987 ÷ 49.8 + 19.9.

steps.why: The options are spread out, so friendly round numbers are accurate enough.
Apply the order of operations to the rounded numbers.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Root and power approximation
questionTypes.idea: Asks the approximate value of an expression with square roots or powers of non-perfect numbers. What makes it different: you round each to the nearest perfect square (or round base).
questionTypes.problem: Find the approximate value of √626 × √99.

steps.why: Rounding to the nearest perfect square lets you take the root instantly.
Combine the approximate roots.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Find the missing number
questionTypes.idea: An expression equals a value with a '?' or blank to solve for. What makes it different: you undo the operations in reverse to isolate the unknown.

steps.why: Undo the +40 first (reverse BODMAS: addition is peeled before multiplication).
Undo the ×15 to leave ? alone.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Complex / continued fraction
questionTypes.idea: A fraction stacked inside another fraction. What makes it different: you must resolve the innermost bracket first and work outward.
questionTypes.problem: Simplify 1 + 1/(1 + 1/2).

steps.why: BODMAS resolves the deepest bracket before anything outside it.
Dividing by a fraction means multiplying by its reciprocal.
Add the outer 1 last.

commonMistakes: Working strictly left to right and ignoring priority. 12 + 6 ÷ 2 is 15, not 9 — the division happens before the addition.
Believing division always comes before multiplication. They share priority; do whichever appears first from the left.
Treating 'of' like an ordinary × done in the ÷× stage. In exam convention 'of' binds tighter — resolve it with the brackets, before a following ÷.
Over-rounding in approximation so the answer jumps to a wrong option. Round to the nearest convenient value (49.8 → 50, not 40).
Undoing operations in the wrong order when solving for '?'. Reverse BODMAS: peel +/− before ÷/×.
Starting a continued fraction from the outside. Always simplify the innermost bracket first.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Chant B-O-DM-AS: Brackets, Orders, then Division/Multiplication together left-to-right, then Addition/Subtraction together left-to-right. For approximation, round to friendly numbers and turn messy percents into clean fractions.

crackIt.oneLine: Simplification is one rule — BODMAS in order (Brackets, Orders, ÷× left-to-right, +− left-to-right) — and approximation is just rounding every number to a friendly value before you compute.

shortcuts.name: One ordered BODMAS pass
shortcuts.method: Scan once: brackets, then powers/roots, then all ÷× left to right, then all +− left to right.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Each operation is done in its priority slot.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Normal: guess the order · Shortcut: one ordered pass.
shortcuts.whenFails: Missing a hidden bracket or an 'of'.

shortcuts.name: 'Of' first
shortcuts.method: Turn 'of' into × and resolve it with the brackets.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: a/b of N = a/b × N, done before any following ÷.
shortcuts.whenFails: Treating 'of' as a normal × placed after a ÷.

shortcuts.name: Round-and-go
shortcuts.method: Replace each number with the nearest 10/100/simple fraction, then compute.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Every messy number → its round neighbour.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Full division vs one clean division.
shortcuts.whenFails: Options very close — compute more exactly.

shortcuts.name: Nearest perfect square / round base
shortcuts.method: √x ≈ √(nearest perfect square); a power ≈ nearest round base.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: x → the nearest perfect square under the root.

shortcuts.name: Percent → fraction swap
shortcuts.method: Swap near-percentages for clean fractions.

shortcuts.name: Reverse-BODMAS for '?'
shortcuts.method: Undo the operations around ? in reverse: +/− first, then ÷/×.

shortcuts.name: Inside-out fractions
shortcuts.method: Simplify the innermost bracket of a continued fraction first, then work outward.

patternGuide.signal: 'of', or a fraction/percent of a number
a root or power of a non-perfect number, approximate
a '?' or blank to solve for
a fraction stacked inside a fraction

topics.description: Roots and powers — simplify surds and apply the laws of indices.

learnIt.realLife: A square garden of area 20 square metres has a side of √20 metres — not a whole number. Rather than reach for a calculator, you learn to tidy √20 into 2√5, add and compare such roots, and fold powers like 2³ × 2⁴ into a single 2⁷. That neat handling of powers (indices) and roots (surds) is exactly this topic.
learnIt.plainMeaning: An index (also called a power or exponent) is shorthand for repeated multiplication: 2⁴ means 2 × 2 × 2 × 2. A surd is a root that does not come out to a whole number, like √2 or the cube root of 5 — we leave it in root form because its decimal never ends. This topic is the set of rules for combining, simplifying, and comparing powers and roots quickly and exactly, without a calculator.
learnIt.mathIntro: Every power rule comes from counting how many times the base is multiplied: multiplying powers adds the exponents, dividing subtracts them, and a power of a power multiplies them. A fractional exponent is a root — a^(1/n) means the n-th root of a. Surds simplify by pulling out perfect-square (or cube) factors, and an awkward surd in a denominator is cleared by multiplying by its conjugate.

formulas.label: Laws of indices
formulas.formula: aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ ;  aᵐ ÷ aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ ;  (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ ;  a⁰ = 1 ;  a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ
formulas.breakdown: Each power is repeated multiplication, so multiplying two powers pools the factors (add exponents), dividing cancels factors (subtract), and a power of a power repeats the repetition (multiply). Any nonzero base to the 0 is 1, and a negative power flips to the denominator.

formulas.label: Fractional exponents are roots
formulas.breakdown: The bottom of the fraction is the root to take, the top is the power to raise. Taking the n-th root n times rebuilds the number, which is why a^(1/n) means the n-th root.

formulas.label: Simplifying surds
formulas.breakdown: A root of a product splits into a product of roots, so you pull out any perfect-square factor as a whole number and leave the rest under the root.

formulas.label: Rationalising the denominator
formulas.breakdown: Multiplying by the conjugate uses (x+y)(x−y) = x²−y², which turns the surds in the denominator into whole numbers.

formulas.label: Solving with equal bases
formulas.formula: If aˣ = aʸ (same base a), then x = y
formulas.breakdown: A number raised to two powers can be equal only if the powers match, so rewrite both sides to the same base and equate the exponents.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Simplify using laws of indices
questionTypes.idea: Gives powers of the same base multiplied and divided. What makes it different: you only combine exponents, never expand the numbers.
questionTypes.problem: Simplify (a³ × a⁵) ÷ a².

steps.why: Multiplying powers of the same base adds the exponents.

steps.action: Divide by a²: a⁸ ÷ a² = a^(8−2) = a⁶.
steps.why: Dividing subtracts the exponents.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Fractional and negative exponents
questionTypes.idea: Asks the value of something like 16^(−3/4). What makes it different: you read the fraction as root-and-power and flip for a negative sign.
questionTypes.problem: Evaluate 16^(−3/4).

steps.why: A negative exponent flips the base to the denominator.
The 4 (bottom) is the root and the 3 (top) is the power.
Take the reciprocal of 8.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Simplify and add surds
questionTypes.idea: Gives roots to simplify and combine. What makes it different: only surds with the same root part can be added, so you must simplify first.
questionTypes.problem: Simplify √50 + √18.

steps.why: Pull out the perfect square 25.
Pull out the perfect square 9.
Same root part means you add the coefficients, like 5x + 3x.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Rationalise the denominator
questionTypes.idea: A fraction has a surd on the bottom. What makes it different: you multiply by the conjugate to clear the root.
questionTypes.problem: Rationalise 3/(√7 − 2).

steps.action: Multiply top and bottom by the conjugate (√7 + 2): 3(√7 + 2) / [(√7 − 2)(√7 + 2)].
steps.why: The conjugate clears the surd using (x−y)(x+y) = x²−y².
The cross terms cancel, leaving whole numbers.
Cancel the common factor 3.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Solve an exponential equation
questionTypes.idea: An unknown sits in the exponent. What makes it different: you rewrite both sides to one base, then equate the powers.

steps.why: Matching bases lets you compare exponents directly.
Equal bases mean equal exponents.
Solve the simple linear equation.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Compare surds
questionTypes.idea: Asks which root is larger when the root orders differ. What makes it different: you raise both to the LCM power to strip the roots.
questionTypes.problem: Which is greater: the cube root of 10, or √5?

steps.action: Root orders are 3 and 2; raise both to the LCM power 6.
steps.why: Raising to a common power removes the roots so you can compare plain numbers.
A cube root to the 6th is squared; a square root to the 6th is cubed.
The larger sixth power belongs to the larger surd.

commonMistakes: Adding exponents when the bases differ. 2³ × 5² is NOT 10⁵ — the add-exponents rule needs the SAME base.
Reading a fractional exponent upside down. In a^(m/n) the denominator n is the root and the numerator m is the power — 27^(2/3) is (³√27)², not (√27)³.
Leaving a surd unsimplified. √72 should become 6√2; a large number left under the root usually means it is not fully simplified.
Trying to add unlike surds. √2 + √3 cannot be merged into √5 — only surds with the same root part add.
Treating a⁰ as 0. Any nonzero base to the power 0 equals 1.
Making a negative exponent negative: a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ, it does not turn the answer negative.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Same base: × adds powers, ÷ subtracts, power-of-power multiplies. Fractional exponent: bottom is the root, top is the power. Surds: pull out perfect squares; clear a surd denominator with its conjugate; compare surds by raising to the LCM power.

crackIt.oneLine: Powers of the same base add on ×, subtract on ÷; a fractional exponent is 'root on the bottom, power on top'; simplify a surd by pulling out perfect squares and clear its denominator with the conjugate.

shortcuts.name: Index laws in one sweep
shortcuts.method: Same base: add exponents on ×, subtract on ÷, multiply for power-of-power.
shortcuts.whenFails: Different bases — factor to a common base first.

shortcuts.name: Root-bottom, power-top
shortcuts.method: a^(m/n) = (ⁿ√a)ᵐ; if negative, take the reciprocal first.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: n is the root, m is the power.

shortcuts.name: Pull out perfect squares
shortcuts.method: √(k²·b) = k√b, then add like surds.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: The largest perfect-square factor comes out.

shortcuts.name: Conjugate clear
shortcuts.method: Multiply by the conjugate to rationalise the denominator.
shortcuts.whenFails: A single surd — just multiply by that surd.

shortcuts.name: Same-base equate
shortcuts.method: Rewrite both sides to one base, set the exponents equal.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Match the base → compare the powers.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Exponential equations with a common base.
shortcuts.whenFails: Bases that cannot be matched (needs logarithms — another topic).

shortcuts.name: LCM-power compare
shortcuts.method: Raise competing surds to the LCM of their root orders.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Each surd ^ LCM removes the root.
shortcuts.whenFails: Same order — just compare the insides.

patternGuide.signal: powers of the same base multiplied or divided
a power like 27^(2/3) or a negative exponent
a root of a non-perfect number, or a sum of roots
patternGuide.use: pull out perfect squares, add like surds.

patternGuide.signal: a fraction with a surd in the denominator
aˣ = a number, solve for x
patternGuide.use: rewrite to the same base, equate exponents.

patternGuide.signal: 'which is greater' between roots of different orders
patternGuide.use: raise both to the LCM power.

groups.description: Percentages and the money maths built on them — profit, loss, discount and stacked percentage changes.

topics.description: Convert between fractions, decimals and percents and handle increase or decrease with ease.

videos.label: Percentage — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: 3M+ views — clear concept plus speed tricks

videos.label: Percentage Aptitude Tricks for Placements — Lecture 01
videos.note: PrepInsta — TCS / Infosys / Wipro focused

learnIt.realLife: Your friend scored 45 out of 50 in one test and you scored 72 out of 80 in another. Who did better? The totals are different, so the raw marks cannot be compared straight away. Percentages fix this by putting everything on the same scale — out of 100 — so any two things instantly become comparable.
learnIt.plainMeaning: The words 'per cent' literally mean 'out of 100'. So 30% simply means 30 out of every 100. If 30% of a class are girls, then for every 100 students, 30 are girls. A percentage is really just a fraction that always has 100 on the bottom.
learnIt.mathIntro: Because percent means 'out of 100', you can move between a fraction and a percentage any time: to turn a fraction into a percent, multiply by 100; to turn a percent into a fraction, divide by 100.

formulas.label: What percent one number is of another
formulas.breakdown: Part is the piece you have, Whole is the full amount, and × 100 turns the fraction into an out-of-100 number.

formulas.label: A percentage of a number
formulas.breakdown: Divide the percent by 100 to make it a fraction, then multiply by the number. Tip: x% of y = y% of x, so flip to whichever is easier.

formulas.label: Percentage change (increase or decrease)
formulas.breakdown: Subtract the old value from the new to get the change, divide by the OLD value (always the starting point), then × 100. A positive answer is a rise, a negative one a fall.

formulas.label: Two successive changes (net effect)
formulas.breakdown: Put the first change as a and the second as b, using a minus sign for a decrease. The extra ab/100 term captures that the second change sits on the already-changed amount.

formulas.label: Reverse a change (find the original)
formulas.breakdown: To undo a rise, divide by (1 + r/100); to undo a fall, divide by (1 − r/100). Never just subtract the percent from the final value.
formulas.example: After a 20% rise a price is ₹360: original = 360 ÷ 1.2 = ₹300.

formulas.label: Price rises, keep the spending the same
formulas.breakdown: If a price rises by r%, you must reduce how much you buy so the total bill stays fixed. (If the price FALLS by r%, you can use r ÷ (100 − r) × 100 % more.)
formulas.example: Sugar price up 25%: cut use by 25/125 × 100 = 20%.

formulas.label: Growth over n periods
formulas.breakdown: A quantity that changes by the same r% each period is multiplied by the factor (1 ± r/100) once per period — the same idea as compound interest.
formulas.example: Population 8000 growing 10% a year for 2 years: 8000 × 1.1² = 9680.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — What percent / percent of a number
questionTypes.idea: Gives a part and a whole (or a percent and a number). What makes it different: decide which is the 'whole' (the denominator).
questionTypes.problem: A student scored 45 out of 60. What percentage is that?

steps.why: The total marks are the whole.
Divide, then × 100 to get an out-of-100 figure.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Percentage increase or decrease
questionTypes.idea: Gives an old and a new value and asks the change. What makes it different: always divide by the OLD value.
questionTypes.problem: A salary rises from ₹20,000 to ₹23,000. What is the percentage increase?

steps.why: New minus old is the rise.
Percentage change is measured on the starting value.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Successive changes / raise then discount
questionTypes.idea: Applies two percentages one after another. What makes it different: they do NOT simply add — the second sits on the new amount.
questionTypes.problem: A price is increased by 20% and then decreased by 25%. What is the overall change?

steps.action: Use net % = a + b + ab/100 with a = +20, b = −25.
steps.why: One formula replaces two separate steps.
A negative result means an overall fall.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Reverse: find the original value
questionTypes.idea: Gives the value AFTER a change and asks for the value before it. What makes it different: you divide by the factor, never subtract.
questionTypes.problem: After a 20% discount, a shirt costs ₹480. What was its original price?

steps.action: A 20% cut leaves 80%, so Final = Original × 0.8.
steps.why: Paying 80% of the original after the discount.
Divide by the factor to undo the change.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Election / two-part split
questionTypes.idea: Two candidates share 100% of votes; the winner's lead is given. What makes it different: the margin is the difference of the two percentages.
questionTypes.problem: In a two-candidate election the winner gets 60% of the votes and wins by 900 votes. How many votes were cast?

steps.action: Loser gets 40%, so the margin = 60% − 40% = 20% of the total.
steps.why: The two shares add to 100%.
Turn the known margin back into the whole.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Exam pass marks (fail by / pass by)
questionTypes.idea: Gives the pass percentage and how much a student missed (or exceeded) it. What makes it different: score + shortfall = pass mark = pass% of the maximum.
questionTypes.problem: A student needs 40% to pass. She scores 150 and fails by 30 marks. Find the maximum marks.

steps.why: She was 30 short of passing, so the pass mark is 180.
The pass mark equals 40% of the maximum.

questionTypes.name: Type 7 — Price up, keep spending fixed
questionTypes.idea: A price rises and you must buy less to keep the bill the same. What makes it different: the cut is r/(100+r), not the same r%.
questionTypes.problem: The price of petrol rises by 25%. By what percent must a driver cut usage to keep the fuel bill unchanged?

steps.why: Spending = price × quantity; to hold it fixed, quantity must fall by this fraction.
So a 25% price rise needs a 20% cut in usage.

commonMistakes: Taking the second percentage on the original amount. A 25% rise then a 20% fall is NOT 'up 5%' (25 − 20). Each percent sits on a different base, so apply them one after the other (or use a + b + ab/100).
Mixing up 'percent' and 'percentage points'. Going from 20% to 25% is a rise of 5 percentage points, but that is a 25% increase in the rate itself.
In percentage change, dividing by the new value instead of the old one. Always divide by where you started.
Reversing a change by subtracting. After a 20% rise to ₹360 the original is 360 ÷ 1.2 = ₹300, NOT 360 − 20% = ₹288. Divide by the factor, never subtract.
In price-rise / consumption questions, cutting by the same r%. A 25% price rise needs only a 25/125 = 20% cut in usage, because the cut is measured on the higher price.
Turning 'of' into a plus. '20% of 150' means 20/100 × 150, never 20 + 150.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Read it straight off: 'of' means × and 'per cent' means ÷ 100, so '20% of 150' is 20 ÷ 100 × 150. Memorise the friendly fractions — 10% = 1/10, 20% = 1/5, 25% = 1/4, 50% = 1/2, 12.5% = 1/8, 33⅓% = 1/3 — and to REVERSE a change always divide by the factor (÷1.2 to undo +20%).

crackIt.oneLine: Turn every percentage into an easy fraction, use 'x% of y = y% of x' to flip the hard number onto the easy one, and reach for one dedicated shortcut per question type (net-change, reverse, margin, pass-marks, consumption).

shortcuts.name: Friendly-fraction / flip
shortcuts.method: Swap the percent for its fraction, or use x% of y = y% of x.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: 10%=1/10, 20%=1/5, 25%=1/4, 50%=1/2, 12.5%=1/8, 33⅓%=1/3. Flip so the hard number becomes the fraction.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any 'percent of a number' with a nice percentage.
shortcuts.whenFails: Odd percentages like 37% — just compute directly.

shortcuts.name: Net-change formula
shortcuts.method: Two successive changes → net % = a + b + ab/100.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: a = first change, b = second (minus sign for a decrease).
shortcuts.timeSaved: One line vs two full steps.
shortcuts.whenFails: Three or more changes — apply the factors one at a time.

shortcuts.name: Reverse with division
shortcuts.method: Original = Final ÷ (1 ± r/100).
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Divide by 1 + r/100 to undo a rise, 1 − r/100 to undo a fall.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Avoids the wrong 'subtract the percent' answer.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any 'find the original before the change' question.
shortcuts.whenFails: Treating Final − r% as the original.

shortcuts.name: Margin = whole
shortcuts.method: Election / split: (lead %) of total = the given lead in numbers.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Lead % = winner% − loser%; divide the vote lead by it.
shortcuts.timeSaved: No need to find each candidate's votes.
shortcuts.whenFails: More than two candidates (shares don't reduce to one margin).

shortcuts.name: Pass-mark trick
shortcuts.method: Pass mark = score + shortfall = pass% × Max.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Add what the student missed to their score, set equal to pass% of Max.
shortcuts.whenFails: When the student PASSES by m — then pass mark = score − m.

shortcuts.name: Consumption cut
shortcuts.method: Price up r% → cut usage by r/(100 + r).
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: r = price rise. (For a price FALL use r/(100 − r) to buy more.)
shortcuts.whenFails: When the bill itself is allowed to change.

patternGuide.use: (Part/Whole)×100, or flip to a friendly fraction.

patternGuide.signal: 'increased by a% then decreased by b%'
'after the change it became …, find original'

patternGuide.use: Lead% of total = the vote margin.

patternGuide.signal: 'needs …% to pass, fails by / passes by'
patternGuide.use: Pass mark = score ± gap = pass% × Max.

drill.question: Winner 55%, loser 45%, wins by 500; total votes?
Pass 33%, scores 120, fails by 30; max marks?

topics.description: Cost price, selling price, marked price, profit, loss and their percentages.

videos.label: Profit & Loss — best tricks & shortcuts with PYQs
videos.note: Concept + previous-year question patterns

learnIt.realLife: You buy a cricket bat for ₹800 and later sell it to a friend for ₹1000. You made ₹200 — but was it a good deal? In percentage terms you gained 25% on the money you spent. Profit and loss is exactly this: comparing what you paid (cost price) with what you got back (selling price), and expressing the gap as a percentage of what you paid.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Cost Price (CP) is what the seller paid; Selling Price (SP) is what the seller sells for. If SP is higher it is a profit, if lower a loss. The single most important rule: profit% and loss% are ALWAYS measured on the cost price, never the selling price, unless a question specifically says otherwise. Marked Price (MP) is the tag/sticker price before any cut, and a discount is a percentage taken off the MP.
learnIt.mathIntro: Profit = SP − CP and Loss = CP − SP. Turn either into a percentage by dividing by CP and multiplying by 100. To go the other way — from a cost price and a target profit% to the selling price — multiply CP by (100 + profit%)/100. Discount works the same way but off the marked price: SP = MP × (100 − discount%)/100.

formulas.label: Profit and loss (rupee amount)
formulas.formula: Profit = SP − CP ;  Loss = CP − SP
formulas.breakdown: Get back more than you paid and it is a profit; less and it is a loss. This gap in rupees is the raw profit or loss before any percentage.

formulas.label: Profit% and loss% (always on CP)
formulas.breakdown: Percentage is measured against the money you invested — the cost price — so CP is always the denominator.

formulas.label: Selling price from cost price (and back)
formulas.breakdown: Add the percent for a profit, subtract for a loss. To recover CP from SP, divide by that same factor instead of multiplying.

formulas.label: Marked price and discount
formulas.breakdown: A discount is a percentage cut from the marked (tag) price, and what remains is the selling price.

formulas.label: Same SP, equal % gain and loss
formulas.breakdown: Selling two items at the same price, one at +x% and one at −x%, is always a net loss because the loss falls on the costlier article. Its size is (x/10)²%.

formulas.label: Dishonest dealer (false weight)
formulas.breakdown: A dealer who hands over less than the true weight but charges the full price gains on the shortfall, measured against what he actually gives.

formulas.label: Cost of x articles = SP of y articles
formulas.breakdown: If the money from selling y items equals the cost of x items and x > y, the extra items are pure profit.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Find profit% or loss%
questionTypes.idea: Gives both CP and SP and asks the percentage. What makes it different: you must remember the percent is on CP.
questionTypes.problem: A man buys a chair for ₹600 and sells it for ₹690. Find his profit percent.

steps.why: Selling price is higher, so it is a profit of 90 rupees.
Percentage is taken on the cost price.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Find SP from CP and profit% (or reverse)
questionTypes.idea: Gives CP and a target percent, asks the price (or gives SP and percent, asks CP). What makes it different: you build or undo the multiplying factor.
questionTypes.problem: A trader buys goods for ₹1200 and wants a 20% profit. At what price should he sell?

steps.why: Add the profit percent to 100 because he wants to sell above cost.
Compute the selling price.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Marked price, discount and profit together
questionTypes.idea: Gives a marked price, a discount and a cost, and asks the profit. What makes it different: you must not confuse MP with CP.
questionTypes.problem: A shopkeeper marks a watch at ₹2000 and gives a 10% discount. If it cost him ₹1500, find his profit percent.

steps.why: Discount is 10% off the marked price.
Profit is measured on the cost price, not the marked price.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Two articles at the same SP
