questionTypes.idea: Sells two items at the same price, one at +x% and one at −x%. What makes it different: the answer is always a fixed net loss, never break-even.
questionTypes.problem: A man sells two cycles at ₹2400 each. On one he gains 20% and on the other he loses 20%. Find his overall gain or loss percent.

steps.action: Equal % gain and loss at the same SP always gives a net LOSS of (x/10)²%.
steps.why: The loss falls on the costlier article, so it outweighs the gain.
Apply the rule with x = 20.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Dishonest dealer / false weight
questionTypes.idea: Sells at cost price but uses a short weight. What makes it different: the gain comes purely from the weight shortfall.
questionTypes.problem: A grocer sells rice at cost price but uses a 900 g weight instead of 1000 g. Find his gain percent.

steps.action: He gives only 900 g but charges for 1000 g; error = 100 g.
steps.why: The shortfall is the source of his gain.
The gain is measured on what he actually hands over (900 g).

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Cost of x = selling price of y
questionTypes.idea: States that the CP of x articles equals the SP of y articles. What makes it different: no rupee value is needed — just the counts.
questionTypes.problem: If the cost price of 15 pens equals the selling price of 12 pens, find the profit percent.

steps.why: Selling 12 pens brings in the cost of 15, so 3 pens' worth is profit.
Compute the percentage.

commonMistakes: Taking the percent on the selling price instead of the cost price. Unless the question says 'on SP', profit% and loss% are always on CP.
Thinking equal % gain and loss cancel out. Two items at the same SP with +x% and −x% always give a net loss of (x/10)²%, never break-even.
Confusing marked price with cost price. Discount is off the MP, but profit is measured against the CP — they are different numbers.
Forgetting to divide by 100: SP = CP × (100 ± %)/100, not CP × (100 ± %).
In false-weight problems, dividing by the true weight. The gain is on what is actually given, so 100/900, not 100/1000.
Recovering CP by subtracting the percent from SP. You must divide SP by (100 ± %)/100, not subtract.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Everything is measured on Cost Price. SP = CP × (100 ± %)/100 — plus for profit, minus for loss. Same SP with equal ± x% is always a loss of (x/10)². Dishonest weight: error over what you actually gave.

crackIt.oneLine: Profit and loss is always a percentage of the COST price: SP = CP × (100 ± %)/100 — add for profit, subtract for loss — and a discount does the same off the marked price.

shortcuts.name: Multiplying factor for SP
shortcuts.method: SP = CP × (100 ± %)/100 in one multiplication.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: % added for profit, subtracted for loss.
shortcuts.whenFails: Percent stated on SP, not CP.

shortcuts.name: (x/10)² net-loss rule
shortcuts.method: Same SP, +x% and −x% → net loss (x/10)²%.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Two items, same SP, equal ± percent.

shortcuts.name: False-weight gain
shortcuts.method: Gain% = error/(true − error) × 100.
shortcuts.whenFails: Also marking up the price (combine effects).

shortcuts.name: x-over-y count trick
shortcuts.method: CP of x = SP of y → gain% = (x−y)/y × 100.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: x = cost count, y = sell count.
shortcuts.whenFails: Mixing which count is which — it is a loss if y > x.

shortcuts.name: Successive discount = single
shortcuts.method: Two discounts a% and b% → single = a + b − ab/100.

patternGuide.signal: CP and SP both given, asks percent
CP and a target percent, asks price
SP and percent given, asks CP
two items, same selling price, +x% and −x%
false / short weight at cost price
'cost of x equals selling price of y'

drill.question: Sell two at same price, ±10% → net?
Uses 800 g for 1 kg at cost → gain%?

topics.description: Marked price, discount and the link between discount and profit.

learnIt.realLife: A ₹2000 jacket has a '25% OFF' tag, so you pay ₹1500. The shop still profits, because the ₹2000 was the marked (inflated) price, not what the jacket actually cost them. Discount is the cut a shop advertises off its marked price — and understanding it lets you see the real price you pay and the real profit the shop keeps.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Marked Price (MP) is the printed tag price. Discount is a percentage knocked off that MP, and what you actually pay is the Selling Price (SP). A discount is always calculated on the MARKED price — not on the cost price, and not on an already-reduced price unless the discounts are applied one after another (called 'successive'). Shops mark prices high on purpose so that even after a discount they still make a profit over their cost.
learnIt.mathIntro: SP = MP × (100 − d)/100. If you know MP and SP, the discount% is (MP − SP)/MP × 100. When two discounts are given one after another they do NOT simply add — the second is taken on the already-reduced price, so the single equivalent is a + b − ab/100.

formulas.label: Selling price after discount
formulas.breakdown: Knock the discount percent off 100 and take that fraction of the marked price.

formulas.label: Discount percent
formulas.breakdown: The rupee discount is MP − SP, taken as a percentage OF the marked price.

formulas.label: Single equivalent of two discounts
formulas.breakdown: The second discount hits the already-reduced price, so you lose the small overlap ab/100 from the plain sum.

formulas.label: Marked price for a target profit
formulas.breakdown: Mark up enough so that after the discount is removed, the selling price still gives the desired profit over cost.

formulas.label: 'Buy x get y free' as a discount
formulas.breakdown: You pay for x items but take home x + y, so the free y is the discount relative to the total received.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Selling price after discount
questionTypes.idea: Gives MP and discount%, asks the price paid. What makes it different: the discount is taken off the marked price only.
questionTypes.problem: A jacket marked at ₹2500 is sold at a 20% discount. Find the selling price.

steps.why: Take 80% of the marked price after removing the 20%.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Find the discount percent
questionTypes.idea: Gives MP and SP, asks the percent. What makes it different: the gap is divided by MP, not SP.
questionTypes.problem: An item marked ₹1200 is sold for ₹900. Find the discount percent.

steps.why: The cut is marked price minus selling price.
The percent is taken on the marked price.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Successive discounts
questionTypes.idea: Two discounts one after another. What makes it different: the second acts on the already-reduced price, so they never simply add.
questionTypes.problem: Two successive discounts of 20% and 25% are given on a marked price of ₹1000. Find the final selling price.

steps.why: Apply the 20% off the marked price.
The 25% applies to the reduced 800, not to the original 1000.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Marked price for a target profit
questionTypes.idea: Wants a profit even after a discount, asks the MP. What makes it different: profit and discount act on different bases (CP and MP).
questionTypes.problem: A shopkeeper wants a 25% profit on a cost of ₹480 even after giving a 20% discount. What should the marked price be?

steps.why: The final selling price must give 25% over cost.
The 20% discount must still leave 600, so mark up to 750.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Compare two offers
questionTypes.idea: Asks which scheme is better. What makes it different: you convert each offer to a single equivalent percent first.
questionTypes.problem: Which is a bigger discount: a single 40%, or two successive discounts of 25% and 20%?

steps.why: Combine the two successive cuts into one.

steps.action: Both equal 40%, so the offers are identical.
steps.why: Compare the single equivalents.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — 'Buy x get y free'
questionTypes.idea: A free-item scheme, asks the effective discount. What makes it different: the discount is on the total quantity received.
questionTypes.problem: A shop offers 'buy 4 get 1 free'. What is the effective discount percent?

steps.action: You pay for 4 but take home 5; the free item is 1 out of 5.
steps.why: The discount is relative to the total quantity received.
Apply y/(x + y).

commonMistakes: Adding successive discounts directly. 20% then 25% is NOT 45% — the second acts on the reduced price, giving 40%.
Taking the discount on the cost price or the selling price. Discount is always on the MARKED price.
Confusing 'buy 3 get 1 free' with 25% off the 3 you pay for. It is 1 out of 4 total = 25%, computed on x + y.
Assuming a discount means the shop lost money. Marked prices are inflated so a shop can discount and still profit.
For target-profit problems, marking up by just (profit% + discount%). Use MP = CP × (100+profit)/(100−discount), because the two percents act on different bases.
Reversing the discount%: dividing (MP − SP) by SP instead of by MP.
learnIt.memoryTrick: SP = MP × (100 − d)/100. Two discounts never add — use a + b − ab/100. 'Buy x get y free' = y/(x+y). To profit after a discount, mark up to CP × (100+profit)/(100−discount).

crackIt.oneLine: A discount is a percentage off the MARKED price — SP = MP × (100 − d)/100 — and two stacked discounts combine as a + b − ab/100, never as a plain sum.

shortcuts.name: a + b − ab/100
shortcuts.method: Single equivalent of two discounts.
shortcuts.whenFails: Three or more — chain the factors instead.

shortcuts.name: Reverse for MP
shortcuts.method: MP = CP × (100 + profit)/(100 − discount).

shortcuts.name: Free-item discount
shortcuts.whenFails: Free item is a different, cheaper product.

shortcuts.name: Discount% from prices
shortcuts.method: (MP − SP)/MP × 100.

shortcuts.name: Compare via single equivalent
shortcuts.method: Convert every offer to one single %, then compare.
shortcuts.whenFails: Offers mixing free items and cashback.

patternGuide.signal: MP and discount% given, asks price
MP and SP given, asks percent
profit wanted after a discount, asks MP

patternGuide.use: convert each to a single equivalent %.

drill.question: CP 100, want 20% profit after 20% discount → MP?

topics.description: Apply two or more percentage changes one after another the right way.

learnIt.realLife: Your salary goes up 10% this year and another 10% next year — but the second 10% is on the already-raised salary, so your two-year rise is not 20%, it is a bit more (21%). Any time one percentage change is followed by another, the second acts on the new amount, and successive percentages is the tool for stacking them correctly.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A successive percentage change means a second change is applied AFTER the first, on the updated value — not on the original. Because the base changes between steps, the two percentages never simply add. This shows up everywhere: back-to-back salary hikes, prices that rise then fall, populations that grow year on year, and stacked discounts.
learnIt.mathIntro: The clean way is multiplying factors: a 10% rise is ×1.10, a 20% fall is ×0.80; multiply the factors in order and read off the net change. The formula version for two changes a% and b% (each signed) is a + b + ab/100. A special case: a rise then a fall by the same x% always leaves a net LOSS of x²/100 %.

formulas.label: Net of two successive changes
formulas.formula: Net% = a + b + (a×b)/100   (a, b signed: + rise, − fall)
formulas.breakdown: a and b are the two changes; the ab/100 term is the overlap, because the second change acts on the already-changed value.

formulas.label: Multiplying-factor method
formulas.breakdown: Turn each change into a factor and multiply in sequence; the product is the overall factor.

formulas.label: Rise then fall by the same %
formulas.breakdown: Going up x% and back down x% never returns to the start, because the fall is taken on a larger amount.

formulas.label: Restoring the original
formulas.formula: After a fall of x%: rise = x/(100 − x) × 100 % ;  after a rise of x%: fall = x/(100 + x) × 100 %
formulas.breakdown: Because the base has shrunk (or grown), the reverse percent is larger (or smaller) than x.

formulas.label: Effect on a product (area, expenditure)
formulas.formula: If two factors change by a% and b%, the product changes by a + b + ab/100
formulas.breakdown: Area = length × breadth (and expenditure = price × quantity), so changing both is exactly two successive percentage changes on the product.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Two successive changes
questionTypes.idea: Two rises/falls one after another, asks the net percent. What makes it different: you must include the overlap term.
questionTypes.problem: The price of a product rises 20% and then rises another 30%. Find the total percentage increase.

steps.why: The second rise is on the already-increased price, so add the overlap term.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Rise then fall by the same %
questionTypes.idea: Up x% then down x% (or vice versa). What makes it different: the answer is a fixed net loss, never zero.
questionTypes.problem: A number is increased by 25% and then decreased by 25%. Find the net change.

steps.action: Rise then fall by the same x% gives −x²/100.
steps.why: The decrease acts on a larger number, so you end below the start.
Apply with x = 25.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Value over years
questionTypes.idea: A value grows or falls by a percent each year. What makes it different: each year's change is on the new value, so you multiply factors.
questionTypes.problem: A town's population is 20000 and grows 10% each year. Find the population after 2 years.

steps.why: A 10% rise is a factor of 1.1.
The second year's 10% is on the new population.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Find the original
questionTypes.idea: Gives the final value after changes, asks the starting value. What makes it different: you divide by the factor, not subtract.
questionTypes.problem: After a 20% increase, a salary becomes ₹36000. Find the original salary.

steps.why: A 20% increase multiplies the original by 1.2.
Divide to undo the increase.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Effect on area / expenditure
questionTypes.idea: Two dimensions (or price and quantity) change. What makes it different: the product changes by the two-successive-change formula.
questionTypes.problem: The length of a rectangle is increased by 20% and its breadth decreased by 10%. Find the percentage change in area.

steps.why: Area is length × breadth, so both changes act successively on the product.
Compute with signs.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Restore the original
questionTypes.idea: A value changes and must be brought back. What makes it different: the restoring percent differs from the original change because the base moved.
questionTypes.problem: A shopkeeper reduces a price by 20%. By what percent must he now increase it to bring it back to the original?

steps.action: After a 20% fall, the base is smaller, so the restoring rise = x/(100 − x) × 100.
steps.why: The increase is measured on the reduced price, which is less than the original.
Apply with x = 20.

commonMistakes: Adding the two percentages directly. +20% then +30% is 56%, not 50% — the ab/100 overlap is missing.
Thinking +x% then −x% returns to the original. It is always a net loss of x²/100%.
Assuming the restoring percent equals the original change. A 20% fall needs a 25% rise back, not 20%.
Applying the second percent to the original value instead of the updated one.
Sign errors in a + b + ab/100 — a decrease must be entered as a negative number, and so must its product term.
Forcing the two-change formula on 3+ changes. Use multiplying factors and multiply them all.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Two successive changes: a + b + ab/100 (signs matter). Same up-then-down x% is always −x²/100. To restore a fall of x%, rise by x/(100−x)×100. When in doubt, just multiply the factors.

crackIt.oneLine: Successive percentage changes never add — combine two of them with a + b + ab/100 (signs included), or just multiply the factors like ×1.2 × 0.9.

shortcuts.name: a + b + ab/100
shortcuts.method: Two signed changes → one net %.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: a, b the changes; + rise, − fall.

shortcuts.name: Factor multiply
shortcuts.method: Multiply (1 ± r/100) for every change.

shortcuts.name: −x²/100 same up-down
shortcuts.method: Rise then fall by the same x% → loss x²/100%.

shortcuts.name: Restore rise x/(100−x)
shortcuts.method: After an x% fall, rise x/(100−x)×100 to restore.

shortcuts.name: Product-change = successive
shortcuts.method: Length ±a%, breadth ±b% → area a + b + ab/100.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: a, b the two dimension changes.

shortcuts.name: Reverse by dividing
shortcuts.method: Undo a change by dividing by its factor.
shortcuts.whenFails: Subtracting the percent from the final.

patternGuide.signal: two changes one after another, asks net
increase then decrease by the SAME percent
grows / falls by the same percent each year

patternGuide.use: a + b + ab/100 on the product.

patternGuide.signal: price cut, then restore to original

groups.description: Compare and share quantities, split profit by investment, and mix things in the right proportion.

topics.description: Compare quantities, split things in a given ratio and solve direct and inverse proportion.

videos.label: Ratio & Proportion — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: Concept + the assume-k shortcut for tough sums

learnIt.realLife: Two friends put money into a food stall — one ₹300, the other ₹200 — and later split the profit. Fairness says the profit should be shared 3:2, matching what each put in. A ratio is just this idea of comparing two amounts, and a proportion is keeping two ratios equal so that a sharing or scaling stays fair.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A ratio a:b compares two quantities by division — it says how many times one is of the other, without fixing the actual amounts. 3:2 could be 3 and 2, or 30 and 20, or 300 and 200 — all the same ratio. A proportion is a statement that two ratios are equal (a:b = c:d). We use ratios to share a total fairly and proportions to scale recipes, maps, speeds and prices.
learnIt.mathIntro: Simplify a ratio by dividing both parts by their HCF. To divide a total in a ratio a:b:c, give each part its fraction of the whole. In a proportion a:b = c:d the cross-products are equal: a×d = b×c. Direct proportion means both quantities rise together (y = kx); inverse proportion means one rises as the other falls (their product xy stays constant).

formulas.label: Simplifying a ratio
formulas.formula: a : b = (a÷h) : (b÷h), where h = HCF(a, b)
formulas.breakdown: Dividing both parts by their common factor gives the smallest whole-number form without changing the comparison.

formulas.label: Dividing a quantity in a ratio
formulas.formula: Share of a part = (that part ÷ sum of parts) × Total
formulas.breakdown: Add the ratio numbers to get the total number of equal parts, then hand each its fraction of the whole.

formulas.label: Proportion (cross-multiplication)
formulas.formula: a : b = c : d  ⇒  a×d = b×c
formulas.breakdown: If two ratios are equal, the product of the outer terms equals the product of the inner terms — the fast way to find a missing value.

formulas.label: Mean and third proportional
formulas.formula: Mean proportional of a, b = √(ab) ;  third proportional to a, b = b²/a
formulas.breakdown: The mean proportional is the number that sits between a and b in a:x = x:b; the third proportional continues a:b = b:x.

formulas.label: Combining ratios
formulas.formula: A:B and B:C → make the common B equal, then read A:B:C
formulas.breakdown: Scale each ratio so the shared term matches, then the three-part ratio can be written directly.

formulas.label: Direct and inverse proportion
formulas.formula: Direct: y = kx (y/x constant) ;  Inverse: xy = k (product constant)
formulas.breakdown: Direct means more gives more at a fixed rate; inverse means more of one gives less of the other, keeping the product fixed.
formulas.example: Direct: 2 pens ₹10 → 5 pens ₹25. Inverse: 4 men 6 days → 8 men 3 days.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Simplify or combine ratios
questionTypes.idea: Gives two ratios with a shared term and asks the combined ratio. What makes it different: you must match the common term first.
questionTypes.problem: If A:B = 2:3 and B:C = 4:5, find A:B:C.

steps.action: B is 3 in the first ratio and 4 in the second; the LCM is 12.
steps.why: The shared term must be the same value before the ratios can be merged.
Now B is 12 in both, so read off all three parts.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Divide a quantity in a ratio
questionTypes.idea: Splits a total among parts in a given ratio. What makes it different: the ratio numbers are 'parts', not amounts.
questionTypes.problem: Divide ₹1200 among three people in the ratio 3:4:5.

steps.why: This is how many equal parts the money is split into.
Each person gets their number of parts out of 12.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Proportion / missing term
questionTypes.idea: Two equal ratios with one unknown. What makes it different: cross-multiplication solves it in one step.
questionTypes.problem: Find x if 8 : 12 = x : 30.

steps.why: In a proportion the product of the extremes equals the product of the means.
Solve for x.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — A changing ratio
questionTypes.idea: A ratio changes when a number is added or removed. What makes it different: you write the terms as multiples of k and solve.
questionTypes.problem: Two numbers are in the ratio 3:4. If 6 is added to each, the ratio becomes 4:5. Find the numbers.

steps.why: Any pair in the ratio 3:4 can be written as multiples of the same k.
Cross-multiply the new ratio and solve.
Substitute k = 6.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Direct proportion
questionTypes.idea: More of one means proportionally more of the other. What makes it different: you scale by the ratio of quantities.
questionTypes.problem: If 5 books cost ₹200, what do 8 books cost?

steps.action: Cost is directly proportional to the number of books.
steps.why: More books cost proportionally more at the same price each.
Scale the cost by the ratio of quantities.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Inverse proportion
questionTypes.idea: More of one means less of the other. What makes it different: the product stays constant.
questionTypes.problem: 12 workers finish a job in 8 days. How many days for 16 workers at the same pace?

steps.action: Workers and days are inversely proportional — more workers, fewer days.
steps.why: The total work (workers × days) is fixed.
Keep the product constant.

commonMistakes: Adding a constant to a ratio directly. If A:B = 3:4, then after adding 6 to each it is NOT 9:10 — you must use 3k, 4k and solve.
Treating the ratio numbers as the amounts. In 3:4 out of ₹1400 the shares are 600 and 800, not ₹3 and ₹4.
Confusing direct and inverse. More men take FEWER days (inverse), but produce MORE work (direct).
Forgetting to bring the common term to the same value before combining ratios.
Comparing ratios by first terms only. Compare 3:5 and 4:7 by cross-multiplying (3×7 vs 4×5 = 21 vs 20), not 3 vs 4.
Mean proportional errors: it is √(ab), not (a+b)/2 (that is the arithmetic mean).
learnIt.memoryTrick: Ratio numbers are 'parts' — add them, then give each its fraction of the total. Proportion: cross-multiply (extremes = means). To change a ratio, write terms as 3k, 4k and solve. Direct = scale the same way; inverse = keep the product fixed.

crackIt.oneLine: Treat ratio numbers as 'parts': add them for sharing, cross-multiply for proportions, and write unknowns as multiples of k (3k, 4k) whenever the ratio changes.

shortcuts.name: Sum-of-parts sharing
shortcuts.method: Share = (part / sum) × total.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: part = the ratio number, sum = all numbers added.
shortcuts.whenFails: Parts are not a clean ratio.

shortcuts.name: Assume-k
shortcuts.method: Write the quantities as 3k, 4k... then solve one equation.
shortcuts.whenFails: Different ratios with no shared k.

shortcuts.name: Cross-multiply
shortcuts.method: a:b = c:d → ad = bc.

shortcuts.name: Common-term merge
shortcuts.method: Scale both ratios so the shared term matches (its LCM).

shortcuts.name: Direct = multiply by quantity ratio
shortcuts.method: New = old × (new qty / old qty).

shortcuts.name: Inverse = keep product
shortcuts.method: x₁y₁ = x₂y₂.

shortcuts.name: Cross-compare ratios
shortcuts.method: To compare a:b and c:d, compare a·d with c·b.

patternGuide.signal: 'ratio is x:y, if k added/removed becomes ...'

topics.description: Split profit between partners based on capital and time invested.

videos.label: Partnership & Share — placement aptitude (MNC pattern)
videos.note: Capital×time profit-ratio method with worked MNC questions

learnIt.realLife: Three friends open a tea stall. Ravi puts in ₹5000, Sita ₹3000, and Amit joins four months late with ₹6000. At year-end they share the profit — but not equally. Fairness ties each person's share to how much money they put in AND for how long. Partnership is the maths of splitting profit by capital and time.
learnIt.plainMeaning: When people invest together in a business, the profit is divided in proportion to each person's contribution. If everyone invests for the same time, the split is simply the ratio of the amounts invested. If they invest for different lengths of time, we weight each investment by its months — money that stays longer earns a bigger share. The single measure of contribution is 'capital × time'.
learnIt.mathIntro: For each partner compute capital × time (in the same unit, usually months). The profit is shared in the ratio of these products. A working partner may first take a salary or commission off the top; the remaining profit is then split by capital × time.

formulas.label: Same time → capital ratio
formulas.formula: Profit ratio = C₁ : C₂ : C₃  (when all invest for equal time)
formulas.breakdown: Time is common, so it cancels and only the amounts matter.

formulas.label: Different time → capital × time
formulas.breakdown: Money working longer deserves more, so weight each capital by its number of months.

formulas.label: One partner's share
formulas.formula: Share = (your product ÷ sum of all products) × Total profit
formulas.breakdown: Convert the ratio into an actual amount by taking your fraction of the total profit.

formulas.label: Working partner (salary first)
formulas.formula: Remaining = Total − salary/commission ; split the remaining by capital × time
formulas.breakdown: The active partner is paid for effort first; only the leftover is shared by investment.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Simple partnership (same time)
questionTypes.idea: All invest for the same period. What makes it different: the profit ratio is just the capital ratio.
questionTypes.problem: A and B invest ₹8000 and ₹12000 for the same period. If the total profit is ₹5000, find each share.

steps.why: Equal time cancels; only the capitals decide the split.
Give each their fraction of the profit.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Compound partnership (different time)
questionTypes.idea: Partners invest for different durations. What makes it different: weight each capital by its months.
questionTypes.problem: A invests ₹6000 for 12 months, B invests ₹9000 for 8 months. Find the profit-sharing ratio.

steps.why: Weight each capital by its months of work.
The products are equal, so they share equally.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — A partner joins late
questionTypes.idea: One partner starts later. What makes it different: count only the months each was actually invested.
questionTypes.problem: A starts a business with ₹10000. After 4 months B joins with ₹12000. At the end of the year, find the profit-sharing ratio.

steps.action: A's time = 12 months; B joined 4 months in, so B's time = 8 months.
steps.why: Count each partner's actual months of investment.
Take the ratio of capital × time.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Investment changes mid-year
questionTypes.idea: A partner adds or withdraws capital during the year. What makes it different: split the year at the change point.
questionTypes.problem: A invests ₹8000. After 6 months he adds ₹4000 more. Find A's total capital-months for the year.

steps.why: Split the year at the point the capital changed.
Add the two periods.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Working partner salary
questionTypes.idea: A partner also manages the business for a salary. What makes it different: remove the salary before splitting.
questionTypes.problem: A and B invest equally. A also manages the business for a salary totalling ₹6000 a year. If the annual profit is ₹30000, find B's share.

steps.action: Deduct A's salary first: 30000 − 6000 = 24000 to be shared by capital.
steps.why: The working partner is paid for effort before the split.
Only the remaining profit is divided by investment.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Find a capital or total from a share
questionTypes.idea: Gives one share and the ratio, asks the total (or a capital). What makes it different: work backward through one part's value.
questionTypes.problem: A and B share profit in the ratio 3:2. If A's share is ₹4500, find the total profit.

steps.why: Divide the share by A's number of parts.
Add all parts.

commonMistakes: Splitting profit by capital only when the times differ. Use capital × time unless every partner invested for the same period.
Counting a late-joining partner for the full year. Count only the months they were actually invested.
Forgetting to deduct the working partner's salary or commission before dividing the rest.
Mixing time units — convert everything to the same unit (usually months) before multiplying.
When capital changes mid-year, using the final capital for the whole year instead of splitting into periods.
Reading the profit ratio backwards when converting a share back to a capital or total.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Contribution = capital × time. Share = your product ÷ total products × profit. The working partner takes salary first, then split the rest. A late joiner counts only the months present.

crackIt.oneLine: Everyone's share is proportional to capital × time — compute that product for each partner and split the profit in that ratio (after removing any working-partner salary).

shortcuts.name: Capital × time ratio
shortcuts.method: For each partner multiply money by months; that ratio is the split.
shortcuts.whenFails: Forgetting to remove a salary first.

shortcuts.name: Same-time shortcut
shortcuts.method: If times are equal, just use the capital ratio.

shortcuts.name: Split-the-year for changes
shortcuts.method: Break the year at each capital change; sum capital × months per period.

shortcuts.name: Salary off the top
shortcuts.method: Subtract salary/commission, then split the remainder by capital × time.

shortcuts.name: Share → part value
shortcuts.method: Share ÷ your parts = one part; × total parts = total profit.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: one part links share and total.

shortcuts.name: Simplify products early
shortcuts.method: Cancel common factors in the products before splitting.

patternGuide.signal: 'invest for the same time' / no months mentioned
'joins after k months' / 'leaves after k months'
given one share, asked total (or vice versa)

drill.question: A joins 12 mo, B 6 mo, equal capital. Ratio?
8000 for 6 mo then 10000 for 6 mo → C×months?

topics.description: Mix two or more things and find ratios using the alligation rule.

learnIt.realLife: A shopkeeper mixes cheap ₹40/kg rice with better ₹60/kg rice to sell a blend at ₹45/kg — customers get a good price and he still profits. Deciding how much of each to mix so the blend hits the target price is exactly mixture and alligation: a fast see-saw rule for combining things of two different values.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A mixture is made by combining two (or more) things of different values — prices, purities, concentrations. Alligation is a quick rule that tells you in what RATIO to mix a cheaper and a dearer item so the blend has a chosen average ('mean') value. It behaves like a see-saw: the mixing ratio is inversely related to how far each item's value is from the mean.
learnIt.mathIntro: The alligation rule says the quantity of the cheaper to the dearer equals (dearer − mean) : (mean − cheaper). The mean value of any mixture is its total value ÷ total quantity. For repeated replacement — removing some liquid and topping up with water again and again — the remaining pure liquid follows P(1 − x/P)ⁿ.

formulas.label: Alligation rule (the see-saw)
formulas.formula: Cheaper qty : Dearer qty = (Dearer − Mean) : (Mean − Cheaper)
formulas.breakdown: Each item's distance from the mean becomes the OTHER item's share, so the item closer to the mean is needed in greater quantity.

formulas.label: Mean value of a mixture
formulas.breakdown: The average value is everything's worth added up, divided by how much there is.

formulas.label: Repeated replacement
formulas.formula: Pure left = P × (1 − x/P)ⁿ   (remove x from P, refill, n times)
formulas.breakdown: Each operation keeps the same fraction (1 − x/P) of the pure liquid, so after n rounds you multiply that fraction n times.
formulas.example: From 40 L remove 4 L and refill, thrice: 40×(36/40)³ = 29.16 L.

formulas.label: Concentration / fraction
formulas.formula: Fraction of an ingredient = its quantity ÷ total quantity
formulas.breakdown: The share of milk (or any part) is that part over the whole mixture.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Alligation ratio
questionTypes.idea: Asks in what ratio to mix two items to hit a target price. What makes it different: use the see-saw of distances from the mean.
questionTypes.problem: In what ratio must rice at ₹30/kg be mixed with rice at ₹40/kg to get a blend worth ₹36/kg?

steps.why: Each item's distance from the mean becomes the other's quantity.
Simplify the ratio.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Mean value of a mixture
questionTypes.idea: Gives quantities and prices, asks the blend price. What makes it different: it is a weighted average, not a plain one.
questionTypes.problem: 5 kg of tea at ₹200/kg is mixed with 3 kg at ₹280/kg. Find the price of the mixture per kg.

steps.why: Add up the worth of each part.
Divide by the total quantity (8 kg).

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Milk & water (add water)
questionTypes.idea: Water is added to change a ratio. What makes it different: the milk quantity stays fixed.
questionTypes.problem: A 40-litre mixture has milk and water in the ratio 3:1. How much water must be added to make the ratio 3:2?

steps.why: Split by the current ratio.

steps.action: Milk stays 30. For 3:2, water must be 20, so add 20 − 10 = 10 L.
steps.why: Only water is added, so keep milk fixed and solve for the new water.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Repeated replacement
questionTypes.idea: Some liquid is removed and replaced with water, repeatedly. What makes it different: multiply the pure part by a fraction each round.
questionTypes.problem: A vessel has 20 litres of pure milk. 4 litres are removed and replaced with water; this is done twice. Find the milk left.

steps.why: Each removal takes 1/5 of whatever milk is present.
Apply the fraction n = 2 times.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Combine two mixtures
questionTypes.idea: Two mixtures with known ratios are poured together. What makes it different: add the ingredient fractions (weighted by volume).
questionTypes.problem: Vessel A has milk:water = 4:1, vessel B has 2:3. Equal quantities from each are mixed. Find the milk:water ratio in the result.

steps.action: Milk fraction: A = 4/5, B = 2/5. Equal volumes, so combined milk = 4/5 + 2/5 = 6/5 out of 2 volumes.
steps.why: Add the milk fractions from equal volumes.
Express over the total of 2 volumes.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Profit by mixing
questionTypes.idea: A blend is sold at a price, asks the profit. What makes it different: find the blend's average cost first.
questionTypes.problem: A trader mixes 30 kg of rice at ₹25/kg with 20 kg at ₹30/kg and sells the mixture at ₹30/kg. Find his profit percent.

steps.action: Cost = 30×25 + 20×30 = 750 + 600 = 1350 for 50 kg; cost of the blend = ₹27/kg.
steps.why: Find the true average cost of the mixture.
Profit is on the cost of the blend.

commonMistakes: Reading the alligation ratio backwards. An item's quantity is proportional to the OTHER item's distance from the mean, so the cheaper's share uses (dearer − mean).
Using simple subtraction for repeated replacement. After n replacements the pure part is P(1 − x/P)ⁿ, not P − nx.
Adding water but also changing the milk amount. When only water is added, the milk quantity stays fixed.
Averaging two prices directly instead of weighting by quantity. Mean = total value ÷ total quantity.
When combining mixtures of unequal volumes, forgetting to weight the fractions by the volumes taken.
Confusing the mean (target) with the cost when finding profit — the blend's cost is the weighted average of the ingredient costs.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Alligation is a see-saw: cheaper:dearer = (dearer − mean):(mean − cheaper). Mean = total value ÷ total quantity. Repeated replacement: multiply by (1 − x/P) once per round. Adding only water keeps the milk fixed.

crackIt.oneLine: Alligation is a see-saw — cheaper : dearer = (dearer − mean) : (mean − cheaper) — and repeated replacement just multiplies the pure part by (1 − x/P) once per round.

shortcuts.name: See-saw alligation
shortcuts.method: Each quantity ∝ the other item's gap from the mean.
shortcuts.whenFails: Three or more items at once.

shortcuts.name: Weighted mean
shortcuts.method: Mean = total value ÷ total quantity.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: sum of value / sum of quantity.

shortcuts.name: (1 − x/P)ⁿ replacement
shortcuts.method: Pure left = P(1 − x/P)ⁿ.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: x removed, P total, n rounds.

shortcuts.name: Keep-milk-fixed
shortcuts.method: When only water is added, milk stays; solve new water from the target ratio.

shortcuts.name: Fraction-add for equal mixes
shortcuts.method: Equal volumes: add the ingredient fractions, divide by the number of vessels.

shortcuts.name: Cost-mean then profit
shortcuts.method: Blend cost = weighted mean; then profit% on that cost.

patternGuide.signal: 'in what ratio to mix' at a target price
'quantities and prices given, find mixture price'

drill.question: 16 L milk, remove 4 & refill once → milk?
Milk:water 3:2 in 15 L, make 3:4 → add water?
10 L pure, remove 2 & refill twice → pure?

groups.description: Find averages (including when values are added, removed or replaced) and crack age puzzles.

topics.description: Find the mean and handle problems where values are added, removed or replaced (including weighted average).

videos.label: Averages — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: Concept from scratch + replacement shortcut

videos.label: Averages — concept + variant questions (CAT level)
videos.note: Deviation & weighted-average methods, harder variants

learnIt.realLife: Your five test scores are 60, 75, 80, 65 and 70. To describe how you did with a single number, you add them and divide by five — that is the average, 70. An average is one fair 'middle' number that stands in for a whole set, and aptitude tests love twisting it: what happens when a new score is added, or one is replaced?
learnIt.plainMeaning: The average (or mean) is the total of all the values divided by how many there are. It is the single number that, if every value were the same, would give the same total. The one working idea behind exam problems: total = average × count. Once you can jump between the total and the average freely, most average problems become easy.
learnIt.mathIntro: Average = sum ÷ number of values, so sum = average × number. When a value is added or removed, work with the TOTAL: find the old total, adjust it, and divide by the new count. For a replacement, the total changes by (number of members) × (change in average), which pins down the value that came in or went out.

formulas.label: Average and total
formulas.formula: Average = Sum ÷ n ;  Sum = Average × n
formulas.breakdown: The average shares the total equally among all members; multiplying back recovers the total.

formulas.label: Average of consecutive numbers
formulas.breakdown: Evenly spaced numbers balance around their centre, so the average is just the midpoint.

formulas.label: Adding, removing or replacing a member
formulas.formula: New member's value = Old member's value + n × (change in average)
formulas.breakdown: Because the total moves by n times the change in average, the incoming member differs from the outgoing one by exactly that amount.
formulas.example: 10 people, average up by 2 when one is replaced → new is 20 more.

formulas.label: Weighted average
formulas.breakdown: Bigger groups pull the overall average toward their own value, so weight each average by its group size.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Basic average / missing value
questionTypes.idea: Gives some values and the average, asks a missing one (or the total). What makes it different: it is a direct total = avg × n calculation.
questionTypes.problem: The average of five numbers is 27. If four of them are 20, 25, 30 and 32, find the fifth.

steps.why: Total = average × count.

steps.action: Known four sum to 107, so fifth = 135 − 107 = 28.
steps.why: Subtract the known part from the total.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — A member joins or leaves
questionTypes.idea: Someone is added or removed and the average shifts. What makes it different: the new total minus the old total is that member's value.
questionTypes.problem: The average age of 10 students is 15 years. When the teacher's age is included, the average becomes 16. Find the teacher's age.

steps.why: Recover the total from the average.

steps.action: With the teacher, 11 people average 16 → total 176; teacher = 176 − 150 = 26.
steps.why: The new total minus the old total is the teacher's age.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Replacement
questionTypes.idea: One member is swapped for another and the average changes. What makes it different: only the total moves, by n × change in average.
questionTypes.problem: The average weight of 8 people increases by 2.5 kg when a new person replaces one weighing 65 kg. Find the new person's weight.

steps.why: The whole total rises by count × change in average.
The incoming person is heavier than the one leaving by that increase.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Consecutive numbers
questionTypes.idea: Asks the average of a run of evenly spaced numbers. What makes it different: the answer is just the midpoint.
questionTypes.problem: Find the average of the first 10 natural numbers.

steps.why: Consecutive numbers balance around their centre.
Midpoint of the first and last.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Weighted average
questionTypes.idea: Two groups of different sizes, each with its own average. What makes it different: weight each average by its group size.
questionTypes.problem: In a class, 20 boys average 62 marks and 30 girls average 72. Find the class average.

steps.why: Weight each group's average by its size.
Divide by the total number of students.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Correcting a wrong value
questionTypes.idea: An average was computed with a misread value. What makes it different: fix the total by the reading error, then re-divide.
questionTypes.problem: The average of 10 numbers was found to be 45, but one number was read as 26 instead of the correct 62. Find the correct average.

steps.why: The mistake reduced the total by 36.
Fix the total, then re-divide.

commonMistakes: Averaging the averages of unequal groups. 20 boys at 62 and 30 girls at 72 do NOT average (62+72)/2 = 67 — the bigger group pulls it to 68.
Adding a new member but dividing by the old count. When one joins, the count goes up by one.
In replacement problems, forgetting that only the total changes by n × Δaverage, not each value.
For consecutive numbers, adding them all up when (first+last)/2 is instant.
Mixing up increase and decrease of the total when correcting a misread value.
Assuming the average always lies exactly between the smallest and largest — that is only true for evenly spaced sets.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Total = average × count — jump between them freely. Consecutive numbers: average = (first + last)/2. Add/remove/replace: the total shifts by count × (change in average). A weighted average leans toward the bigger group.

crackIt.oneLine: Every average problem is really about the TOTAL: total = average × count, so find the total, adjust it for what changed, and divide by the new count.

shortcuts.name: Total = avg × n
shortcuts.method: Convert the average to a total, adjust, and re-divide.

shortcuts.name: (first + last)/2
shortcuts.method: Average of an evenly spaced set = its midpoint.

shortcuts.name: n × Δavg for a change
shortcuts.method: Total change = count × change in average → the joining/leaving value.
shortcuts.whenFails: Using the wrong count (before vs after).

shortcuts.name: Weighted = Σ(n·a)/Σn
shortcuts.method: Weight each average by its group size.

shortcuts.name: Correction = fix the total
shortcuts.method: Add the reading error to the total and re-divide.
shortcuts.whenFails: Getting the sign of the error wrong.

shortcuts.name: Deviation method
shortcuts.method: Pick a base, average the deviations, add back.

patternGuide.use: work with totals, adjust the count.

drill.question: Average of the first 5 odd numbers?
Avg of 10 is 30; one added, avg 32 → new value?
5 numbers avg 12; one read 8 not 18 → correct avg?
8 people avg up 1 when one of 40 replaced → new?

topics.description: Solve present, past and future age puzzles using simple equations.

learnIt.realLife: Your dad says, 'When you were born I was 30, so I will always be 30 years older than you.' That one fact — the gap between two people's ages never changes — is the heart of every age puzzle. Ages problems dress up simple 'find the number' equations in words about the past and the future.
learnIt.plainMeaning: An age problem gives clues about how two or more people's ages relate now, and how they related (or will relate) some years ago or later. You translate each clue into a small equation. The most useful truth is that the difference between two people's ages stays the same forever — only their ratio changes with time.
learnIt.mathIntro: Let an unknown present age be a letter (or write ages in a given ratio as 5k, 7k). For 'k years ago' subtract k from each present age; for 'k years hence' add k. Then turn the worded condition into an equation and solve. Always answer for the present age unless the question asks otherwise.

formulas.label: Past and future ages
formulas.formula: k years ago: (age − k) ;  k years hence: (age + k)
formulas.breakdown: Everyone ages at the same rate, so shift every person's age by the same k.

formulas.label: Constant age difference
formulas.formula: (A − B) is the same at every point in time
formulas.breakdown: Since both grow by the same amount each year, the gap between two ages never changes.

formulas.label: Ratio ages as multiples
formulas.formula: Ages in ratio a:b → write them as ak and bk, then apply the time clue
formulas.breakdown: The common multiplier k turns a ratio into workable numbers with a single unknown.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Simple present age
questionTypes.idea: A multiple relation now plus a future/past clue. What makes it different: one unknown captures both ages.
questionTypes.problem: A father is 4 times as old as his son. In 20 years he will be twice as old. Find their present ages.

steps.action: Let the son be x; father = 4x.
steps.why: Express both ages in one unknown.
Translate the future condition and solve.
Substitute back.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Ratio of ages with a future clue
questionTypes.idea: Ratio now and a different ratio after some years. What makes it different: write ages as ak, bk and add the years.
questionTypes.problem: The ages of A and B are in the ratio 5:7. After 6 years the ratio becomes 3:4. Find their present ages.

steps.why: Turn the ratio into multiples of k.
Cross-multiply the future ratio.
Substitute k = 6.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Ratio of ages with a past clue
questionTypes.idea: Ratio now and a ratio some years ago. What makes it different: subtract the years from each before cross-multiplying.
questionTypes.problem: The present ratio of ages of A and B is 4:5. Five years ago it was 3:4. Find B's present age.

steps.action: Let the present ages be 4k and 5k.
steps.why: Ratio to multiples.
Subtract 5 from each and cross-multiply.
Substitute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Sum or difference of ages
questionTypes.idea: The sum (or difference) is given plus a time clue. What makes it different: use the sum to write both ages with one unknown.
questionTypes.problem: The sum of the ages of a mother and daughter is 50 years. Five years ago the mother was 7 times as old as the daughter. Find their present ages.

steps.action: Let daughter now = x, mother = 50 − x.
steps.why: Use the sum to write both with one unknown.
Translate the past condition.
Substitute.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — One age in terms of another
questionTypes.idea: One person's age is described using another's past/future age. What makes it different: chain two time references.
questionTypes.problem: A is as old as B was 10 years ago. In 5 years A will be 25. Find B's present age.

steps.action: In 5 years A is 25, so A now = 20.
steps.why: Subtract 5 to get A's present age.

steps.action: A now equals B's age 10 years ago: B − 10 = 20 → B = 30.
steps.why: Translate the comparison.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Average / family ages
questionTypes.idea: An average of several ages, sometimes across time. What makes it different: total = average × count links the ages.
questionTypes.problem: The average age of a family of 4 is 25 years. If the two children are 10 and 12, and the parents are of equal age, find each parent's age.

steps.why: Total = average × count.

steps.action: Children sum to 22, so parents sum to 78; equal → each 39.
steps.why: Split the parents' total equally.

commonMistakes: Applying a time shift to only one person. If it is '5 years ago', subtract 5 from EVERY person's age.
Using the ratio numbers as actual ages. 5:7 does not mean 5 and 7 years — introduce k and solve.
Forgetting the age difference is constant, and re-deriving it each time.
Answering the past/future age when the present age is asked (or vice versa).
Setting up 'twice as old' backwards — read carefully who is older.
Mixing 'ago' and 'hence': ago subtracts, hence adds.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Present age = a letter; 'ago' subtracts and 'hence' adds — to everyone equally. Ratios become ak, bk. The age gap never changes. Solve for what is asked (usually the present age).

crackIt.oneLine: Turn each age clue into a small equation: name the present age, subtract for 'ago' and add for 'hence' (to everyone), and write ratios as ak, bk.

shortcuts.name: One-letter setup
shortcuts.method: Let one present age be x, others in terms of x; solve one equation.

shortcuts.name: Ratio → ak, bk
shortcuts.method: Write ratio ages as ak and bk, then apply the time clue.
shortcuts.whenFails: Forgetting to shift both by the time.

shortcuts.name: Constant difference
shortcuts.method: Use that A − B never changes to link two times.
shortcuts.whenFails: Comparing ratios (the gap alone is not enough).

shortcuts.name: Shift everyone
shortcuts.method: For 'k years ago/hence', change every age by k.

shortcuts.name: Sum setup
shortcuts.method: If the sum is given, write x and (S − x).
shortcuts.whenFails: Three people with a single sum.

shortcuts.name: Answer-check by plugging
shortcuts.method: Put the found ages back into every clue.

patternGuide.signal: 'X times as old ... in/after k years'

patternGuide.use: ak, bk with the added years.
ak, bk with the subtracted years.

patternGuide.signal: 'as old as B was k years ago'

drill.question: A is 25, B is 5 years younger → B?
In 5 years A is 30 → A now?
Sum of ages 60, one is 40 → other?
Gap between two ages is 8; younger 12 → older?

groups.description: Simple and compound interest, plus depreciation and growth-rate problems.

topics.description: Calculate interest that stays flat on the original principal.

learnIt.realLife: You lend a friend ₹1000 and agree he pays you ₹100 extra each year until he returns it. That ₹100 a year is 'interest' — a fee for using your money — and because it is always figured on the original ₹1000, it is called SIMPLE interest. Banks, chit funds and loans all run on this idea.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Interest is the rent paid for borrowing money. Simple interest is the easiest kind: the interest each year is a fixed percentage of the ORIGINAL amount borrowed (the principal), and it never changes no matter how long the loan runs. So the total interest grows in equal yearly steps.
learnIt.mathIntro: Simple interest = principal × rate × time ÷ 100, where the rate is per year (a percent) and time is in years. The amount you finally repay is the principal plus this interest. Rearranging the same formula lets you find any one of principal, rate or time when the other three are known.

formulas.label: Simple interest
formulas.breakdown: P is the sum borrowed, R the yearly rate percent, T the years; the same slice of P is charged each year, then added up.

formulas.label: Amount repaid
formulas.breakdown: You return the original sum plus all the interest it earned.

formulas.label: Finding P, R or T
formulas.breakdown: The one formula rearranges to isolate whichever quantity is unknown.

formulas.label: Doubling / tripling at SI
formulas.formula: Doubles in n years → R = 100/n ;  triples → R = 200/n
formulas.breakdown: To double, the interest must equal the principal (100% of it) over n years; to triple, it must equal twice the principal.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Find the simple interest
questionTypes.idea: Gives P, R and T, asks the interest. What makes it different: a direct formula plug-in.
questionTypes.problem: Find the simple interest on ₹5000 at 8% per annum for 3 years.

steps.why: Apply the formula with P = 5000, R = 8, T = 3.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Find the amount
questionTypes.idea: Asks the total repaid. What makes it different: add the principal to the interest.
questionTypes.problem: What amount is repaid on ₹4000 at 6% per annum simple interest after 5 years?

steps.why: First find the interest.
Add the principal and interest.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Find rate, time or principal
questionTypes.idea: Three of the four are known. What makes it different: rearrange the formula for the missing one.
questionTypes.problem: At what rate will ₹2500 give ₹600 as simple interest in 4 years?

steps.why: Rearrange the formula to isolate R.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Doubling / tripling
questionTypes.idea: A sum grows to a multiple of itself. What makes it different: the interest equals a whole multiple of the principal.
questionTypes.problem: In how many years will a sum double itself at 12.5% per annum simple interest?

steps.action: To double, the interest must equal the principal, i.e. 100%.
steps.why: The amount goes from P to 2P, so SI = P.
Time to earn 100% at 12.5% per year.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Amounts at two different times
questionTypes.idea: Gives the amount after two different periods. What makes it different: the yearly interest is the gap between the two amounts.
questionTypes.problem: A sum amounts to ₹2600 in 3 years and ₹3000 in 5 years at simple interest. Find the sum.

steps.action: Interest for the 2 extra years = 3000 − 2600 = 400, so 200 per year.
steps.why: The extra 2 years added 400 of interest, and SI is equal each year.
Subtract 3 years' interest from the 3-year amount.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Becomes a fraction of itself
questionTypes.idea: A sum grows to k times itself. What makes it different: the principal cancels out.
questionTypes.problem: A sum becomes 7/6 of itself in 2 years at simple interest. Find the rate.

steps.action: Interest = 7/6 P − P = P/6, over 2 years.
steps.why: The growth beyond the principal is the interest.
Cancel P and compute.

commonMistakes: Charging interest on interest. Simple interest is always on the original principal, never on the growing amount (that is compound interest).
Forgetting to add the principal when the AMOUNT is asked. Amount = principal + interest.
Using time in months directly as years. Convert months to years (9 months = 9/12 = 0.75 year).
For doubling, using R = 100/n but forgetting it means SI = 100% of P (tripling is 200%).
Treating the rate as a decimal and dividing by 100 again — R in the formula is the percent number (8, not 0.08).
In amount-at-two-times problems, treating the amounts as principals instead of first finding the yearly interest.
learnIt.memoryTrick: SI = PRT/100 — always on the ORIGINAL principal. Amount = P + SI. To double at SI, time = 100/rate; to triple, 200/rate. Convert months to years before using T.

crackIt.oneLine: Simple interest is one flat formula on the original principal: SI = PRT/100, and the amount you repay is just P + SI.

shortcuts.name: PRT/100
shortcuts.method: Plug P, R (percent) and T (years) straight in.

shortcuts.name: Rearrange for the unknown
shortcuts.method: P = 100SI/RT, R = 100SI/PT, T = 100SI/PR.

shortcuts.name: Double = 100/R, triple = 200/R
shortcuts.method: Time (or rate) for a multiple of the sum.

shortcuts.name: Per-year interest gap
shortcuts.method: From two amounts, one year's interest = (A₂ − A₁)/(years apart).

shortcuts.name: Becomes-k-times rate
shortcuts.method: k times → SI = (k−1)P; R = 100(k−1)/T.

shortcuts.name: Months → years
shortcuts.method: T = months/12 before plugging in.

patternGuide.signal: 'at what rate / in how many years / what sum'
'amounts to A₁ in t₁ and A₂ in t₂'

topics.description: Interest that grows on interest — yearly, half-yearly and quarterly.

learnIt.realLife: You put ₹1000 in a savings account at 10% a year. After year 1 you have ₹1100 — and in year 2 the bank pays 10% on ₹1100, not on ₹1000, so you earn a little extra. That 'interest on interest' is compound interest, and over years it snowballs — it is how savings, EMIs and investments really grow.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Compound interest is interest that itself earns interest. Unlike simple interest (always on the original sum), each period's interest is added to the balance, and the next period's interest is calculated on this bigger balance. So the money grows by a fixed FACTOR each period rather than a fixed amount — slowly at first, then faster.
learnIt.mathIntro: The amount after n years is A = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ, and the compound interest is A − P. If interest is added more often than yearly, split the rate and multiply the periods: half-yearly uses rate R/2 over 2n periods, quarterly uses R/4 over 4n. A handy exam fact: for the same P, R and 2 years, CI beats SI by exactly P(R/100)².

formulas.label: Compound amount
formulas.formula: A = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ ;  CI = A − P
formulas.breakdown: Each year multiplies the balance by (1 + R/100), so n years multiply it n times; the interest is the extra over the principal.

formulas.label: Non-annual compounding
formulas.formula: Half-yearly: rate R/2, periods 2n ;  Quarterly: rate R/4, periods 4n
formulas.breakdown: Compounding more often splits the yearly rate but applies it more times, giving slightly more interest.

formulas.label: Difference of CI and SI
formulas.formula: 2 years: CI − SI = P(R/100)² ;  3 years: P(R/100)²(3 + R/100)
formulas.breakdown: The gap is the interest earned on the earlier interest, which you can compute directly.

formulas.label: Different rate each year
formulas.breakdown: Just multiply a separate growth factor for each year's rate.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Find CI or amount (annual)
questionTypes.idea: Standard yearly compounding. What makes it different: multiply by the factor once per year.
questionTypes.problem: Find the compound interest on ₹8000 at 10% per annum for 2 years.

steps.why: Multiply by the yearly factor 1.1 twice.
Interest is the amount minus the principal.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Half-yearly / quarterly
questionTypes.idea: Interest added more than once a year. What makes it different: split the rate and multiply the periods.
questionTypes.problem: Find the amount on ₹4000 at 10% per annum compounded half-yearly for 1 year.

steps.why: Split the 10% into two 5% periods.
Apply the factor 1.05 twice.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Difference between CI and SI
questionTypes.idea: Asks the gap between the two. What makes it different: a one-line formula avoids computing both.
questionTypes.problem: Find the difference between the compound and simple interest on ₹6000 at 10% per annum for 2 years.

steps.why: The difference is the interest on the first year's interest.
Compute directly.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Find rate or time
questionTypes.idea: Gives the amount and asks the rate/time. What makes it different: divide out P, then take a root.
questionTypes.problem: A sum of ₹5000 becomes ₹5832 in 2 years at compound interest. Find the rate.

steps.why: Divide the amount by the principal to isolate the factor.
Take the square root for 2 years.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Becomes a multiple (doubling)
questionTypes.idea: A sum becomes k times, asks when it becomes a power of k. What makes it different: multiply the years.
questionTypes.problem: A sum becomes 4 times itself in 4 years at compound interest. In how many years will it become 16 times?

steps.why: Each 'times 4' is one 4-year block.
Two blocks of 4 years.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Different rate each year
questionTypes.idea: The rate changes year to year. What makes it different: multiply a factor per year.
questionTypes.problem: Find the amount on ₹10000 for 2 years if the rate is 10% in the first year and 20% in the second.

steps.why: Use a separate growth factor for each year.
Multiply the factors in turn.

commonMistakes: Using simple-interest thinking for CI. Each year's interest is on the NEW balance, so multiply by (1+R/100), don't add PRT.
For half-yearly, halving the rate but forgetting to double the number of periods (and vice versa).
Confusing CI (A − P) with the amount A. The interest is only the part above the principal.
For the 2-year difference, using P(R/100) instead of P(R/100)² — it is the square.
Rounding the growth factor too early; keep (1 + R/100) exact until the end.
With different yearly rates, adding the rates instead of multiplying the factors.
learnIt.memoryTrick: A = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ; CI = A − P. Half-yearly → rate/2, periods×2; quarterly → rate/4, periods×4. Two-year gap between CI and SI = P(R/100)². Different rates → multiply the factors.

crackIt.oneLine: Compound interest multiplies by a fixed factor each period: A = P(1 + R/100)ⁿ, so build the factor, raise it to the number of periods, and subtract P for the interest.

shortcuts.name: Factor power
shortcuts.whenFails: Odd rates without a known power.

shortcuts.name: Split for half / quarter
shortcuts.method: Half-yearly rate/2 with periods×2; quarterly rate/4 with periods×4.
shortcuts.whenFails: Changing only one of the two.

shortcuts.name: 2-yr gap = P(R/100)²
shortcuts.method: CI − SI for 2 years.

shortcuts.name: Ratio → rate
shortcuts.method: Amount/Principal = factorⁿ; take the n-th root.

shortcuts.name: Times-in-years
shortcuts.method: If it becomes k times in t years, k^m times takes m·t years.

shortcuts.name: Multiply the factors
shortcuts.method: Different yearly rates → product of (1 + Rᵢ/100).

drill.question: CI − SI on 2000 at 5% for 2 yr?

topics.description: Values that fall (depreciation) or rise (growth) year on year — same maths as compound interest.

learnIt.realLife: A new bike worth ₹1,00,000 loses about 10% of its value every year — after a year it is worth ₹90,000, after two about ₹81,000. Meanwhile a town's population might GROW 10% a year. Both follow the exact same compound formula; one just multiplies by less than 1, the other by more.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Depreciation is a value shrinking by a fixed percentage each year (machines, vehicles and gadgets lose worth). Growth is a value rising by a fixed percentage each year (population, production, users). Mathematically both are compound interest: you multiply by (1 − R/100) each year to shrink, or (1 + R/100) to grow.
learnIt.mathIntro: For depreciation, value after n years = P(1 − R/100)ⁿ; for growth, P(1 + R/100)ⁿ. To go backward in time (find an earlier value), divide instead of multiply. When the rate differs year to year, multiply a separate factor for each year.

formulas.label: Depreciation
formulas.breakdown: Each year keeps (1 − R/100) of the value, so after n years you multiply that shrink factor n times.

formulas.label: Growth
formulas.breakdown: Each year multiplies by (1 + R/100), exactly like compound interest.

formulas.label: Going back in time
formulas.breakdown: To undo n years of change, divide by the same factor instead of multiplying.
formulas.example: Population now 24200 at 10% growth → 2 yr ago = 24200/1.21 = 20000.

formulas.label: Different rates per year
formulas.breakdown: Apply each year's own grow/shrink factor in turn.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Depreciation over n years
questionTypes.idea: A value falls a fixed percent each year. What makes it different: multiply by (1 − R/100).
questionTypes.problem: A machine worth ₹50000 depreciates 10% per year. Find its value after 2 years.

steps.why: Keep 90% of the value each year.
Compute the shrink factor and apply.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Growth over n years
questionTypes.idea: A population or output rises a fixed percent each year. What makes it different: multiply by (1 + R/100).
questionTypes.problem: The population of a town is 20000 and grows 10% per year. Find it after 2 years.

steps.why: Grow by 10% each year (compound).
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Find an earlier value
questionTypes.idea: Gives the present value, asks the past. What makes it different: divide by the factor.
questionTypes.problem: A town's population is now 26620 after growing 10% per year for 3 years. Find the population 3 years ago.

steps.why: Divide to undo three years of 10% growth.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Different rates each year
questionTypes.idea: The percent changes year to year. What makes it different: multiply a factor per year.
questionTypes.problem: A car worth ₹8,00,000 loses 20% in the first year and 10% in the second. Find its value after 2 years.

steps.why: Apply each year's own depreciation factor.
Multiply the factors.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Find the rate
questionTypes.idea: Gives start and end values, asks the rate. What makes it different: take the n-th root of the ratio.
questionTypes.problem: A machine bought for ₹10000 is worth ₹8100 after 2 years. Find the annual rate of depreciation.

steps.why: Divide the final by the original to isolate the factor.
Take the square root for 2 years.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Rise then fall (net effect)
questionTypes.idea: A value goes up then down. What makes it different: a rise and equal fall do NOT cancel.
questionTypes.problem: A company's output rises 20% in one year and falls 20% the next. If it started at 5000 units, find the final output.

steps.why: One growth factor, one decline factor.
A rise then equal fall leaves a net drop.

commonMistakes: Using 1 + R/100 for depreciation. A falling value multiplies by 1 − R/100.
Subtracting R% n times (simple) instead of compounding. Depreciation compounds like CI.
To find a past value, multiplying instead of dividing by the factor.
Assuming a 20% rise then 20% fall returns to the start — it gives a 4% net fall.
Mixing the growth and shrink signs when rates differ each year.
Rounding the factor early instead of keeping it exact until the end.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Depreciation multiplies by (1 − R/100) each year; growth by (1 + R/100). Same compound formula. Go back in time by dividing. Different rates → multiply the factors.

crackIt.oneLine: Depreciation and growth are compound interest in disguise: multiply by (1 − R/100) to shrink or (1 + R/100) to grow, once per year.

shortcuts.name: Divide to go back
shortcuts.method: Earlier value = current ÷ factorⁿ.

shortcuts.name: Multiply mixed factors
shortcuts.method: Different yearly rates → product of factors.

shortcuts.name: Root for the rate
shortcuts.method: factorⁿ = V/P; take the n-th root.

shortcuts.name: Rise-then-equal-fall = −(x/10)²%
shortcuts.method: Up x% then down x% → net (x/10)²% fall.

groups.description: How long people, machines and pipes take to finish a job — alone, together, or in turns.

topics.description: Work out how long people or machines take — alone, together or in turns (including man-day problems).

videos.label: Work & Wages — Shortcuts & Tricks (CareerRide)
videos.note: Full lecture — efficiency/LCM method + wage-sharing problems

learnIt.realLife: You and a friend paint a room. Alone you would take 6 hours, your friend 3 hours. Working together the room gets done faster — but how much faster? Not 'somewhere in the middle' — there is an exact answer. Time and work is about combining the speeds of people, machines or pipes to see how quickly a job finishes.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Time and work problems compare how fast different people (or machines) finish the same job. The key idea: if someone finishes a job in n days, then in one day they do 1/n of it — that fraction is their 'one-day work' or efficiency. Add efficiencies when people work together, and the whole job is always counted as 1 (or, more easily, as the LCM of the times).
learnIt.mathIntro: If A alone takes 'a' days, A's one-day work is 1/a. Two people together do 1/a + 1/b per day and finish in ab/(a+b) days. The fastest exam method is the LCM trick: let total work = LCM of the given times so each person's per-day units are whole numbers; then just add or subtract units. Wages are shared in the ratio of work actually done.

formulas.label: One-day work
formulas.formula: If a job takes n days, one day's work = 1/n
formulas.breakdown: Split the whole job (counted as 1) evenly across the days.

formulas.label: Two together
formulas.breakdown: Add one-day works 1/a + 1/b, then invert.

formulas.label: LCM method
formulas.formula: Total work = LCM of times; efficiency = total ÷ own time
formulas.breakdown: Whole-number units make adding and subtracting easy; time = total ÷ combined efficiency.

formulas.label: Man-days
formulas.breakdown: The total effort (people × days × hours) per unit of work stays constant.
formulas.example: 10 men × 6 days = 12 men × D → D = 5 days.

formulas.label: Wages
formulas.formula: Share of pay ∝ work done (efficiency × days worked)
formulas.breakdown: Whoever does more of the job earns proportionally more.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Working together
questionTypes.idea: Two rates given, asks combined time. What makes it different: add one-day works, don't average times.
questionTypes.problem: A can do a job in 12 days and B in 6 days. How long together?

steps.why: Add their one-day works.
Invert the one-day total.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Find the missing person's time
questionTypes.idea: Together time and one person given. What makes it different: subtract rates.
questionTypes.problem: A and B together finish a job in 4 days; A alone takes 12 days. How long does B alone take?

steps.why: Subtract A's share from the combined.
Invert.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Work and wages
questionTypes.idea: Split the pay. What makes it different: money follows work done, not time.
questionTypes.problem: A can do a job in 6 days and B in 12 days. They finish it together and earn ₹900. Find B's share.

steps.why: Wages split in the ratio of work done.
B is 1 part out of 3.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Someone leaves partway
questionTypes.idea: They start together, one leaves. What makes it different: track work done, then divide the rest.
questionTypes.problem: A can do a job in 6 days, B in 12 days. They start together but A leaves after 2 days. In how many more days will B finish?

steps.action: Take total work = 12 units (LCM). A does 2/day, B does 1/day.
steps.why: LCM method for whole numbers.

steps.action: In 2 days together they do (2+1)×2 = 6 units, leaving 12 − 6 = 6.
steps.why: Combined 3 units/day for 2 days.

steps.action: B finishes 6 units at 1/day → 6 more days.
steps.why: Remaining ÷ B's rate.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Alternate days
questionTypes.idea: They take turns day by day. What makes it different: block the cycle, finish the leftover.
questionTypes.problem: A can do a job in 4 days and B in 6 days. They work on alternate days starting with A. In how many days is the job finished?

steps.why: LCM method.

steps.action: A pair of days (A then B) does 3 + 2 = 5 units; two pairs (4 days) do 10 units.
steps.why: Group the alternating days.

steps.action: Remaining 2 units on day 5 (A's turn, 3/day) → 2/3 day. Total = 4⅔ days.
steps.why: Finish the leftover at the next person's rate.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Man-days
questionTypes.idea: More/fewer people or hours. What makes it different: total effort is fixed, so men and days are inverse.
questionTypes.problem: 10 men build a wall in 6 days. How many days will 12 men take to build the same wall?

steps.why: Total effort is fixed for the same job.
More men, fewer days (inverse).

commonMistakes: Averaging the times. If A takes 12 and B takes 6, together is NOT 9 — add one-day works, not times.
Adding times instead of rates. Efficiencies add; times do not.
In wages, splitting equally or by time instead of by work done (efficiency × days).
In alternate-day problems, forgetting whose turn the leftover falls on — the last bit is done at that person's rate.
Man-day problems: treating men and days as directly proportional. More men means FEWER days (inverse).
Forgetting to convert when hours per day change — use M×D×H, not just M×D.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Turn every time into one-day work (1/n) or use the LCM as total work. Add rates for together, subtract to find a missing person. Wages follow work done. Men × days is constant for the same job.

crackIt.oneLine: Set total work = LCM of the times so everyone's daily output is a whole number, then just add and subtract units.

shortcuts.name: LCM = total work
shortcuts.method: Total = LCM of times; efficiency = total/own time.

shortcuts.name: Together = ab/(a+b)
shortcuts.method: Two people, product over sum.

shortcuts.name: Missing = subtract rates
shortcuts.method: B = combined rate − A's rate, then invert.

shortcuts.name: Wages ∝ efficiency
shortcuts.method: Split pay in the ratio of daily work (× days worked).

shortcuts.name: Alternate-day pairing
shortcuts.method: Sum two days' units, count full pairs, finish the leftover at the next rate.

shortcuts.name: Man-days constant
shortcuts.method: M₁D₁H₁ = M₂D₂H₂ for the same job.

patternGuide.use: LCM units, do the done part, divide the rest.
pair the cycle, finish the leftover.

drill.question: Together 6 d, A 10 d, B alone?
A 8 d, B 8 d, wages 800 → A's?
A 6, B 12; A leaves after 2, B finishes rest?
A twice as fast as B, together 8 d, A alone?

topics.description: Filling and emptying tanks — the same idea as time & work.

videos.label: Pipes & Cisterns — basics to advanced (Sahil Sir)
videos.note: LCM method, inlet/outlet & leak problems with shortcuts

learnIt.realLife: A tank in your building has a tap filling it and a drain emptying it. If both are open, whether the tank fills or empties — and how fast — depends on which is stronger. Pipes and cisterns is exactly time-and-work, but with some pipes ADDING water and some REMOVING it.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A pipe that fills a tank in n hours does 1/n of the tank each hour — just like a worker. Inlet pipes add water (positive rate); outlet pipes and leaks remove it (negative rate). To find the net effect, add the fill rates and subtract the empty rates; the leftover rate tells you how fast the tank fills (or empties).
learnIt.mathIntro: If a pipe fills a tank in 'a' hours, its rate is +1/a per hour; an outlet emptying in 'b' hours is −1/b. Add the rates of everything that is open: a positive total fills the tank (time = 1 ÷ net rate), a negative total empties it. The LCM trick works here too — set the capacity to the LCM so every rate is a whole number.

formulas.label: Fill / empty rate
formulas.formula: Fills in n hours → +1/n per hour; empties in n → −1/n
formulas.breakdown: Same as one-day work, with a sign for direction.

formulas.label: Net rate
formulas.formula: Net = (sum of inlet rates) − (sum of outlet rates); time = 1 ÷ net
formulas.breakdown: Combine everything open; positive fills, negative empties.

formulas.label: Leak formula
formulas.formula: Fills in a alone, a+leak in b → leak empties a full tank in ab/(b−a)
formulas.breakdown: The extra time the leak adds tells its emptying speed.

formulas.label: LCM capacity
formulas.formula: Capacity = LCM of times; rate = capacity ÷ own time
formulas.breakdown: Whole-number litres per hour make adding easy.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Two inlet pipes
questionTypes.idea: Both fill. What makes it different: add the fill rates.
questionTypes.problem: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours and pipe B in 12 hours. How long to fill together?

steps.why: Add the fill rates.
Invert the net rate.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Inlet plus outlet
questionTypes.idea: One fills, one empties. What makes it different: subtract the outlet.
questionTypes.problem: An inlet fills a tank in 4 hours; an outlet empties it in 12 hours. If both are open, how long to fill?

steps.why: Subtract the outlet from the inlet.
Invert the positive net rate.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Leak slows filling
questionTypes.idea: A leak adds time. What makes it different: use ab/(b−a) to find the leak's speed.
questionTypes.problem: A pipe fills a tank in 6 hours, but due to a leak it takes 8 hours. How long will the leak alone take to empty a full tank?

steps.why: The leak added 2 hours to the fill time.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Pipes opened by turns
questionTypes.idea: Alternate hours. What makes it different: block the cycle and finish the leftover.
questionTypes.problem: Pipe A fills a tank in 4 hours and pipe B in 6 hours. They are opened alternately for one hour each, starting with A. When is the tank full?

steps.why: LCM method.

steps.action: Each 2-hour cycle fills 3 + 2 = 5. After 2 cycles (4 hr): 10 units.
steps.why: Group the alternating hours.

steps.action: Remaining 2 units in hour 5 (A, 3/hr) → 2/3 hr. Total 4⅔ hours.
steps.why: Finish the leftover at A's rate.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — A pipe closed partway
questionTypes.idea: One pipe shuts off. What makes it different: track units filled, then divide the rest.
questionTypes.problem: Two pipes fill a tank in 20 and 30 minutes respectively. Both are opened together, but the first is closed after 10 minutes. How long in total to fill the tank?

steps.why: LCM method.
Both open for 10 minutes.

steps.action: Second pipe fills 10 at 2/min → 5 more min. Total 15 min.
steps.why: Finish with the remaining pipe.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Three pipes (inlets and outlet)
questionTypes.idea: Mix of fillers and drainer. What makes it different: add inlets, subtract the outlet.
questionTypes.problem: Two inlets fill a tank in 12 and 15 hours; an outlet empties it in 20 hours. If all three are open, how long to fill?

steps.why: Add inlets, subtract the outlet.
Combine over 60.
Invert.
