commonMistakes: Adding the outlet instead of subtracting. Outlets and leaks REMOVE water — use a minus sign.
Assuming the tank fills when the outlet is faster. If the net rate is negative, the tank empties (or never fills).
In leak problems, mixing up ab/(b−a) — b is the slower (with-leak) time.
Ignoring water already in the tank when a pipe is closed partway — track units filled so far.
Mixing minutes and hours between pipes — keep one unit throughout.
For alternate pipes, forgetting whose hour the leftover falls in.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Inlet = +1/time, outlet/leak = −1/time. Add them for the net rate; time = 1 ÷ net. Leak that adds time: ab/(b−a). Use the LCM as capacity for whole-number rates.

crackIt.oneLine: Pipes are just workers with a sign — inlets add, outlets subtract — so set capacity = LCM and add the signed rates.

shortcuts.name: LCM capacity
shortcuts.method: Capacity = LCM of times; rate = cap/time, minus for outlets.

shortcuts.name: Net = inlets − outlets
shortcuts.method: Add signed rates, time = 1/net.

shortcuts.name: Leak = ab/(b−a)
shortcuts.method: Fills in a, with leak in b.

shortcuts.name: Both = ab/(a+b)
shortcuts.method: Two inlets, product over sum.

shortcuts.name: Track filled units
shortcuts.method: Units done before a pipe closes = rate × time; divide the rest.

shortcuts.name: Alternate-hour cycle
shortcuts.method: Sum a cycle's units, count cycles, finish the leftover.

patternGuide.signal: 'because of a leak it takes longer'

drill.question: Fills in 10, with leak 15 → leak empties?
Inlet 5 hr, outlet 5 hr both open?

groups.description: Everything motion — travel sums, trains, boats, races and relative speed.

topics.description: Link speed, distance and time to solve travel, average-speed and unit-conversion problems.

videos.label: Time, Speed & Distance — full placement session
videos.note: PrepInsta — concept plus trains and boats

videos.label: Speed, Time & Distance — Basic to Advanced
videos.note: Basics to advanced solved questions

learnIt.realLife: You walk to school every day. Some days you walk fast and reach in 10 minutes; some days you stroll and it takes 20 minutes. The road never changed — only your speed and your time did. That single everyday fact is this whole topic: how fast you go and how long you take together decide how far you reach.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Speed just means how much distance you cover in one unit of time — like 'in one hour'. If a car covers 60 kilometres in 1 hour, its speed is 60 kilometres per hour (written 60 km/h). Distance is the total length you travelled. Time is how long it took. These three are joined together: if you know any two of them, you can always find the third.
learnIt.mathIntro: One simple relationship connects all three: distance equals speed multiplied by time. From this one idea we can rearrange the letters to find speed or time whenever we need to.

formulas.label: Distance
formulas.breakdown: Distance (how far you go) equals Speed (how far you cover in one hour) times Time (how many hours you travel). Going 50 km every hour for 3 hours gives 50 × 3 = 150 km.

formulas.label: Speed
formulas.breakdown: Speed equals the total Distance divided by the Time taken. Covering 150 km in 3 hours means each hour you did 150 ÷ 3 = 50, so your speed is 50 km/h.

formulas.label: Time
formulas.breakdown: Time equals the total Distance divided by the Speed. To cover 150 km at 50 km/h you need 150 ÷ 50 = 3 hours.

formulas.label: Changing units (km/h and m/s)
formulas.breakdown: When a question mixes kilometres-per-hour with metres-per-second, put them in the same unit first. To turn km/h into m/s multiply by 5/18; to go back, multiply by 18/5. So 36 km/h = 36 × 5/18 = 10 m/s.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Find the third quantity
questionTypes.idea: Gives two of {distance, speed, time} and asks the third. What makes it different: just pick the matching form of D = S × T.
questionTypes.problem: A train covers 240 km in 4 hours. What is its speed?

steps.why: List knowns before choosing a formula.
Have distance and time, want speed → divide.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Unit conversion (km/h ↔ m/s)
questionTypes.idea: The question mixes km/h and m/s (common in train/pole problems). What makes it different: convert first, then solve.
questionTypes.problem: Convert 72 km/h into metres per second.

steps.why: Cancel 72/18 = 4, then × 5 = 20.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Average speed for a round trip
questionTypes.idea: Same distance covered at two different speeds. What makes it different: you must NOT average the speeds — use total distance ÷ total time.
questionTypes.problem: A man goes to office at 40 km/h and returns on the same road at 60 km/h. Find his average speed.

steps.action: Equal distances, so average = 2ab/(a+b) with a = 40, b = 60.
steps.why: Total-distance ÷ total-time simplifies to this when the two distances are equal.
More time is spent at the slower speed, pulling the average below 50.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Late / early (two speeds, two timings)
questionTypes.idea: Same journey done at two speeds gives two arrival times. What makes it different: the DIFFERENCE in the two times is what you use.
questionTypes.problem: A boy walking at 4 km/h reaches school 5 minutes late; at 5 km/h he reaches 10 minutes early. Find the distance.

steps.action: Time gap between the two = 5 + 10 = 15 min = 1/4 hour.
steps.why: 'Late' plus 'early' add up to the total time saved.
The slower speed takes longer; the difference equals the time gap.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Speed and time are inversely proportional
questionTypes.idea: For a fixed distance, if speed changes in a ratio, time changes in the OPPOSITE ratio. What makes it different: no distance value is needed.
questionTypes.problem: For the same distance a car's speed is increased in the ratio 4 : 5. If it first took 30 minutes, how long now?

steps.why: Distance is fixed, so faster means proportionally less time.
Scale the old time by the inverse ratio.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Stoppage time
questionTypes.idea: A bus/train has one speed without stops and a lower average with stops. What makes it different: the speed lost measures the stopping time per hour.
questionTypes.problem: Without stoppages a bus averages 60 km/h; with stoppages it averages 45 km/h. How many minutes per hour does it stop?

steps.why: The 15 km not covered each hour is due to standing still.
Divide the lost distance by the no-stop speed, then × 60 for minutes.

commonMistakes: Averaging the two speeds directly. For a there-and-back trip at 40 and 60, the answer is 48, not (40 + 60) ÷ 2 = 50. Equal distances are NOT equal times, so the plain average is wrong.
Mixing units. If speed is in km/h then time must be in hours, not minutes. 30 minutes is 0.5 hour, not 30.
Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s (or back) when a question mixes them. Multiply by 5/18 to go from km/h to m/s.
Reaching for a formula before writing down what is given — that is how the wrong values get plugged in.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Draw a triangle with D on top and S and T in the two bottom corners. Cover the letter you want with your finger: cover D and you see S × T; cover S and you see D over T (D ÷ T); cover T and you see D over S (D ÷ S). This 'D-S-T triangle' hands you the right formula every time.

crackIt.oneLine: Fix the units first (×5/18 for km/h→m/s), then reach for one dedicated tool per question type: the D-S-T triangle for basics, 2ab/(a+b) for round trips, the time-gap method for late/early, and the inverse-ratio rule when speed changes.

shortcuts.name: D-S-T triangle
shortcuts.method: D on top, S and T below; cover the one you want.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Cover D → S × T; cover S → D ÷ T; cover T → D ÷ S.
shortcuts.example: 240 km in 4 h → cover S → 240 ÷ 4 = 60 km/h.
shortcuts.timeSaved: No formula recall — read it off.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any basic 'two given, find the third'.
shortcuts.whenFails: Units not matched (mixed km/h and m/s, or minutes).

shortcuts.name: Unit conversion ×5/18
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Multiply the given speed by the fraction.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any problem mixing metres and kilometres (trains, poles).

shortcuts.name: Round-trip average 2ab/(a+b)
shortcuts.method: Equal distance at speeds a and b → avg = 2ab/(a+b).
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: a and b are the two speeds.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Same distance covered at two speeds (go & return).
shortcuts.whenFails: Different distances — use total distance ÷ total time.

shortcuts.name: Late/early time-gap
shortcuts.method: d × (1/s₁ − 1/s₂) = total time gap → solve for d.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: s₁ = slower speed, s₂ = faster; gap = late + early (in hours).
shortcuts.timeSaved: One equation instead of two full timings.
shortcuts.whenFails: When distance changes between the two trips.

shortcuts.name: Speed↔time inverse ratio
shortcuts.method: Fixed distance: speeds a:b ⇒ times b:a.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Flip the speed ratio to scale the time.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Same distance, speed given as a ratio or %.

shortcuts.name: Stoppage time per hour
shortcuts.method: Stop min/hr = (speed lost ÷ no-stop speed) × 60.
shortcuts.whenFails: When stop lengths, not the average, are asked.

patternGuide.signal: 'goes and returns' / equal distance, two speeds

patternGuide.use: Time changes in the inverse ratio.

drill.question: At 30 km/h 10 min late, at 45 km/h 10 min early → distance?
Speed ratio 2:3, slower took 6 h → faster takes?
60 km/h no stops, 50 with stops → stop min/hr?

topics.description: Speed of two moving bodies relative to each other — same and opposite directions.

learnIt.realLife: You are walking in a mall and a friend walks toward you — you close the gap fast because both of you move. But if they walk ahead in the same direction, you barely catch up. Relative speed is just how fast the gap between two moving things changes.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Relative speed is how fast one moving object approaches or moves away from another, seen from the other's point of view. If two things move toward each other (or apart), the gap changes at the SUM of their speeds. If they move the same way, the gap changes at the DIFFERENCE. Everything else — meeting time, catching up — comes from this one idea.
learnIt.mathIntro: For two bodies at speeds a and b: moving in opposite directions the relative speed is a+b; in the same direction it is |a−b|. The time for a gap to close (to meet or overtake) is (distance between them) ÷ (relative speed). Keep units consistent — convert km/h to m/s by multiplying by 5/18.

formulas.label: Opposite directions
formulas.breakdown: Both close (or open) the gap, so the speeds add.
formulas.example: 60 and 40 km/h toward each other → 100 km/h.

formulas.label: Same direction
formulas.breakdown: Only the extra speed of the faster one closes the gap.

formulas.label: Meeting / catch-up time
formulas.breakdown: The gap shrinks at the relative speed until it hits zero.
formulas.example: 200 km apart, closing at 100 km/h → 2 hr.

formulas.label: Unit conversion
formulas.breakdown: 1 km = 1000 m and 1 hr = 3600 s.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Opposite directions, when they meet
questionTypes.idea: Two move toward each other. What makes it different: add the speeds.
questionTypes.problem: Two trains 300 km apart move toward each other at 40 and 60 km/h. When do they meet?

steps.why: Opposite directions: add.
Gap ÷ relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Same direction, catching up
questionTypes.idea: One chases another. What makes it different: subtract the speeds.
questionTypes.problem: A car at 40 km/h is 20 km ahead of a car at 60 km/h moving the same way. How long to catch up?

steps.why: Same direction: subtract.
Gap ÷ relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Distance covered before meeting
questionTypes.idea: Find how far one travels. What makes it different: find the meeting time first.
questionTypes.problem: Two people 36 km apart walk toward each other at 4 and 5 km/h. How far does the faster walk before they meet?

steps.why: Add speeds, divide the gap.
Distance = own speed × time.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Head start (thief and police)
questionTypes.idea: A lead has to be closed. What makes it different: the lead is the gap.
questionTypes.problem: A thief runs at 8 km/h. A police officer 10 km behind chases at 10 km/h. How long to catch the thief?

steps.why: Same direction.
Gap ÷ relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Gap after some time
questionTypes.idea: How far apart later. What makes it different: gap grows at the relative speed.
questionTypes.problem: Two cars start from the same point in opposite directions at 50 and 70 km/h. How far apart after 2 hours?

steps.why: Opposite directions.
Gap grows at the relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Find a missing speed
questionTypes.idea: One speed unknown. What makes it different: get the relative speed, then back out one.
questionTypes.problem: Two trains move toward each other, one at 45 km/h and one unknown. They are 250 km apart and meet in 2 hours. Find the second speed.

steps.why: Gap ÷ time.
Subtract the known speed.

commonMistakes: Adding speeds when both move the same way. Same direction → subtract.
Subtracting when they move toward or apart. Opposite → add.
Mixing km/h and m/s. Convert with 5/18 before using them together.
Using the total distance instead of the GAP between them for meeting time.
In catch-up, forgetting the head start is the gap to close.
Assuming 'meet' means each covers half the distance — only true if the speeds are equal.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Opposite → add, same → subtract. Time to meet or catch = gap ÷ relative speed. km/h to m/s ×5/18.

crackIt.oneLine: The gap between two movers changes at the SUM of their speeds if they head opposite ways, and the DIFFERENCE if the same way.

shortcuts.name: Add / subtract rule
shortcuts.method: Opposite = a+b, same = |a−b|.

shortcuts.name: Meet/catch = gap/relative
shortcuts.method: Time = distance apart ÷ relative speed.

shortcuts.name: ×5/18 and ×18/5
shortcuts.method: km/h ↔ m/s.

shortcuts.name: Head start = gap
shortcuts.method: Catch-up time = lead ÷ speed difference.

shortcuts.name: Split by speed ratio
shortcuts.method: When two meet, distances are in the ratio of their speeds.

shortcuts.name: Back out a speed
shortcuts.method: Relative = gap/time, then subtract/add the known.

patternGuide.signal: 'when / after how long do they meet'

drill.question: Opposite from same point, 60+40, 2 hr apart?
Meet in 2 hr, 250 apart, one 45 → other?

topics.description: Crossing poles, platforms and other trains using relative speed.

learnIt.realLife: Standing at a station, you watch a train zoom past — it takes a few seconds for the whole train, engine to last coach, to clear the point where you stand. A train cannot be treated as a dot; its own LENGTH is part of the distance it must travel to pass something.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A train has length, so 'crossing' something means the whole train — front to back — must get past it. To pass a pole (a point), the train travels its own length. To pass a platform or bridge, it travels its own length PLUS the platform's length. When two trains pass each other, both lengths add up as the distance, and their relative speed decides how fast.
learnIt.mathIntro: Time to cross = distance ÷ speed, where the distance depends on what is crossed: a pole or standing man = train's length; a platform or bridge = train length + platform length; another train = sum of both lengths (using relative speed — add if opposite, subtract if same direction). Convert km/h to m/s (×5/18) since lengths are in metres.

formulas.label: Crossing a pole or man
formulas.breakdown: A pole is a point, so the train covers just its own length.

formulas.label: Crossing a platform
formulas.breakdown: The train must clear its own length plus the platform.

formulas.label: Two trains crossing
formulas.breakdown: Both lengths pass; add speeds if opposite, subtract if same way.

formulas.label: Convert speed
formulas.breakdown: Lengths are in metres, so work in m/s.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Crossing a pole
questionTypes.idea: Pass a point. What makes it different: distance is just the train's length.
questionTypes.problem: A train 150 m long runs at 54 km/h. How long to cross a pole?

steps.why: Convert to m/s.
Cross a pole = length ÷ speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Crossing a platform
questionTypes.idea: Pass a length. What makes it different: add the platform length.
questionTypes.problem: A train 200 m long at 72 km/h crosses a platform 100 m long. How long?

steps.why: Train + platform length.
Distance ÷ speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Crossing a standing man
questionTypes.idea: A still man is a point. What makes it different: length only, like a pole.
questionTypes.problem: A train 120 m long at 36 km/h passes a man standing on the platform. How long?

steps.why: Convert.
A standing man is a point — length only.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Crossing a moving man
questionTypes.idea: The man moves too. What makes it different: use relative speed.
questionTypes.problem: A train 150 m long at 63 km/h crosses a man running at 9 km/h in the opposite direction. How long?

steps.why: Opposite directions add.
Length ÷ relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Two trains, opposite directions
questionTypes.idea: Both pass each other head-on. What makes it different: add lengths and add speeds.
questionTypes.problem: Two trains 120 m and 130 m long run toward each other at 40 and 50 km/h. How long to cross each other?

steps.why: Opposite: add, then convert.
Both lengths ÷ relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Two trains, same direction
questionTypes.idea: One overtakes the other. What makes it different: add lengths but subtract speeds.
questionTypes.problem: A 150 m train at 72 km/h overtakes a 100 m train at 36 km/h on a parallel track. How long to fully pass it?

steps.why: Same direction: subtract.
Both lengths ÷ relative speed.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to add the platform length. Pole = train length only; platform = train + platform.
Not converting km/h to m/s when lengths are in metres.
For two trains, forgetting to add BOTH lengths as the distance.
Using the sum of speeds for same-direction crossing — subtract there.
Treating a moving man like a standing one — use relative speed if he moves.
Mixing up which length is the train's and which is the platform's.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Pole/man = train length. Platform = train + platform. Two trains = both lengths ÷ relative speed (add opposite, subtract same). Always work in m/s.

crackIt.oneLine: Crossing distance = train length (+ platform length, or + the other train's length); divide by the right speed in m/s.

shortcuts.name: Pole = L/v
shortcuts.method: Cross a pole or standing man.

shortcuts.name: Platform = (L+P)/v
shortcuts.method: Add the platform length.

shortcuts.name: ×5/18 always
shortcuts.method: Convert km/h to m/s first.

shortcuts.name: Two trains = (L₁+L₂)/rel
shortcuts.method: Add lengths, relative speed.

shortcuts.name: Opposite add, same subtract
shortcuts.method: Pick the relative speed by direction.

shortcuts.name: Two times → find L or v
shortcuts.method: Pole time and platform time solve length and speed.

drill.question: 120 m at 36 km/h crosses standing man?
150 m 72 vs 100 m 36 km/h same dir?
200 m at 90 km/h crosses 100 m bridge?

topics.description: Upstream, downstream and still-water speed problems.

learnIt.realLife: Paddling a boat downriver feels easy — the current pushes you along. Going back upstream against the current is a struggle and takes longer. Boats and streams is about how a river's current adds to your speed one way and subtracts it the other.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A boat in still water has its own speed. In a river, the stream (current) helps when you go downstream — so your effective speed is boat + stream — and fights you upstream, giving boat − stream. Every boats-and-streams problem is built from these two combined speeds.
learnIt.mathIntro: If the boat's still-water speed is b and the stream's speed is s: downstream speed = b + s, upstream speed = b − s. Given the two, you can recover both: b = (downstream + upstream)/2 and s = (downstream − upstream)/2. Times and distances then come from time = distance ÷ speed.

formulas.label: Downstream & upstream
formulas.formula: Down = b + s ;  Up = b − s
formulas.breakdown: The current adds going down, subtracts going up.

formulas.label: Recover boat & stream
formulas.breakdown: Average the two speeds for the boat; half their difference for the stream.

formulas.label: Time
formulas.breakdown: Use down-speed going down, up-speed going up.

formulas.label: Round trip
formulas.breakdown: Add the down and up times for the same distance.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Find downstream / upstream speed
questionTypes.idea: Boat and stream given. What makes it different: add or subtract the current.
questionTypes.problem: A boat's speed in still water is 10 km/h and the stream is 2 km/h. Find its downstream and upstream speeds.

steps.why: Current helps.
Current opposes.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Find boat & stream speed
questionTypes.idea: Down and up given. What makes it different: average and half-difference.
questionTypes.problem: A boat goes downstream at 15 km/h and upstream at 9 km/h. Find the boat's speed and the stream's speed.

steps.why: Average of the two.
Half the difference.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Time for a one-way trip
questionTypes.idea: Find time up or down. What makes it different: pick the right combined speed.
questionTypes.problem: A boat's still-water speed is 8 km/h and the stream 2 km/h. How long to go 30 km upstream?

steps.why: Against the current.
Distance ÷ speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Round trip
questionTypes.idea: Go and return. What makes it different: the two legs have different speeds.
questionTypes.problem: A boat rows to a place 24 km away and back. Its still-water speed is 10 km/h and the stream 2 km/h. Find the total time.

steps.why: b+s and b−s.
Add both legs.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Ratio of times
questionTypes.idea: Up takes k times down. What makes it different: time is inverse to speed for equal distance.
questionTypes.problem: A man rows upstream in twice the time he takes downstream over the same distance. If the stream is 3 km/h, find the boat's speed.

steps.action: Time up = 2 × time down → speed up = ½ speed down, so (b−s) = ½(b+s).
steps.why: Equal distance means time ∝ 1/speed.
Solve with s = 3.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Find stream from times
questionTypes.idea: Times each way given. What makes it different: get speeds from times, then half the difference.
questionTypes.problem: A boat covers 12 km downstream in 1 hour and returns in 1.5 hours. Find the stream's speed.

steps.why: Distance ÷ time each way.
Half the difference.

commonMistakes: Swapping downstream and upstream. Downstream is faster (b + s).
Using b + s for upstream. Upstream is b − s.
Averaging times instead of speeds to get the boat's speed — average the SPEEDS.
In round trips, using one speed for both legs — down and up differ.
Forgetting time is inverse to speed when comparing up and down times.
Mixing 'speed of boat' (still water) with the 'downstream speed'.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Down = boat + stream, Up = boat − stream. Boat = (D+U)/2, Stream = (D−U)/2. Round trip: add the two leg times separately.

crackIt.oneLine: Downstream adds the current and upstream subtracts it, so boat = average of the two speeds and stream = half their difference.

shortcuts.name: Down/Up = b±s
shortcuts.method: Add or subtract the stream.

shortcuts.name: b=(D+U)/2, s=(D−U)/2
shortcuts.method: Recover boat and stream.

shortcuts.name: Time = d/(b±s)
shortcuts.method: Use the right combined speed.

shortcuts.name: Time ratio → speed ratio
shortcuts.method: Times inverse to speeds for equal distance.

shortcuts.name: Times → stream
shortcuts.method: Down and up speeds from times, then (D−U)/2.

drill.question: 24 km each way, down 12 up 8 → total?
Down 12 km in 1 hr, up in 2 hr → stream?

topics.description: Head-starts, beating an opponent, and runners meeting on a circular track.

learnIt.realLife: In a 100 m sprint, the winner crosses the line while the runner-up is still a few metres behind — 'A beats B by 10 metres.' On a circular running track, two joggers going opposite ways keep crossing each other. Races are just speed problems dressed up with head-starts and finish lines.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A race compares two runners over a fixed distance. 'A beats B by 10 m' means when A finishes, B still has 10 m to go — so in the same time A ran the full distance and B ran 10 m less; their speeds are in that ratio. On a circular track, runners meet whenever the faster gains a full lap (same direction) or together they cover one lap (opposite directions).
learnIt.mathIntro: If 'A beats B by x metres' in a race of length d, then in the time A covers d, B covers d − x, so speed_A : speed_B = d : (d − x). A 'start of x metres' means B only has to run d − x. On a circular track of length L, two runners meet after time L ÷ (relative speed) — add speeds if opposite, subtract if same way; they next meet at the start after the LCM of their individual lap times.

formulas.label: Beats by distance
formulas.formula: A beats B by x m in a d-m race → speeds in ratio d : (d − x)
formulas.breakdown: In the same time A runs d and B runs d − x.

formulas.label: Head start
formulas.formula: B given x m start → B runs only (d − x)
formulas.breakdown: B's finish line is x m closer.

formulas.label: Circular meeting
formulas.formula: Time to meet = L ÷ relative speed (add opposite, subtract same)
formulas.breakdown: One lap of gap must close.

formulas.label: Meet at start
formulas.breakdown: Both must be back at the start together.
formulas.example: laps 40 s and 60 s → LCM 120 s.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Beats by a distance
questionTypes.idea: Winner finishes, loser is short. What makes it different: distance ratio = speed ratio.
questionTypes.problem: In a 100 m race, A beats B by 20 m. Find the ratio of their speeds.

steps.action: When A runs 100 m, B runs 100 − 20 = 80 m in the same time.
steps.why: Beat by 20 means B is 20 m short.
Same time → distance ratio = speed ratio.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Head start, who wins
questionTypes.idea: Loser starts ahead. What makes it different: subtract the start from the loser's distance.
questionTypes.problem: In a 100 m race, A gives B a start of 10 m. A runs at 10 m/s and B at 8 m/s. Who wins?

steps.action: A runs 100 m at 10 m/s → 10 s. B runs 100 − 10 = 90 m at 8 m/s → 11.25 s.
steps.why: Start of 10 m means B runs only 90 m.

steps.action: A takes less time, so A wins.
steps.why: Lower time wins.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Beats by time
questionTypes.idea: Loser is slower by t seconds. What makes it different: add the gap to the loser's time.
questionTypes.problem: In a 100 m race, A finishes in 20 seconds and beats B by 5 seconds. Find B's speed.

steps.action: B takes 20 + 5 = 25 s to run 100 m.
steps.why: Beaten by 5 s means B is 5 s slower.
Distance ÷ time.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Beats by distance OR time
questionTypes.idea: Both gaps given. What makes it different: the x m equals the t s for the loser.
questionTypes.problem: In a 200 m race, A beats B by 20 m or 5 seconds. Find A's time for 200 m.

steps.action: B covers the last 20 m in 5 s, so B's speed = 20/5 = 4 m/s.
steps.why: The 20 m gap equals the 5 s gap.

steps.action: B's time for 200 m = 200/4 = 50 s; A beats by 5 s → A = 45 s.
steps.why: A finishes 5 s earlier.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Circular track, first meeting
questionTypes.idea: Two on a loop. What makes it different: one lap of gap over relative speed.
questionTypes.problem: Two runners on a 400 m circular track start together and run in opposite directions at 5 m/s and 3 m/s. When do they first meet?

steps.why: Opposite: add.
One lap of gap ÷ relative speed.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Meeting at the starting point
questionTypes.idea: Both back at the start. What makes it different: LCM of lap times.
questionTypes.problem: On a circular track A completes a lap in 40 s and B in 60 s, running from the start. When do they next meet at the starting point?

steps.action: They meet at the start after the LCM of their lap times.
steps.why: Both must be back together at the start.
Smallest common multiple.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'beats by 20 m' with B running 20 m — it means B is 20 m SHORT of the finish.
Adding a head start to A's distance instead of subtracting it from B's.
For beat-by-time, adding the time to A instead of B (the loser is slower).
On a circular track, using L over the sum for same-direction meets — use the difference of speeds.
Mixing 'first meet anywhere' (L ÷ relative) with 'first meet at start' (LCM of lap times).
Forgetting that speed ratio = distance ratio only when the TIME is the same.
learnIt.memoryTrick: 'Beats by x m' → loser ran d − x in the same time (ratio d:(d−x)). Head start subtracts from the loser's distance. Circular: meet = L ÷ relative (add opposite, subtract same); meet at start = LCM of lap times.

crackIt.oneLine: 'A beats B by x metres' means A ran the full distance while B ran x less in the same time, fixing their speed ratio; on a track, meetings come from relative speed.

shortcuts.name: Beat = ratio d:(d−x)
shortcuts.method: Speeds in ratio of distances.

shortcuts.name: Start subtracts from loser
shortcuts.method: B runs d − start.

shortcuts.name: Beat by time → add to loser
shortcuts.method: Loser's time = winner + gap.

shortcuts.name: x m = t s → loser's speed
shortcuts.method: Beat by x m or t s: speed = x/t.

shortcuts.name: Circular meet = L/rel
shortcuts.method: Add opposite, subtract same.

shortcuts.name: Meet at start = LCM of laps
shortcuts.method: LCM of individual lap times.

patternGuide.signal: 'gives B a start of x m'
'beats by x m or t seconds'

drill.question: 100 m, beat by 25 m → speed ratio?
100 m, B start 20 m → B runs?
A 20 s, beats B by 5 s → B time?
Beat by 20 m or 4 s → loser speed?
400 m track, 6 & 4 m/s same dir → meet?
Laps 30 s & 45 s → meet at start?

groups.description: Work with variables and equations, and handle number sequences like AP and GP.

topics.description: Work with variables, expressions and algebraic identities.

learnIt.realLife: When a shop says 'the price went up by some amount and I bought a few of them,' you cannot put a number on the total until you know that amount — so you call it a letter and carry it through. Algebra is just arithmetic where some numbers are still unknown, held as letters until you learn their value.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Algebra uses letters to stand for numbers you do not know yet. An expression like 3x + 5 is a recipe: whatever x turns out to be, triple it and add 5. Algebraic identities are shortcuts that are ALWAYS true — like (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b² — so you can expand, factor or simplify without multiplying everything out each time.
learnIt.mathIntro: The core tools are a handful of identities. Squares: (a±b)² = a² ± 2ab + b². Difference of squares: a² − b² = (a+b)(a−b). From these, a² + b² = (a+b)² − 2ab. Cubes: a³ ± b³ = (a ± b)(a² ∓ ab + b²), and (a+b)³ = a³ + b³ + 3ab(a+b). Substituting given values into these finds quantities without knowing a and b separately.

formulas.label: Square of a sum/difference
formulas.breakdown: Square each term and add twice their product (sign follows the middle).

formulas.label: Difference of squares
formulas.breakdown: A difference of two squares always factors into sum × difference.

formulas.label: Sum of squares from sum & product
formulas.breakdown: Rearranged from the square identity to avoid finding a and b.

formulas.label: Sum/difference of cubes
formulas.breakdown: Cubes factor with a matching linear and quadratic piece.

formulas.label: Reciprocal identity
formulas.breakdown: The cross term 2·a·(1/a) is always 2.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Evaluate an expression
questionTypes.idea: Numbers given for the letters. What makes it different: pure substitution.
questionTypes.problem: If x = 2 and y = 3, find the value of 2x² + 3xy − y².

steps.why: Substitute x=2, y=3.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Expand with the square identity
questionTypes.idea: A squared bracket. What makes it different: use (a+b)², not FOIL.
questionTypes.problem: Expand (2x + 5)².

steps.why: Apply (a+b)² = a²+2ab+b².
Compute each term.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Difference of squares
questionTypes.idea: Two squares subtracted. What makes it different: factor into sum × difference.
questionTypes.problem: Evaluate 105² − 95² without long multiplication.

steps.why: a²−b² = (a+b)(a−b).
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — a² + b² from sum & product
questionTypes.idea: Sum and product given. What makes it different: rearrange the square identity.
questionTypes.problem: If a + b = 7 and ab = 10, find a² + b².

steps.why: Rearranged identity.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Cubes and reciprocals
questionTypes.idea: a + 1/a given. What makes it different: climb the power ladder.
questionTypes.problem: If a + 1/a = 4, find a³ + 1/a³.

steps.why: Cube identity with a·(1/a) = 1.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Symmetric expressions
questionTypes.idea: Difference and product given. What makes it different: use (a−b)².
questionTypes.problem: If a − b = 3 and ab = 4, find a² + b².

steps.why: From (a−b)² = a²−2ab+b².
Compute.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the middle term: (a+b)² is a²+2ab+b², NOT a²+b².
Sign slip in (a−b)²: the middle term is −2ab, but a² and b² stay positive.
Treating a²+b² as (a+b)² — it is (a+b)² minus 2ab.
In a²−b² factoring, losing track of which bracket is the sum and which the difference.
For cubes, forgetting the −3ab(a+b) correction term.
Dropping the +2 (or −2) cross term in reciprocal identities.
learnIt.memoryTrick: (a±b)² = a² ± 2ab + b². a²−b² = (a+b)(a−b). a²+b² = (a+b)²−2ab. a³±b³ = (a±b)(a²∓ab+b²). For a+1/a, the cross term is always 2.

crackIt.oneLine: Memorise the core identities — squares, difference of squares, sum/difference of cubes — and most algebra questions collapse to a single substitution.

shortcuts.name: (a±b)² instant
shortcuts.method: Square the ends, double the product.

shortcuts.name: Diff of squares
shortcuts.method: x²−y²=(x+y)(x−y).

shortcuts.name: a²+b²=(a+b)²−2ab
shortcuts.method: From sum and product.

shortcuts.name: Cube from sum
shortcuts.method: a³+b³=(a+b)³−3ab(a+b).

shortcuts.name: Reciprocal ladder
shortcuts.method: a+1/a → a²+1/a² (square, −2) → a³+1/a³ (cube, −3·sum).

shortcuts.name: Plug and go
shortcuts.method: Substitute the given numbers directly.

topics.description: Solve linear, quadratic and simultaneous equations and word problems.

learnIt.realLife: You have ₹100 and buy some ₹15 pens; how many can you get? You are really solving 15x = 100 (or ≤) — turning a real question into a small equation and finding the unknown. Equations are the tool for 'find the number that makes this true.'
learnIt.plainMeaning: An equation says two things are equal and hides an unknown in it. Solving means finding the value(s) of the unknown that make both sides balance. A linear equation has the variable to the first power (one answer); a quadratic has it squared (up to two answers). Simultaneous equations are two conditions solved together to pin down two unknowns.
learnIt.mathIntro: A linear equation ax + b = 0 gives x = −b/a. Two linear equations in x and y are solved by elimination or substitution. A quadratic ax² + bx + c = 0 is solved by factoring, or by x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac))/(2a). Even without solving, the roots satisfy: sum = −b/a and product = c/a, and the discriminant b²−4ac tells how many real roots exist.

formulas.label: Linear equation
formulas.breakdown: Isolate x by undoing the operations.

formulas.label: Quadratic formula
formulas.breakdown: Solves any quadratic when factoring is hard.

formulas.label: Sum & product of roots
formulas.breakdown: Read straight off the coefficients, no solving.

formulas.label: Discriminant
formulas.formula: D = b² − 4ac : >0 two real, =0 equal, <0 none
formulas.breakdown: Tells the number and type of roots.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Linear equation
questionTypes.idea: One variable, first power. What makes it different: isolate x.
questionTypes.problem: Solve 3x − 7 = 11.

steps.why: Move the constant across.
Divide by the coefficient.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Simultaneous equations
questionTypes.idea: Two equations, two unknowns. What makes it different: eliminate one variable.
questionTypes.problem: Solve x + y = 10 and x − y = 4.

steps.why: Adding cancels y.
Substitute back.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Quadratic by factoring
questionTypes.idea: x² term present. What makes it different: split into two factors.
questionTypes.problem: Solve x² − 5x + 6 = 0.

steps.action: Two numbers multiplying to 6 and adding to −5: −2 and −3.
steps.why: Factor by product/sum.
Set each factor to zero.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Quadratic formula / discriminant
questionTypes.idea: No easy factors. What makes it different: use the formula.
questionTypes.problem: Solve 2x² − 4x − 6 = 0 using the formula.

steps.why: Discriminant first.
Apply the formula.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Sum & product of roots
questionTypes.idea: Asks about roots without solving. What makes it different: read the coefficients.
questionTypes.problem: Without solving, find the sum and product of the roots of 3x² − 12x + 9 = 0.

steps.why: −b/a.
c/a.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Word problem to equation
questionTypes.idea: A story hides an equation. What makes it different: define the variable first.
questionTypes.problem: The sum of two numbers is 15 and their difference is 3. Find the numbers.

steps.why: Set up and eliminate.
Substitute.

commonMistakes: Sign errors when moving a term across the equals sign — it flips sign.
Dividing only part of a side by the coefficient — divide the whole side.
Forgetting a quadratic can have TWO answers.
Using the formula with the wrong sign of b or c — write ax²+bx+c first.
Confusing sum of roots (−b/a) with product (c/a).
Setting up word problems with the wrong unknown — define the variable clearly first.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Linear: isolate x. Simultaneous: add/subtract to kill a variable. Quadratic: factor to product=c, sum=b, or use (−b±√(b²−4ac))/2a. Sum of roots −b/a, product c/a.

crackIt.oneLine: Linear equations isolate the unknown; quadratics factor to the pair whose product is c and sum is b, or fall to the formula.

shortcuts.name: Isolate x
shortcuts.method: Undo +/− then ×/÷.

shortcuts.name: Add/subtract to eliminate
shortcuts.method: Combine the two equations to drop a variable.

shortcuts.name: Product=c, sum=b factor
shortcuts.method: Split the middle term.

shortcuts.name: Sum/product shortcut
shortcuts.method: −b/a and c/a directly.

shortcuts.name: Discriminant check
shortcuts.method: b²−4ac sign → root type.

topics.description: Compare expressions with <, >, ≤, ≥ and solve for the range of values.

learnIt.realLife: A ride charges ₹50 base plus ₹12 a km, and you have ₹200. How far can you go? You need 50 + 12x ≤ 200 — not an exact number but a limit. Inequalities describe 'at most,' 'at least,' 'more than' — the everyday language of budgets and limits.
learnIt.plainMeaning: An inequality compares two sides that are NOT necessarily equal — one is greater or smaller. You solve it almost exactly like an equation, isolating the variable, with one crucial twist: if you multiply or divide both sides by a negative number, the inequality sign flips direction. The answer is a range of values, not a single number.
learnIt.mathIntro: Solve a linear inequality by isolating x, remembering to reverse the sign (< becomes >) whenever you multiply or divide by a negative. A double inequality like 3 < 2x + 1 < 9 is solved on all three parts at once. A quadratic inequality (x − a)(x − b) < 0 is true BETWEEN the roots; > 0 means OUTSIDE them.

formulas.label: Linear inequality
formulas.formula: Solve like an equation; flip the sign on ×/÷ by a negative
formulas.breakdown: Isolate x; reverse < ↔ > only when the multiplier is negative.

formulas.label: Double inequality
formulas.formula: a < expression < b: operate on all three parts
formulas.breakdown: Keep the variable in the middle.

formulas.label: Quadratic inequality
formulas.breakdown: The product is negative only between the roots.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Linear inequality
questionTypes.idea: Isolate x. What makes it different: same steps as an equation.
questionTypes.problem: Solve 2x + 3 < 11.

steps.why: Subtract 3.
Divide by 2 (positive, no flip).

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Flip on a negative
questionTypes.idea: Divide by a negative. What makes it different: reverse the sign.
questionTypes.problem: Solve −3x + 2 ≥ 11.

steps.why: Subtract 2.
Divide by −3 — flip the sign.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Double inequality
questionTypes.idea: Variable trapped between two bounds. What makes it different: act on all three parts.
questionTypes.problem: Solve 3 ≤ 2x − 1 < 7.

steps.why: Same operation on all three parts.
Positive divisor, no flip.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Counting integer solutions
questionTypes.idea: Asks how many integers fit. What makes it different: honour < vs ≤ at the ends.
questionTypes.problem: How many integers satisfy −2 < x ≤ 3?

steps.why: Integers strictly above −2 and up to 3.
Count them.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Quadratic inequality
questionTypes.idea: x² term with an inequality. What makes it different: between or outside the roots.
questionTypes.problem: Solve x² − 5x + 6 < 0.

steps.why: Find the roots 2 and 3.
Product is negative only between.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Word problem
questionTypes.idea: A limit in words. What makes it different: translate 'at least/at most' into ≥/≤.
questionTypes.problem: A student needs an average of at least 80 over two tests. She scored 75 on the first. What must she score on the second?

steps.why: Set up the average condition.
Solve.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to flip the sign when dividing/multiplying by a negative.
Flipping the sign when multiplying by a POSITIVE (only negatives flip).
In double inequalities, operating on only two of the three parts.
Counting endpoints wrong: < excludes, ≤ includes.
For quadratic inequalities, giving 'outside' when it should be 'between' (or vice versa).
Treating an inequality answer as a single value instead of a range.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Solve like an equation — but flip < ↔ > when you ×/÷ by a negative. Double inequality: do everything to all three parts. Quadratic: (x−p)(x−q)<0 is BETWEEN the roots, >0 is OUTSIDE.

crackIt.oneLine: An inequality is solved exactly like an equation, except the sign flips whenever you multiply or divide by a negative number.

shortcuts.name: Isolate, flip on negative
shortcuts.method: Move terms; reverse the sign only on ×/÷ by a negative.

shortcuts.name: All-three-parts
shortcuts.method: Operate on the whole double inequality at once.

shortcuts.name: Count integers
shortcuts.method: List the endpoints, honour < vs ≤.

shortcuts.name: Between vs outside
shortcuts.method: (x−p)(x−q)<0 between; >0 outside.

shortcuts.name: Endpoint rule
shortcuts.method: < / > open (exclude); ≤ / ≥ closed (include).

shortcuts.name: Word → symbol
shortcuts.method: 'at least' ≥, 'at most' ≤, 'more than' >.

topics.description: Arithmetic and geometric sequences and their sums.

learnIt.realLife: Your salary starts at ₹20,000 and rises ₹2,000 every year — that is an arithmetic progression, a fixed amount added each step. A rumour that doubles the number of people who have heard it each hour is a geometric progression, multiplied by a fixed factor. Progressions describe anything that grows by a steady rule.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A progression is a list of numbers following a fixed rule. In an arithmetic progression (AP) you ADD the same number (the common difference) to get the next term. In a geometric progression (GP) you MULTIPLY by the same number (the common ratio). Once you spot which one it is, simple formulas give any term or the total of many terms.
learnIt.mathIntro: For an AP with first term a and common difference d, the nth term is a + (n−1)d and the sum of n terms is (n/2)[2a + (n−1)d], which equals (n/2)(first + last). For a GP with first term a and ratio r, the nth term is a·r^(n−1) and the sum of n terms is a(rⁿ − 1)/(r − 1); if |r| < 1, an infinite GP sums to a/(1 − r).

formulas.label: AP nth term
formulas.breakdown: Start at a and add d, (n−1) times.

formulas.label: AP sum
formulas.breakdown: Average of first and last, times how many.

formulas.label: GP nth term
formulas.breakdown: Start at a and multiply by r, (n−1) times.

formulas.label: GP sum (finite)
formulas.breakdown: Total of n terms of a GP.

formulas.label: GP infinite sum
formulas.breakdown: A shrinking GP settles on a finite total.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — AP nth term
questionTypes.idea: Fixed difference. What makes it different: add d, (n−1) times.
questionTypes.problem: Find the 10th term of the AP 3, 7, 11, ...

steps.why: Tₙ = a + (n−1)d.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — AP sum
questionTypes.idea: Add many AP terms. What makes it different: average of ends × count.
questionTypes.problem: Find the sum of the first 20 terms of the AP 2, 5, 8, ...

steps.why: Sum formula.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — GP nth term
questionTypes.idea: Fixed ratio. What makes it different: multiply by r, (n−1) times.
questionTypes.problem: Find the 6th term of the GP 2, 6, 18, ...

steps.why: Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — GP finite sum
questionTypes.idea: Add many GP terms. What makes it different: use a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1).
questionTypes.problem: Find the sum of the first 5 terms of the GP 3, 6, 12, ...

steps.why: GP sum formula.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — GP infinite sum
questionTypes.idea: A never-ending shrinking GP. What makes it different: a/(1−r) when |r|<1.
questionTypes.problem: Find the sum of the infinite GP 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 + ...

steps.why: |r| < 1, so it converges.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Number of terms / word problem
questionTypes.idea: Given the last term, find n. What makes it different: set Tₙ = last.
questionTypes.problem: How many terms are there in the AP 5, 8, 11, ..., 50?

steps.why: Set Tₙ = last.
Solve for n.

commonMistakes: Using n instead of (n−1) in the nth-term formula.
Mixing up AP (add d) and GP (multiply r).
In the AP sum, forgetting the (n/2) factor or using the wrong last term.
Applying the infinite GP sum when |r| ≥ 1 (it does not converge).
Off-by-one when counting the number of terms (n = 16, not 15).
Confusing the common difference d with the common ratio r.
learnIt.memoryTrick: AP: add d, Tₙ = a+(n−1)d, Sₙ = (n/2)(first+last). GP: multiply r, Tₙ = ar^(n−1), Sₙ = a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1), infinite = a/(1−r) when |r|<1.

crackIt.oneLine: AP adds a fixed d (use a+(n−1)d and average-times-count for the sum); GP multiplies a fixed r (use ar^(n−1) and a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1)).

shortcuts.name: AP nth = a+(n−1)d
shortcuts.method: Plug a, d, n.

shortcuts.name: AP sum = (n/2)(first+last)
shortcuts.method: Average of ends × count.

shortcuts.name: GP nth = ar^(n−1)
shortcuts.method: Multiply r, (n−1) times.

shortcuts.name: GP sum = a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1)
shortcuts.method: Finite GP total.

shortcuts.name: Infinite GP = a/(1−r)
shortcuts.method: Only for |r|<1.

shortcuts.name: Count = (last−first)/d + 1
shortcuts.method: Number of AP terms.

groups.description: Shapes and space — lines, angles, triangles and circles, plus area, surface area and volume.

topics.description: Lines, angles, triangles, circles and polygons and their properties.

videos.label: Complete Geometry — all types (SSC PYQ) | Maths By Ashish Sir
videos.note: Angles, triangles and circles worked through previous-year questions

videos.label: Geometry Complete Chapter — Zero to Hero | SK Ojha Sir
videos.note: Right angles, triangle properties and circle theorems from scratch

learnIt.realLife: Look at the corner where two walls meet the floor, the slice of a pizza, or the spokes of a bicycle wheel. Geometry is simply the study of shapes and the angles and lengths inside them. You do not need to see the figure in real life — every geometry question hides a small set of fixed rules, and once you know which rule fits, the answer falls out.
learnIt.plainMeaning: An angle measures how much you turn, from 0° to 360°. A triangle is a 3-sided shape whose three angles always add up to 180°. A circle is all the points the same distance from a centre. Most questions just ask you to find a missing angle or a missing length using a rule you already know — the skill is spotting which rule applies.
learnIt.mathIntro: A few rules cover almost everything: angles on a straight line add to 180° and around a point to 360°; the three angles of any triangle add to 180°; a right-angled triangle obeys Pythagoras (a² + b² = c²); and the angles of an n-sided polygon add to (n − 2) × 180°. Circle questions lean on two facts: the angle in a semicircle is 90°, and the angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference on the same arc.

formulas.label: Angles on a line / around a point
formulas.breakdown: Angles that sit together on one straight line always total 180°; angles that go all the way around a single point total 360°.
formulas.example: If one angle on a line is 130°, the other is 180 − 130 = 50°.

formulas.label: Angle sum of a triangle
formulas.breakdown: The three inside angles of any triangle always add up to 180°, no matter its shape.

formulas.label: Exterior angle of a triangle
formulas.formula: Exterior angle = sum of the two opposite interior angles
formulas.breakdown: An angle made by extending one side equals the two inside angles it does not touch, added together.

formulas.label: Pythagoras' theorem
formulas.formula: a² + b² = c²  (c is the longest side, the hypotenuse)
formulas.breakdown: In a right-angled triangle the square of the longest side equals the sum of the squares of the other two.

formulas.label: Angles of a polygon
formulas.breakdown: Split an n-sided polygon into (n − 2) triangles; the exterior angles of any polygon always total 360°.

formulas.label: Key circle facts
formulas.formula: Angle in a semicircle = 90° ;  Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference
formulas.breakdown: A triangle drawn on a diameter has a right angle at the third point; an angle measured from the centre is double the same-arc angle measured from the edge.
formulas.example: Circumference angle 40° on an arc → centre angle = 80°.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Missing angle in a triangle
questionTypes.idea: Gives two angles (or a relation) and asks the third. What makes it different: use the 180° sum.
questionTypes.problem: Two angles of a triangle are 65° and 75°. Find the third angle.

steps.why: The three angles must total 180°, so first see how much is used up.
Whatever is left of 180° is the missing angle.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Exterior angle of a triangle
questionTypes.idea: Extends one side and asks the outside angle. What makes it different: add the two far interior angles instead of using 180°.
questionTypes.problem: A triangle has interior angles 55° and 65° not touching the extended side. Find the exterior angle.

steps.action: Exterior angle = sum of the two opposite interior angles = 55 + 65.
steps.why: The exterior-angle rule skips the long way through 180°.
Add them.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Pythagoras: find a side
questionTypes.idea: Right-angled triangle with two sides given. What makes it different: decide whether you are finding the hypotenuse or a leg.
questionTypes.problem: A right-angled triangle has legs 6 cm and 8 cm. Find the hypotenuse.

steps.why: The hypotenuse squared is the sum of the squares of the legs.
Take the square root to get the length.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Angles in parallel lines
questionTypes.idea: A line cuts two parallel lines. What makes it different: name the pair (co-interior add to 180°, alternate/corresponding are equal).
questionTypes.problem: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One co-interior (same-side interior) angle is 110°. Find the other.

steps.why: Same-side interior angles between parallels are supplementary.
Subtract from 180°.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Polygon angles
questionTypes.idea: Gives the number of sides and asks an interior or exterior angle. What makes it different: pick interior sum or exterior 360°.
questionTypes.problem: Find each interior angle of a regular hexagon.

steps.why: A hexagon splits into 4 triangles.
Regular means all angles are equal, so divide by the number of sides.

questionTypes.name: Type 6 — Circle theorem
questionTypes.idea: Angle at the centre vs at the edge, or angle in a semicircle. What makes it different: double/halve or spot the right angle.
questionTypes.problem: An arc makes an angle of 50° at the circumference. What angle does it make at the centre?

steps.action: Angle at centre = 2 × angle at circumference.
steps.why: Same arc, so the centre angle is double.
Multiply by 2.

commonMistakes: Forgetting that a triangle's angles total 180°, not 360°. Only quadrilaterals total 360°.
In Pythagoras, treating a leg as the hypotenuse. The hypotenuse is always the longest side and sits opposite the right angle.
Confusing co-interior angles (add to 180°) with alternate/corresponding angles (equal). Name the pair before you decide.
Using (n − 2) × 180 for a single angle. That formula gives the SUM; divide by n for one angle of a regular polygon.
Mixing up the centre angle and circumference angle — the centre one is the bigger (double) one.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Triangle = 180, quadrilateral = 360, and each extra side adds 180. Pythagoras only for right angles, and the hypotenuse is the longest side. Circle: 'centre is double, semicircle is 90'.

crackIt.oneLine: Almost every figure reduces to one rule — triangle angles make 180°, exterior = sum of the two far angles, Pythagoras for right angles, polygon exteriors total 360°, and 'centre is double the edge'.

shortcuts.name: Missing angle in a triangle
shortcuts.method: Third angle = 180 − (sum of the other two).
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Add the two given angles, subtract from 180.
shortcuts.whenWorks: Any triangle with two angles known.
shortcuts.whenFails: When sides, not angles, are given.

shortcuts.name: Exterior angle
shortcuts.method: Exterior = sum of the two opposite interior angles.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Add the two interior angles away from the exterior one.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Skips finding the third interior angle first.
shortcuts.whenFails: When the two angles given touch the exterior angle.

shortcuts.name: Pythagoras
shortcuts.method: Longest² = sum of the other two squared; memorise triples 3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Recognising a triple gives the answer instantly.

shortcuts.name: Regular polygon angle
shortcuts.method: Each exterior = 360/n; each interior = 180 − 360/n.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Divide 360 by the number of sides.

patternGuide.use: Exterior angle = sum of two opposite interiors.

drill.question: Two angles of a triangle are 40° and 70°. Third?
Each exterior angle of a regular pentagon?

crackIt.whyItWorks: The triangle sum, exterior-angle and polygon rules are all the same fact seen differently — a polygon is just triangles glued together. Pythagoras is the fixed relationship of a right angle, and the circle facts come from the centre 'seeing' an arc from twice as wide as the edge does.
whenWorks: Angle-chasing questions where you know two of the angles around a point, line or triangle.
Right-triangle side questions, especially when a 3-4-5 style triple appears.
whenNot: Pythagoras never applies unless there is a right angle.
The 360/n shortcut is for REGULAR polygons only — irregular ones need the full angle sum.
crackIt.practicePattern: Any 'find the missing angle' figure is a triangle-sum or exterior-angle question; any right triangle with two sides is a Pythagoras question; any 'regular n-gon' is a 360/n question.

topics.description: Points, lines and distances on the x–y plane.

videos.label: Coordinate Geometry — distance & section formula (full chapter)
videos.note: Distance formula derivation, questions and the section formula

videos.label: Slope and Distance — Math Antics
videos.note: Clear English walk-through of slope and the distance formula

learnIt.realLife: A map uses two numbers to pin any place — how far across and how far up. A chessboard names a square by its column and row. Coordinate geometry is exactly this: describe any point by a pair (x, y), and then answer questions like how far apart two points are or where the midpoint lies — all with simple formulas instead of a ruler.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A point is written (x, y): x is how far right (or left if negative) and y is how far up (or down). Two points define a line. From the coordinates alone you can find the distance between two points, the midpoint, how a point divides a line, the slope (steepness) of the line, and the area of a triangle from its three corners.
learnIt.mathIntro: Everything comes from the two coordinates of each point. Distance uses Pythagoras on the differences in x and y. The midpoint is the average of the coordinates. Slope is rise over run, (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁). Parallel lines share a slope; perpendicular slopes multiply to −1.

formulas.label: Distance between two points
formulas.breakdown: Take the gap in x and the gap in y, square each, add, and square-root — it is just Pythagoras on the plane.

formulas.label: Midpoint
formulas.breakdown: The middle point is simply the average of the two x-values and the average of the two y-values.

formulas.label: Section formula (ratio m:n)
formulas.breakdown: Finds the point dividing the join of two points in the ratio m:n internally.

formulas.label: Slope of a line
formulas.breakdown: Slope is rise (change in y) over run (change in x) — how steep the line is.

formulas.label: Parallel and perpendicular
formulas.formula: Parallel: m₁ = m₂ ;  Perpendicular: m₁ × m₂ = −1
formulas.breakdown: Lines with equal slope never meet; lines whose slopes multiply to −1 cross at a right angle.

formulas.label: Area of a triangle from vertices
formulas.formula: Area = ½ |x₁(y₂ − y₃) + x₂(y₃ − y₁) + x₃(y₁ − y₂)|
formulas.breakdown: Plug the three corners into the formula; the bars mean take the positive value.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Distance between two points
questionTypes.idea: Gives two points, asks the gap. What makes it different: Pythagoras on the coordinate differences.
questionTypes.problem: Find the distance between (1, 2) and (4, 6).

steps.action: Gap in x = 4 − 1 = 3; gap in y = 6 − 2 = 4.
steps.why: Distance uses the differences of the coordinates.
Square, add, square-root.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Midpoint
questionTypes.idea: Asks the middle of a segment. What makes it different: just average the coordinates.
questionTypes.problem: Find the midpoint of (2, 3) and (6, 7).

steps.why: The midpoint's x is the mean of the two x-values.
Likewise for y.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Slope of a line
questionTypes.idea: Gives two points, asks steepness. What makes it different: rise over run.
questionTypes.problem: Find the slope of the line through (1, 2) and (3, 8).

steps.why: Change in y over change in x.
Simplify.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Perpendicular slope
questionTypes.idea: Gives one slope, asks the perpendicular. What makes it different: negative reciprocal.
questionTypes.problem: A line has slope 2. What is the slope of a line perpendicular to it?

steps.action: Perpendicular slopes multiply to −1, so m₂ = −1/m₁.
steps.why: That is the right-angle condition.
Flip and change sign.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Area of a triangle from vertices
questionTypes.idea: Three corner coordinates, asks the area. What makes it different: use the vertex-area formula.
questionTypes.problem: Find the area of the triangle with vertices (0, 0), (4, 0) and (0, 3).

steps.action: Area = ½ |x₁(y₂ − y₃) + x₂(y₃ − y₁) + x₃(y₁ − y₂)|.
steps.why: The standard formula for area from coordinates.
Substitute and simplify.

commonMistakes: Subtracting coordinates in a different order for x and y in the distance formula. Squaring hides the sign, so it is forgiving — but be consistent.
Adding instead of averaging for the midpoint. Midpoint is the mean, so divide by 2.
Writing slope as run over rise. It is rise over run: change in y on top.
Using the reciprocal without the minus sign for a perpendicular. It must be the NEGATIVE reciprocal.
Forgetting the ½ or the absolute-value bars in the triangle-area formula.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Distance = Pythagoras on the gaps. Midpoint = average. Slope = rise/run. Perpendicular = flip and negate. These four cover most questions.

crackIt.oneLine: On the plane it is always the same four moves — distance is Pythagoras on (Δx, Δy), midpoint is the average, slope is rise/run, and perpendicular is the negative reciprocal.

shortcuts.name: Distance
shortcuts.method: √(Δx² + Δy²); spot 3-4-5 / 5-12-13 to skip the surd.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Δx and Δy are the coordinate differences.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Recognising a triple gives it instantly.

shortcuts.name: Midpoint
shortcuts.method: Average each coordinate.

shortcuts.name: Slope & perpendicular
shortcuts.method: Slope = Δy/Δx; perpendicular = −Δx/Δy (flip and negate).

patternGuide.use: Rise over run; equal slopes for parallel.

drill.question: Perpendicular slope to a line of slope 4?

crackIt.whyItWorks: The distance formula is Pythagoras with the horizontal and vertical gaps as the two legs. Slope captures steepness as a ratio, and the perpendicular condition m₁m₂ = −1 is what makes two lines meet at a right angle.
whenWorks: Any question giving coordinates and asking distance, midpoint, slope or area.
Parallel/perpendicular checks by comparing slopes.
whenNot: Curved shapes (circles, parabolas) need their own equations, not these point formulas.
Vertical lines have an undefined slope — handle them separately.
crackIt.practicePattern: Any 'find the distance/midpoint/slope' question, or 'area of the triangle with vertices…', is a direct plug-in of one of these four formulas.

topics.description: Area and perimeter of 2D shapes and the surface area and volume of 3D solids.

videos.label: Mensuration 2D & 3D — one shot (all formulas + tricks)
videos.note: Every 2D/3D formula with shortcut methods & exam questions

learnIt.realLife: How much paint for a wall, how much water a tank holds, how much wrapping paper for a box — all of these are mensuration. It is the maths of measuring flat shapes (area and perimeter) and solid objects (surface area and volume). Every question is really just 'pick the right shape's formula and put the numbers in'.
learnIt.plainMeaning: Perimeter is the distance around the edge of a flat shape. Area is the space inside it. For a solid, surface area is the total skin covering it and volume is the space it fills. The whole topic is a small library of formulas, one per shape — the skill is recognising the shape and using the matching formula with consistent units.
learnIt.mathIntro: For 2D shapes: rectangle area = length × breadth, triangle = ½ × base × height, circle = πr². For 3D solids: cuboid volume = l × b × h, cylinder = πr²h, sphere = 4/3 πr³. Surface areas follow their own formulas per solid. Use π ≈ 22/7 (or 3.14) and keep every length in the same unit before you calculate.

formulas.label: Rectangle and square
formulas.formula: Rectangle: area = l × b, perimeter = 2(l + b) ;  Square: area = a², perimeter = 4a
formulas.breakdown: Multiply the two sides for area; add all sides for perimeter. A square is a rectangle with equal sides.

formulas.label: Triangle
formulas.formula: Area = ½ × base × height ;  Equilateral area = (√3/4) a²
formulas.breakdown: Half of base times the perpendicular height. An equilateral triangle has its own shortcut using the side a.

formulas.label: Circle
formulas.breakdown: r is the radius (half the diameter). π ≈ 22/7. Area uses r squared; circumference is the distance around.

formulas.label: Cuboid and cube
formulas.formula: Cuboid: volume = l·b·h, surface = 2(lb + bh + hl) ;  Cube: volume = a³, surface = 6a²
formulas.breakdown: Volume multiplies all three dimensions; surface area adds up all the faces.

formulas.label: Cylinder
formulas.formula: Volume = πr²h ;  Curved surface = 2πrh ;  Total surface = 2πr(r + h)
formulas.breakdown: A cylinder is a circle pushed up by height h. Curved surface is the label around it; total adds the two circular ends.

formulas.label: Sphere and cone
formulas.formula: Sphere: volume = 4/3 πr³, surface = 4πr² ;  Cone: volume = 1/3 πr²h
formulas.breakdown: A cone holds one-third of the cylinder with the same base and height; a sphere's surface is 4πr².

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Area of a rectangle or square
questionTypes.idea: Gives sides, asks area or perimeter. What makes it different: multiply for area, add for perimeter.
questionTypes.problem: A rectangle is 12 m long and 5 m wide. Find its area.

steps.why: Area of a rectangle is the product of its two sides.
Multiply.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Area and circumference of a circle
questionTypes.idea: Gives the radius (or diameter), asks area or circumference. What makes it different: use π and r².
questionTypes.problem: Find the area of a circle of radius 7 cm (take π = 22/7).

steps.why: Area of a circle uses the radius squared.
The 7 cancels neatly with 22/7.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Volume of a cuboid or cube
questionTypes.idea: Gives dimensions, asks volume. What makes it different: multiply all three lengths.
questionTypes.problem: Find the volume of a cube of side 5 cm.

steps.why: All three sides of a cube are equal.
Cube the side.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Volume of a cylinder
questionTypes.idea: Gives radius and height, asks volume. What makes it different: circle area × height.
questionTypes.problem: Find the volume of a cylinder of radius 7 cm and height 10 cm (π = 22/7).

steps.why: A cylinder is its circular base area times its height.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Surface area of a cuboid
questionTypes.idea: Gives the three dimensions, asks total surface. What makes it different: add all six faces.
questionTypes.problem: Find the total surface area of a cuboid 5 × 4 × 3 cm.

steps.why: Opposite faces are equal, so double the three distinct face areas.
Add inside the bracket, then double.

commonMistakes: Mixing units — adding centimetres to metres. Convert everything to one unit before calculating.
Using diameter in place of radius. r is half the diameter; area = πr², not πd².
Confusing area (square units) with volume (cubic units). Check the units the answer should have.
Forgetting the ½ for a triangle or the 1/3 for a cone.
Using curved surface area when the question asks for total surface area (which also includes the ends).
learnIt.memoryTrick: Flat shapes give area in square units; solids give volume in cubic units. Cone = ⅓ of its cylinder; sphere volume = 4/3 πr³. Use π = 22/7 whenever the radius is a multiple of 7.

crackIt.oneLine: Recognise the shape, recall its one formula, and keep units the same — circles love r = 7 with π = 22/7, and a cone is always one-third of its matching cylinder.

shortcuts.name: Circle with r a multiple of 7
shortcuts.method: Use π = 22/7 so the 7s cancel.

shortcuts.name: Cone from cylinder
shortcuts.method: Cone volume = ⅓ × matching cylinder volume.

shortcuts.name: Cube scaling
shortcuts.method: Volume scales as the cube of the side ratio; surface as the square.

shortcuts.name: Square from diagonal
shortcuts.method: Area = d²/2.

drill.question: Area of a triangle, base 8, height 5?
Volume of a cylinder r=7, h=2 (π=22/7)?

crackIt.whyItWorks: Every formula is built from the same idea of counting unit squares (area) or unit cubes (volume). The circle uses π because that is the fixed ratio of circumference to diameter, and π = 22/7 is the friendly fraction that cancels when the radius is a multiple of 7.
whenWorks: Any single-shape area, perimeter, surface-area or volume question.
Scaling questions where a solid is enlarged by a fixed ratio.
whenNot: Composite shapes must be split into standard pieces first, then added.
Do not use π = 22/7 for radii that are not multiples of 7 — the arithmetic gets messy; use 3.14.
crackIt.practicePattern: Any 'find the area/perimeter/volume/surface area of a [shape]' is a direct formula question; recognise the shape, then plug in with matching units.

groups.description: The higher-level maths topics — logarithms, counting, probability and letter-digit puzzles.

topics.description: Log rules and how to simplify and solve log expressions.

videos.label: Logarithm — master it in 30 minutes (SSC)
videos.note: Product/quotient/power rules & change of base with shortcuts

learnIt.realLife: Every time you say a number has '6 digits' or that a sound is '10 times louder', you are thinking in logarithms without knowing it. A logarithm just answers one question: 'to what power must I raise this base to get that number?' It turns hard multiplication and powers into easy addition.
learnIt.plainMeaning: log to base b of x, written logₙ x, asks 'b to what power gives x?'. So log₂ 8 = 3 because 2³ = 8, and log₁₀ 1000 = 3 because 10³ = 1000. A logarithm is simply the exponent. 'Log' with no base written means base 10.
learnIt.mathIntro: Because a log is an exponent, the rules of powers become rules of logs: multiplying inside becomes adding, dividing becomes subtracting, and a power comes out to the front as a multiplier. Two anchors: logₙ b = 1 (any base to the power 1 is itself) and logₙ 1 = 0 (anything to the power 0 is 1). To switch base, use logₙ x = log x ÷ log b.

formulas.label: Definition
formulas.breakdown: The log is the power you raise the base b to in order to reach x.

formulas.label: Product and quotient rules
formulas.formula: log(mn) = log m + log n ;  log(m/n) = log m − log n
formulas.breakdown: Multiplying inside a log turns into adding logs; dividing turns into subtracting.

formulas.label: Power rule
formulas.breakdown: A power inside a log jumps out to the front as a multiplier.

formulas.label: Anchor values
formulas.breakdown: The log of the base itself is 1, and the log of 1 is always 0 for any base.

formulas.label: Change of base
formulas.breakdown: Rewrite any log using a base your calculator or the question prefers.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Evaluate a simple log
questionTypes.idea: Asks logₙ x directly. What makes it different: express x as a power of b.
questionTypes.problem: Find log₃ 81.

steps.why: The log is the exponent, so we need 81 as 3 to some power.
The exponent is the answer.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Product / quotient rule
questionTypes.idea: Combine or split logs. What makes it different: adding logs = multiplying arguments.
questionTypes.problem: If log 2 = 0.301 and log 3 = 0.477, find log 6.

steps.why: A product inside becomes a sum of logs.
Add the given values.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Power rule
questionTypes.idea: A power inside the log. What makes it different: bring the exponent to the front.
questionTypes.problem: If log 2 = 0.301, find log 8.

steps.why: The power rule brings the 3 out front.
Multiply.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Solve a log equation
questionTypes.idea: log of something equals a number. What makes it different: turn it back into a power.
questionTypes.problem: Solve log₂ x = 5.

steps.why: logₙ x = y means x = bʸ.
Compute the power.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Change of base
questionTypes.idea: Base does not divide the number neatly. What makes it different: rewrite as a ratio of logs.
questionTypes.problem: Find log₈ 16.

steps.action: Write both as powers of 2: log₈ 16 = log 2⁴ / log 2³ = 4/3.
steps.why: Change of base with base 2 makes both numbers simple powers.
The log 2 cancels, leaving the ratio of exponents.

commonMistakes: Thinking log(m + n) = log m + log n. The sum rule is for PRODUCTS: log(mn) = log m + log n.
Forgetting that logₙ 1 = 0 for every base, and logₙ b = 1.
Dropping the base. 'log' alone means base 10; a different base changes the answer.
Bringing a coefficient inside as a factor instead of a power: 2 log m = log m², not log 2m.
Trying to take the log of zero or a negative number — it is undefined.
learnIt.memoryTrick: A log IS an exponent. Times becomes plus, divide becomes minus, power jumps to the front. And remember the two anchors: log of the base = 1, log of 1 = 0.

crackIt.oneLine: A log is just 'the power', so rewrite every number as a power of the base — times→plus, ÷→minus, exponent→front — and use logₙb = 1, logₙ1 = 0.

shortcuts.name: Evaluate a log
shortcuts.method: Express the number as a power of the base; the exponent is the answer.
shortcuts.whenWorks: When the number is a clean power of the base.
shortcuts.whenFails: When it is not — use change of base.

shortcuts.name: Break with product/power rules
shortcuts.method: Split into known logs (log 2, log 3) using +, − and the power rule.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Uses only the two values you memorised.
shortcuts.whenFails: Prime arguments with no known log.

shortcuts.name: Solve logₙ x = k
shortcuts.method: Flip to x = bᵏ.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Base to the power of the right-hand side.

shortcuts.name: Change of base
shortcuts.method: logₙ x = log x / log b, choosing a base where both are powers.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Rewrite both as powers of a common small base.
shortcuts.timeSaved: Turns an awkward base into a ratio.

patternGuide.signal: 'Find logₙ x' with x a power of b
log of a product or fraction

patternGuide.use: Bring the exponent to the front.

patternGuide.signal: Base and number share no clean power
patternGuide.use: Change of base = log x / log b.

crackIt.whyItWorks: Because a logarithm is literally the exponent, every exponent law (aᵐ·aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ, etc.) becomes a log law. Adding logs mirrors multiplying powers, which is why logs historically turned multiplication into addition.
whenWorks: Numbers that are clean powers of the base, or that split into known logs (2, 3, 5).
Straight log equations of the form logₙ x = k.
whenNot: log(m + n) has no simple rule — only products, quotients and powers simplify.
Logs of zero or negatives are undefined; watch the domain.
crackIt.practicePattern: Any 'evaluate logₙ x', 'simplify using log rules' or 'solve logₙ x = k' question is a rewrite-as-a-power question.

topics.description: Count arrangements and selections — order matters vs not.

videos.label: Permutations & Combinations — full tutorial
videos.note: When order matters vs not, counting principle & word problems

videos.label: Permutation & Combination — full chapter (SSC + PYQ)
videos.note: Tricks with previous-year questions

learnIt.realLife: Choosing 3 friends to invite is different from deciding who sits first, second and third in a race. In the first, the order does not matter; in the second, it does. That single difference — does order matter? — is the whole topic. When order matters it is a permutation; when it does not, a combination.
learnIt.plainMeaning: A permutation counts arrangements, where the order counts (a PIN, a race finish, seats in a row). A combination counts selections, where order does not count (a team, a handful of fruits, a committee). The factorial n! means n × (n−1) × … × 1 and is just the number of ways to arrange n distinct things in a line.
learnIt.mathIntro: The counting principle says: if one step has a ways and the next has b ways, together they have a × b ways. From it, arrangements of r things chosen from n is nPr = n!/(n−r)!, and selections is nCr = n!/(r!(n−r)!). Because a selection ignores order, we divide the permutation by r! to remove the repeats: nCr = nPr / r!.

formulas.label: Factorial
formulas.breakdown: The number of ways to arrange n distinct things in a row. By convention 0! = 1.

formulas.label: Counting principle
formulas.breakdown: Independent choices multiply together.

formulas.label: Permutation (order matters)
formulas.breakdown: Number of ordered arrangements of r items taken from n.

formulas.label: Combination (order does not matter)
formulas.breakdown: Number of unordered selections of r items from n; divide out the r! orderings.

formulas.label: Handy facts
formulas.formula: nC0 = nCn = 1 ;  nC1 = n ;  nCr = nC(n−r) ;  circular arrangements = (n−1)!
formulas.breakdown: Symmetry and edge cases that save time; seating around a round table fixes one seat.

questionTypes.name: Type 1 — Counting principle
questionTypes.idea: Independent choices in sequence. What makes it different: just multiply the options.
questionTypes.problem: A menu has 4 starters and 3 main courses. How many starter-plus-main meals are possible?

steps.why: Each starter can go with each main, so the counts multiply.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 2 — Permutation (order matters)
questionTypes.idea: Arrange r from n where position counts. What makes it different: use nPr.
questionTypes.problem: In how many ways can gold, silver and bronze be awarded among 6 runners?

steps.action: Order matters (the medals differ), so use 6P3 = 6!/(6−3)!.
steps.why: A different order is a different result.
Multiply the top three falling factors.

questionTypes.name: Type 3 — Combination (selection)
questionTypes.idea: Choose r from n where order is irrelevant. What makes it different: use nCr.
questionTypes.problem: How many ways can a team of 3 be chosen from 6 players?

steps.action: Order does not matter, so use 6C3 = 6!/(3! · 3!).
steps.why: A team is the same whatever order you pick them in.
Cancel and compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 4 — Arrangements of a word
questionTypes.idea: Arrange all letters, some repeated. What makes it different: divide by the factorial of each repeat.
questionTypes.problem: How many arrangements of the letters of the word 'LEVEL' are there?

steps.action: 5 letters with L twice and E twice: 5!/(2! · 2!).
steps.why: Divide by 2! for each repeated letter to avoid counting identical arrangements twice.
Compute.

questionTypes.name: Type 5 — Circular arrangement
questionTypes.idea: Seat n around a round table. What makes it different: fix one seat, use (n−1)!.
questionTypes.problem: In how many ways can 5 people sit around a round table?

steps.why: Rotations look the same, so one seat is fixed.
Compute.

commonMistakes: Using a permutation when order does not matter (or a combination when it does). Ask 'does swapping two of them make a new answer?' first.
Forgetting to divide by the factorial of repeated letters when arranging a word.
Adding instead of multiplying independent choices — the counting principle multiplies.
Using n! for a round table instead of (n − 1)!.
Thinking 0! = 0. It is 1.
learnIt.memoryTrick: Order matters → Permutation (P for Position). Order does not → Combination (C for Committee). nCr = nPr ÷ r!, because a selection ignores the r! orderings.

crackIt.oneLine: First ask 'does order matter?' — yes → nPr, no → nCr — and remember nCr = nPr/r!, with round tables using (n−1)! and repeated letters dividing by their factorials.

shortcuts.name: Order test
shortcuts.method: Swap two chosen items; if it is a new outcome use P, else use C.
shortcuts.whereNumbersGo: Decides P vs C before any arithmetic.

shortcuts.name: nCr quick form
shortcuts.method: nCr = product of r top terms ÷ r!.

shortcuts.name: Symmetry
shortcuts.method: nCr = nC(n−r); pick the smaller r.
